1â†’# The Koide formula and quark-lepton mass relations: evidence and statistical assessment
     2â†’
     3â†’## Abstract
     4â†’
     5â†’We compile and statistically evaluate a family of mass relations among Standard Model
     6â†’fermions built on the Koide ratio $Q(m_1,m_2,m_3)\equiv(m_1+m_2+m_3)/(\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}+\sqrt{m_3})^2$.
     7â†’The original charged-lepton relation $Q(m_e,m_\mu,m_\tau)=2/3$ holds to $0.001\%$
     8â†’($0.91\sigma$ in $m_\tau$); a Monte Carlo look-elsewhere analysis over all fermion triples
     9â†’gives $2.8\sigma$.  Extending the formula to signed mass charges
    10â†’$\mathfrak{z}_k=\pm\sqrt{m_k}$, we find the quark triples
    11â†’$(-m_s,m_c,m_b)$ and $(m_c,m_b,m_t)$ satisfy $Q\approx 2/3$ to $1.2\%$ and $0.4\%$
    12â†’respectively, both obligatorily mixing up- and down-type quarks.
    13â†’A seed triple $(0,m_s,m_c)$ with the O'Raifeartaigh mass ratio
    14â†’$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}=2-\sqrt{3}$ is confirmed to $1.2\%$.
    15â†’The vacuum-charge doubling relation $\sqrt{m_b}=3\sqrt{m_s}+\sqrt{m_c}$ predicts
    16â†’$m_b=4177$ MeV ($0.1\sigma$ from PDG).
    17â†’The Koide phase parameter satisfies
    18â†’$\delta_0\bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ to 35 ppm ($3.1\sigma$ with look-elsewhere, $4.3\sigma$ specific).
    19â†’A meson triple $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ extends $Q\approx 2/3$ to composite hadrons at $0.1\%$.
    20â†’The dual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b)=0.665$ ($0.22\%$ from $2/3$) suggests a
    21â†’Seiberg-dual structure.
    22â†’The de Vries angle $R=(\sqrt{19}-3)(\sqrt{19}-\sqrt{3})/16=0.22310\ldots$ matches
    23â†’the on-shell $\sin^2\theta_W$ to $0.13\sigma$,
    24â†’and the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation $\sin\theta_C=\sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ holds to $0.9\%$.
    25â†’Taken individually, several of these relations have modest significance; taken together,
    26â†’their interconnections---shared algebraic structure, overlapping mass triplets, and
    27â†’common Koide phase---point to a single organizing principle.
    28â†’The companion paper [I] constructs the $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian that realizes this
    29â†’principle via an O'Raifeartaigh superpotential with structural invariant $Q=2/3$.
    30â†’
    31â†’---
    32â†’
    33â†’## 1. Introduction
    34â†’
    35â†’### 1.1 The Koide formula
    36â†’
    37â†’In 1981, Koide derived a relation among the charged lepton masses from a composite model
    38â†’with $S_3$ permutation symmetry acting on preon constituents [1].  Stripped of its
    39â†’model-dependent origin, the relation states that the ratio
    40â†’$$
    41â†’Q(m_1,m_2,m_3) \;\equiv\; \frac{m_1+m_2+m_3}{\bigl(\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}+\sqrt{m_3}\bigr)^2}
    42â†’$$
    43â†’equals $2/3$ when evaluated on the electron, muon, and tau masses.  With current PDG
    44â†’values [2],
    45â†’$$
    46â†’Q(m_e,\,m_\mu,\,m_\tau) \;=\; 0.666661 \pm 0.000007\,,
    47â†’$$
    48â†’where the uncertainty is dominated by $\delta m_\tau = 0.12$ MeV.  If the formula is
    49â†’exact, it predicts $m_\tau = 1776.97$ MeV, consistent with the experimental value
    50â†’$1776.86 \pm 0.12$ MeV to $0.91\sigma$.
    51â†’
    52â†’Foot reformulated this as a geometric condition: the vector of square roots of masses
    53â†’makes a $45^\circ$ angle with the democratic vector $(1,1,1)$ [3].  Equivalently, in the
    54â†’angular parametrization
    55â†’$$
    56â†’\sqrt{m_k} \;=\; \sqrt{M_0}\,\Bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\,\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2\pi k}{3}+\delta_0\Bigr)\Bigr)\,,
    57â†’\qquad k=0,1,2\,,
    58â†’$$
    59â†’the condition $Q=2/3$ is satisfied identically for any $M_0$ and $\delta_0$, and the
    60â†’three masses are determined by two parameters [4].  The phase for charged leptons is
    61â†’$\delta_0 = 0.2222$ (in natural units of $2\pi/3$).
    62â†’
    63â†’The precision of the lepton relation---better than one part in $10^5$---has prompted
    64â†’four decades of theoretical attempts to explain it, ranging from discrete symmetries
    65â†’($S_3$, $A_4$) acting on flavon fields [5,6] to radiative mechanisms that protect
    66â†’the formula against renormalization group running [7].  Yet no consensus model exists.
    67â†’The formula has been called an "unexplained coincidence" [8] and a "tantalizing hint"
    68â†’[9] in roughly equal measure.
    69â†’
    70â†’### 1.2 The skeptic's objection: look-elsewhere and scheme dependence
    71â†’
    72â†’Two objections are standard.
    73â†’
    74â†’**Look-elsewhere.**  With six quarks and three charged leptons forming
    75â†’$\binom{9}{3} = 84$ triples (or more, allowing signed square roots), a near-hit at
    76â†’$Q=2/3$ somewhere in the data is not implausible by chance.  We address this head-on.
    77â†’A Monte Carlo simulation drawing nine masses log-uniformly over the range
    78â†’$[0.1,\,200{,}000]$ MeV and scanning all 84 triples times 8 sign assignments finds that
    79â†’a match as precise as the charged leptons occurs in only $0.24\%$ of random mass
    80â†’spectra.  This is a look-elsewhere corrected significance of $2.8\sigma$ [10].  Not
    81â†’overwhelming---but the lepton Koide is only the first entry in a longer list.
    82â†’
    83â†’**Renormalization scheme.**  The Koide formula is stated in terms of pole masses (or
    84â†’$\overline{\text{MS}}$ masses evaluated at the mass itself, $m_q(m_q)$).  Running to the
    85â†’GUT scale shifts $Q$ by a few percent for quarks and by less than $0.1\%$ for leptons
    86â†’[7,11].  This is sometimes used to argue that the relation is an accident of the
    87â†’infrared.  But it is equally true that many well-established mass relations---the
    88â†’Gell-Mann--Okubo formula, the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin (GST) relation---are statements about
    89â†’low-energy masses, not running couplings at a unification scale.  If the Koide relation
    90â†’originates from boundary conditions on an effective superpotential (as argued in [I]),
    91â†’then the infrared is exactly where it should hold.
    92â†’
    93â†’### 1.3 Beyond the leptons: a web of mass relations
    94â†’
    95â†’The real case for the Koide formula rests not on a single relation but on a web of
    96â†’interlocking observations.  In 2011, Rodejohann and Zhang [12] noted that the triple
    97â†’$(m_c, m_b, m_t)$ satisfies $Q = 0.6693$, within $0.4\%$ of $2/3$.  This was surprising
    98â†’because it mixes quark generations and charge sectors.  In [13], one of us showed that
    99â†’allowing signed square roots reveals a further triple $(-m_s, m_c, m_b)$ with $Q = 0.675$
   100â†’($1.2\%$ from $2/3$), and that these triples chain together: the output of one is the
   101â†’input of the next, covering five of the six quark masses.
   102â†’
   103â†’The present paper collects all known mass relations of this type---ten observations
   104â†’spanning charged leptons, quarks, and pseudoscalar mesons---and evaluates each one
   105â†’statistically.  The observations, in order of discovery, are:
   106â†’
   107â†’1. **The charged-lepton Koide** [1]:
   108â†’   $Q(m_e,m_\mu,m_\tau) = 2/3$ to $0.001\%$.  Significance: $0.91\sigma$ in $m_\tau$;
   109â†’   $2.8\sigma$ after look-elsewhere.
   110â†’
   111â†’2. **The quark seed** [13]:
   112â†’   $Q(0,\,m_s,\,m_c) \approx 2/3$, meaning $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} \approx 2-\sqrt{3}$.
   113â†’   Observed ratio $0.271$, theoretical $0.268$; deviation $1.2\%$.
   114â†’
   115â†’3. **The signed quark triple** [13]:
   116â†’   $Q(-m_s,\,m_c,\,m_b) = 0.675$, deviation $1.24\%$ from $2/3$.
   117â†’
   118â†’4. **The heavy triple** [12]:
   119â†’   $Q(m_c,\,m_b,\,m_t) = 0.6693$, deviation $0.40\%$.
   120â†’
   121â†’5. **The meson triple** [14]:
   122â†’   $Q(-m_\pi,\,m_{D_s},\,m_B) = 0.6674$, deviation $0.10\%$.
   123â†’
   124â†’6. **$v_0$-doubling** [this work]:
   125â†’   $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ to $0.07\%$, predicting $m_b = 4177$ MeV
   126â†’   (PDG: $4180 \pm 30$ MeV, $0.1\sigma$).  In isolation, the look-elsewhere probability
   127â†’   is $24.9\%$ (not significant); the significance comes from its structural role within
   128â†’   the triple framework.
   129â†’
   130â†’7. **The phase relation** [15,16]:
   131â†’   $\delta_0 \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ to 35 ppm.  Significance: $3.1\sigma$ with
   132â†’   look-elsewhere over 128 simple fractions; $4.3\sigma$ for the specific value $2/9$.
   133â†’
   134â†’8. **The dual Koide** [this work]:
   135â†’   $Q(1/m_d,\,1/m_s,\,1/m_b) = 0.665$, $0.22\%$ from $2/3$.
   136â†’   Suggests a Seiberg-dual seesaw structure $M_j \propto 1/m_j$.
   137â†’
   138â†’9. **The cyclic echo** [this work]:
   139â†’   Iterating the Koide equation as $t \to c \to s \to \text{stall} \to c$ produces an
   140â†’   algebraically exact cycle (agreement to machine epsilon).
   141â†’
   142â†’10. **The second scaling fails** [this work]:
   143â†’    $M_2 = 9M_0$, $\delta_2 = 9\delta_0$ does *not* produce $(c,b,t)$ or any physical
   144â†’    triple.  The pattern stops at one step.  This negative result constrains the
   145â†’    mechanism.
   146â†’
   147â†’Two additional relations, while not Koide triples, belong to the same family:
   148â†’
   149â†’- **The de Vries angle** [17]:
   150â†’  $R = (\sqrt{19}-3)(\sqrt{19}-\sqrt{3})/16 = 0.22310\ldots$ matches
   151â†’  $\sin^2\theta_W(\text{on-shell})$ to $0.13\sigma$ in $M_W$.
   152â†’
   153â†’- **The GST/Oakes relation** [18,19]:
   154â†’  $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s} = 0.224$, matching $|V_{us}| = 0.2243$ to $0.9\%$.
   155â†’
   156â†’### 1.4 What this paper adds
   157â†’
   158â†’Previous discussions of these relations appear scattered across individual papers,
   159â†’conference proceedings, and blog posts.  No systematic statistical assessment exists.
   160â†’This paper provides three things:
   161â†’
   162â†’**First**, a self-contained compilation of all the observations, with current PDG values
   163â†’and error bars, in a single reference.  We state every number with its uncertainty and
   164â†’pull, not just a percentage deviation.
   165â†’
   166â†’**Second**, a rigorous look-elsewhere analysis.  For each observation we define the trial
   167â†’space---how many similar relations could have been examined---and compute corrected
   168â†’$p$-values.  We are explicit about which observations are significant in isolation
   169â†’($2.8\sigma$ for the lepton Koide, $3.1\sigma$ for the phase relation) and which derive
   170â†’their significance only from the structural context ($v_0$-doubling, dual Koide).
   171â†’
   172â†’**Third**, an argument that the observations are not independent.  The quark seed, the
   173â†’signed triple, the heavy triple, and the $v_0$-doubling relation share three of the same
   174â†’masses ($m_s$, $m_c$, $m_b$) and are linked by a common algebraic structure.
   175â†’An exhaustive scan of all $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ quark triples reveals that *every* triple
   176â†’close to $Q = 2/3$ obligatorily mixes up-type and down-type quarks [13]; pure
   177â†’same-charge triples $(d,s,b)$ and $(u,c,t)$ give $Q = 0.73$ and $Q = 0.85$
   178â†’respectively, both far from $2/3$.  This is not a selection effect: while $90\%$ of
   179â†’random triples from six quarks are mixed by combinatorics, the $100\%$ rate among
   180â†’Koide-satisfying triples is a structural feature pointing toward CKM-type mixing.
   181â†’
   182â†’The companion paper [I] constructs the $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetric Lagrangian that
   183â†’produces these relations.  The key result there is that the O'Raifeartaigh superpotential
   184â†’$$
   185â†’W = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_a\Phi_b + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_a^2
   186â†’$$
   187â†’produces a Koide seed triple $(0,\,m_-,\,m_+)$ with $Q = 2/3$ exactly when
   188â†’$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$, where $v = \langle\Phi_0\rangle$ is the pseudo-modulus vacuum
   189â†’expectation value.  The mass hierarchy $m_s/m_c = (2-\sqrt{3})^2 \approx 0.072$ emerges
   190â†’from the *minimal* model without additional dynamics: it is the unique mass ratio
   191â†’consistent with a massless goldstino (the seed zero), the Koide condition, and the single
   192â†’algebraic constraint on the superpotential couplings.
   193â†’
   194â†’The present paper is deliberately agnostic about the Lagrangian.  We state the empirical
   195â†’relations, assess their statistical weight, and leave the dynamical interpretation to [I].
   196â†’The question this paper asks is precise: *given the data, is there evidence for a common
   197â†’algebraic structure in the fermion mass spectrum, or can the observations be dismissed as
   198â†’look-elsewhere artifacts?*
   199â†’
   200â†’### 1.5 Conventions and mass inputs
   201â†’
   202â†’Throughout this paper, quark masses refer to $\overline{\text{MS}}$ values at the
   203â†’self-energy scale $m_q(m_q)$ for $c$ and $b$, and the pole mass for the top quark:
   204â†’$m_s = 93.4 \pm 5.8$ MeV, $m_c = 1270 \pm 20$ MeV, $m_b = 4180 \pm 30$ MeV,
   205â†’$m_t = 172.76 \pm 0.30$ GeV [2].  Light quark masses are $m_u = 2.16 \pm 0.49$ MeV,
   206â†’$m_d = 4.67 \pm 0.48$ MeV.  Lepton masses are pole masses: $m_e = 0.51100$ MeV,
   207â†’$m_\mu = 105.658$ MeV, $m_\tau = 1776.86 \pm 0.12$ MeV.  Meson masses are PDG
   208â†’averages: $m_\pi = 139.57$ MeV, $m_{D_s} = 1968.35$ MeV, $m_B = 5279.34$ MeV.
   209â†’
   210â†’We use natural units $c = \hbar = 1$.  The Koide ratio is denoted $Q$.  We write
   211â†’$\mathfrak{z}_k = \pm\sqrt{m_k}$ for signed mass charges, with the sign conventions
   212â†’specified for each triple.  Pulls are quoted as
   213â†’$(x_{\text{pred}} - x_{\text{obs}})/\sigma_{\text{obs}}$ in units of $\sigma$.
   214â†’
   215â†’### 1.6 Outline
   216â†’
   217â†’Section 2 reviews the Koide parametrization and the seed structure.
   218â†’Section 3 presents the quark chain: the seed, the signed triple, and the heavy triple.
   219â†’Section 4 discusses the $v_0$-doubling and the overlap prediction.
   220â†’Section 5 extends the analysis to pseudoscalar mesons.
   221â†’Section 6 covers the phase relation $\delta_0 \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ and the dual
   222â†’Koide.
   223â†’Section 7 presents the de Vries angle and the GST relation.
   224â†’Section 8 gives the full statistical assessment, including combined significance.
   225â†’Section 9 discusses scheme dependence, renormalization stability, and negative results.
   226â†’Section 10 summarizes the conclusions and the connection to [I].
   227â†’
   228â†’---
   229â†’
   230â†’### References (partial, to be completed)
   231â†’
   232â†’[1] Y. Koide, Phys. Rev. Lett. 47, 1241 (1981).
   233â†’
   234â†’[2] Particle Data Group, R.L. Workman et al., Prog. Theor. Exp. Phys. 2022, 083C01.
   235â†’
   236â†’[3] R. Foot, arXiv:hep-ph/9402242.
   237â†’
   238â†’[4] D. Look and M.D. Sheppeard (see [13] and references therein).
   239â†’
   240â†’[5] Y. Koide, Mod. Phys. Lett. A 5, 2319 (1990).
   241â†’
   242â†’[6] E. Ma, Phys. Rev. D 73, 077301 (2006).
   243â†’
   244â†’[7] Z.-z. Xing, H. Zhang, and S. Zhou, Phys. Lett. B 666, 462 (2008).
   245â†’
   246â†’[8] M. Ratz, Lecture notes (2013).
   247â†’
   248â†’[9] F. Wilczek, Nature 439, 547 (2006).
   249â†’
   250â†’[10] Computed in this work; Monte Carlo with $10^4$ trials, see Section 8.
   251â†’
   252â†’[11] S. Esposito and P. Santorelli, Mod. Phys. Lett. A 10, 3077 (1995).
   253â†’
   254â†’[12] W. Rodejohann and H. Zhang, Phys. Lett. B 698, 152 (2011).
   255â†’
   256â†’[13] A. Rivero, arXiv:1111.7232 [hep-ph].
   257â†’
   258â†’[14] A. Rivero, arXiv:0710.1526 [hep-ph].
   259â†’
   260â†’[15] P. Zenczykowski, Phys. Rev. D 86, 117303 (2012).
   261â†’
   262â†’[16] M.D. Sheppeard, PhD thesis (2007).
   263â†’
   264â†’[17] H. de Vries, unpublished; discussed in A. Rivero, arXiv:hep-ph/0606171.
   265â†’
   266â†’[18] R. Gatto, G. Sartori, and M. Tonin, Phys. Lett. B 28, 128 (1968).
   267â†’
   268â†’[19] R.J. Oakes, Phys. Lett. B 29, 683 (1969).
   269â†’

1â†’# Paper Split Audit: sbootstrap_v4d.tex
     2â†’
     3â†’**Paper A** ("Lagrangian paper"): SUSY Lagrangian, vacuum structure, particle spectrum, mass predictions as consequences of the O'Raifeartaigh vacuum and bion Kahler corrections. Must NOT mention "Koide" by name, nor display Q = 2/3 as an empirical observation. Mass relations are derived from the superpotential structure.
     4â†’
     5â†’**Paper B** ("Koide evidence paper"): Empirical mass relations, statistical significance, the Koide formula Q = 2/3 as an observed regularity, look-elsewhere corrections, the de Vries angle, historical observations, framing mass relations as unexplained empirical facts.
     6â†’
     7â†’**Both**: Content needed by both papers (notation, basic setup, sBootstrap postulate).
     8â†’
     9â†’---
    10â†’
    11â†’## Section-by-Section Audit
    12â†’
    13â†’### Abstract (lines 29--98)
    14â†’
    15â†’| Classification | **SPLIT** |
    16â†’|---|---|
    17â†’| Reason | The abstract mixes Lagrangian results with Koide-empirical language throughout. |
    18â†’| Modifications for A | Rewrite entirely. Paper A abstract should lead with the Lagrangian, the O'Raifeartaigh superpotential, the bion Kahler correction, the $v_0$-doubling as a vacuum condition, the prediction chain $m_s \to m_c \to m_b \to m_t$, the tan(beta)=1 prediction, and the Diophantine uniqueness. Replace every instance of "Koide ratio/triple/formula" with "mass-ratio condition" or "energy-balance condition" or "O'Raifeartaigh mass relation". Never display $Q = (\sum m)/(\sum\sqrt{m})^2 = 2/3$ as a stand-alone empirical formula. |
    19â†’| Modifications for B | Paper B abstract should lead with the empirical Koide formula, its statistical significance, the look-elsewhere corrections, the seed triples as observed patterns, and frame the meson Koide triple, de Vries angle, and iterative chains as empirical evidence requiring explanation. |
    20â†’
    21â†’---
    22â†’
    23â†’### Section 1: Introduction (lines 103--177)
    24â†’
    25â†’| Classification | **SPLIT** |
    26â†’|---|---|
    27â†’| Reason | Introduces both the sBootstrap postulate (Both/A) and the Koide ratio as an observed formula (B). The three "themes" are: (1) signed charges = Both/A; (2) three Koide tuples = B; (3) Koide condition constrains superpotential = A. |
    28â†’| Modifications for A | Rewrite to lead with the Lagrangian motivation. Theme 1 (signed mass charges) can stay but reframe: "The mass spectrum of the O'Raifeartaigh vacuum is parametrized by signed mass charges..." Theme 3 (superpotential constraint) is the core of Paper A. Theme 2 (three Koide tuples) should become: "The superpotential produces three mass triples satisfying the energy-balance condition..." The "New results" paragraph should emphasize Lagrangian-derived results. Remove all mentions of "Koide" by name; use "energy-balance condition" or "mass-ratio constraint". |
    29â†’| Modifications for B | Theme 2 is the core. Keep Koide language. Add the historical context that Paper A omits. |
    30â†’
    31â†’---
    32â†’
    33â†’### Section 2: The Koide Formula as Energy Balance (lines 180--228)
    34â†’
    35â†’#### 2.1 Charges, not masses (lines 184--188)
    36â†’
    37â†’| Classification | **Both** |
    38â†’|---|---|
    39â†’| Reason | Defines $\mathfrak{z} = z_0 + z_i$, $m = \mathfrak{z}^2$. Pure notation needed by both papers. |
    40â†’| Modifications for A | None; notation is theory-neutral. |
    41â†’
    42â†’#### 2.2 Derivation of $Q = 2/3$ (lines 189--221)
    43â†’
    44â†’| Classification | **SPLIT** |
    45â†’|---|---|
    46â†’| Reason | The algebra itself (conditions 1 and 2) is needed by Paper A as the "energy-balance condition" derived from the vacuum structure. But the framing as "The Koide Formula" and the boxed $Q = 2/3$ equation presented as an empirical regularity is Paper B. |

   621â†’8. Mass relations and iterative chains (Sec. 7, entire)

   623â†’10. Statistical significance: look-elsewhere corrections for all patterns

   629â†’## Content Gaps for Paper A
   630â†’
   631â†’The following content is needed for Paper A but is not currently in the paper (or is present only in "Koide language"):
   632â†’
   633â†’1. **Self-contained derivation of the energy-balance condition from the superpotential.** Currently, Sec. 2 derives $Q = 2/3$ from the signed-charge formalism, and then Sec. 11.3 shows the O'Raifeartaigh model produces it. Paper A needs a unified presentation: start from the superpotential, derive the fermionic mass matrix, show that $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ produces the energy-balance condition, then present the condition as a theorem about the vacuum, not as an empirical observation.
   634â†’
   635â†’2. **The Kahler stabilization at $c = -1/12$ needs to be presented as a self-contained argument**, not as an aside in the "Koide phase" discussion. Currently it appears in Sec. 11.3 and again in Sec. 14.2. Paper A should have a dedicated subsection.
   636â†’
   637â†’3. **The prediction chain $m_s \to m_c \to m_b \to m_t$ needs a clean, forward presentation.** Currently the chain is assembled from pieces scattered across Sections 4, 7, and 11.12. Paper A should present it linearly: given $m_s$, the seed condition gives $m_c$; the bion Kahler gives $m_b$; the overdetermination test gives $m_t$.
   638â†’
   639â†’4. **The $\tan\beta = 1$ prediction needs more prominence.** It currently appears in Sec. 11.12 (lines 2724--2749) but is somewhat buried. In Paper A it should be a highlighted result.
   640â†’
   641â†’5. **The SU(2) lepton confining sector** (lines 3420--3448) is currently a brief sketch in "Open Problems". Paper A should either expand it into a proper section or clearly flag it as a conjecture.
   642â†’
   643â†’6. **RG analysis**: The negative result (Sec. 11.9, energy balance is not an RG attractor) should be more prominent in Paper A, as it establishes that the condition must be a boundary condition.
   644â†’
   645â†’7. **Parameter count**: The explicit statement "only $m_u$, $m_d$, $m_s$ remain free" (currently at line 3055) should be a highlighted conclusion of Paper A, with a clear diagram showing which Lagrangian term fixes which mass.
   646â†’
   647â†’8. **The "mass spectrum from the Lagrangian" table (lines 2978--3001) should be the centerpiece of Paper A**, not buried deep in Sec. 11.12.
   648â†’
