1â†’\documentclass[12pt,a4paper]{article}
     2â†’\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
     3â†’\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
     4â†’\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsfonts}
     5â†’\usepackage{mathtools}
     6â†’\usepackage{booktabs}
     7â†’\usepackage{array}
     8â†’\usepackage{geometry}
     9â†’\usepackage{hyperref}
    10â†’\usepackage{cite}
    11â†’\geometry{margin=2.5cm}
    12â†’
    13â†’\newcommand{\MeV}{\,\text{MeV}}
    14â†’\newcommand{\GeV}{\,\text{GeV}}
    15â†’\newcommand{\fpi}{f_\pi}
    16â†’\newcommand{\qqbar}{\langle\bar{q}q\rangle}
    17â†’\newcommand{\zch}{\mathfrak{z}}
    18â†’
    19â†’\title{A Tale of Two Tuples: Seed Triples, Signed Charges, \\
    20â†’and SUSY Breaking in the sBootstrap}
    21â†’
    22â†’\author{A.~Rivero\thanks{Manuscript prepared with AI assistance (Claude/Anthropic). 
    23â†’Continues the program of \cite{Rivero2005,Rivero2012}.}}
    24â†’\date{March 2026}
    25â†’
    26â†’\begin{document}
    27â†’\maketitle
    28â†’
    29â†’\begin{abstract}
    30â†’We reformulate the sBootstrap in terms of signed mass charges 
    31â†’$\zch = z_0 + z_i$ with $m = \zch^2$, and show that the Koide ratio 
    32â†’$Q = 2/3$ is the algebraic consequence of two conditions: 
    33â†’$\sum z_i = 0$ and $\langle z_i^2\rangle = z_0^2$ (perturbation 
    34â†’energies average to the base-scale energy). Allowing negative charges, 
    35â†’we find that pseudoscalar mesons satisfy a Koide triple 
    36â†’$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ to 0.1\%, and identify \emph{seed triples} with 
    37â†’one massless member---$(0, s, c)$ for quarks, $(0, \pi, D_s)$ for 
    38â†’mesons---as the pre-breaking configurations from which the full 
    39â†’spectrum emerges. These seed triples share the same analytical 
    40â†’structure (charge ratio $2-\sqrt{3}$) and, strikingly, the meson 
    41â†’seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ has Koide scale $v_0^2 \approx 351\MeV$,
    42â†’close to the lepton triple's $v_0^2 \approx 314\MeV$. A third 
    43â†’tuple $(c,b,t)$ stands apart, having no meson counterpart because 
    44â†’top diquarks do not hadronize. We discuss the diquark sector---which 
    45â†’fills the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ of $SU(5)$ 
    46â†’flavor, in conflict with Pauli statistics for point-like diquarks, 
    47â†’proving that the diquark cannot be a $qq$ composite---and outline 
    48â†’how SUSY breaking proceeds through two mechanisms---F-term (chiral
    49â†’condensate, with Volkov--Akulov as its low-energy realization) and
    50â†’D-term (electromagnetic Fayet--Iliopoulos)---plus a separate
    51â†’heavy-sector origin for the $(c,b,t)$ triple. The O'Raifeartaigh
    52â†’superpotential produces the Koide seed spectrum exactly when
    53â†’$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$, reducing the energy-balance condition to a
    54â†’single constraint on the couplings. The bloom from seed to full
    55â†’triple doubles the vacuum charge, yielding
    56â†’$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ (matching PDG to
    57â†’$0.1\sigma$), and all quark Koide triples obligatorily mix
    58â†’up-type and down-type mass eigenstates.
    59â†’We construct the explicit $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian---K\"ahler
    60â†’potential, superpotential (Seiberg constraint, Yukawa with
    61â†’separate $H_u$, $H_d$ doublets),
    62â†’$D$-terms, and soft breaking from a spurion with
    63â†’$\langle F\rangle = f_\pi^2$---and show that the mixed up/down-type
    64â†’Koide triples require $\tan\beta = 1$ for the mass-level condition
    65â†’to equal a Yukawa-level condition.  A negative quartic
    66â†’K\"ahler correction $c = -1/12$ stabilizes the pseudo-modulus at
    67â†’$\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ (the K\"ahler pole), while the
    68â†’$v_0$-doubling originates in the K\"ahler sector via a bion
    69â†’correction $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ from
    70â†’monopole-instantons on $\mathbb{R}^3\times S^1$; a
    71â†’three-instanton superpotential $(\det M)^3/\Lambda^{18}$
    72â†’generates a $\cos(9\delta)$ potential whose minima include the
    73â†’observed lepton Koide phase to $0.9\sigma$ in $m_\tau$.
    74â†’The Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$ has no kinetic term
    75â†’($N_f = N_c$ admits no magnetic dual), so $F_X = 0$ and all
    76â†’off-diagonal meson masses are positive ($\sim 2\fpi^2$);
    77â†’a fundamental block-diagonal obstruction prevents any polynomial
    78â†’K\"ahler correction from generating cross-flavour meson
    79â†’condensates.  The seesaw vacuum is cosmologically stable
    80â†’($S_{\text{bounce}} > 10^7$), and dimensional analysis forces
    81â†’the NMSSM singlet~$S$ to be a separate field from~$X$
    82â†’($\lambda_{\text{Higgs}} / \lambda_{\text{max}} \sim 10^5$).
    83â†’The Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation
    84â†’$\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ is a structural property of the
    85â†’seesaw-inverted mass hierarchy rather than a derived prediction.
    86â†’The isospin-summed mass $m_u + m_d$ satisfies $Q(0,\,m_u{+}m_d,\,m_s) = 0.665$ ($0.27\%$ from $2/3$), extending the Koide chain to the first generation.
    87â†’A separate $SU(2)$ confining sector (s-confining, $N_f = 3$) with an
    88â†’O'Raifeartaigh deformation generates the lepton Koide seed
    89â†’via the same universal mechanism.
    90â†’After these constraints, only two to three
    91â†’free parameters remain ($m_u$, $m_d$, $m_s$), with $m_u$ and
    92â†’$m_d$ connected to the Cabibbo angle via the GST relation.
    93â†’The $SO(32)$ adjoint accommodates the sBootstrap representations;
    94â†’$E_8 \times E_8$ is blocked because $E_8$ decompositions produce
    95â†’only antisymmetric $\mathbf{10}$, never the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$
    96â†’required for diquarks. A Diophantine system on
    97â†’the scalar content uniquely selects $N=3$ generations.
    98â†’\end{abstract}
    99â†’
   100â†’\tableofcontents
   101â†’
   102â†’%======================================================================
   103â†’\section{Introduction}
   104â†’\label{sec:intro}
   105â†’%======================================================================
   106â†’
   107â†’The sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005,Rivero2012} proposes that the Standard 
   108â†’Model's pseudoscalar mesons and leptons fill common $\mathcal{N}=1$ 
   109â†’supermultiplets. Its foundational relation is
   110â†’\begin{equation}
   111â†’\label{eq:pimu}
   112â†’m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2\,,
   113â†’\end{equation}
   114â†’identifying the pion decay constant $f_\pi \approx 92.2\MeV$ as the 
   115â†’SUSY-breaking order parameter. Numerically, $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = 
   116â†’8{,}316\MeV^2$ versus $f_\pi^2 = 8{,}501\MeV^2$, a $2.2\%$ 
   117â†’discrepancy---comparable to the precision of the quark Koide triples 
   118â†’discussed below. In this paper we develop two themes 
   119â†’that change the structure of the program.
   120â†’
   121â†’First, \textbf{mass is the square of a signed charge}: $m = \zch^2$ 
   122â†’where $\zch = z_0 + z_i$ can be negative. The Koide ratio $Q = 2/3$ 
   123â†’is then not numerology but the energy-balance condition 
   124â†’$\langle z_i^2\rangle = z_0^2$. With signed charges, pseudoscalar 
   125â†’mesons satisfy Koide, and the spectrum organizes into \emph{seed 
   126â†’triples} with a massless member that grow into full triples upon 
   127â†’symmetry breaking.
   128â†’
   129â†’Second, \textbf{the full sBootstrap has three Koide tuples, not one}, 
   130â†’and the breaking structure must generate all three: a light sector 
   131â†’$(0,s,c) \to (-s,c,b)$ seen in both quarks and mesons, a lepton 
   132â†’sector $(e\!\approx\!0, \mu, \tau)$, and a heavy sector $(c,b,t)$ 
   133â†’that decouples from meson physics because the top quark does not 
   134â†’hadronize. The diquark partners of quarks, needed to complete the 
   135â†’SUSY multiplets, fill the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ 
   136â†’of $SU(5)$ flavor---the representation produced naturally by the 
   137â†’SUSY generator and by the $SO(32)$ decomposition. A kinematic 
   138â†’theorem shows that no $J=0$, color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have 
   139â†’symmetric flavor---regardless of orbital angular momentum---so the 
   140â†’sBootstrap diquark cannot be a $qq$ composite but must be an
   141â†’algebraically or string-theoretically generated object.
   142â†’
   143â†’Third, \textbf{the Koide condition constrains the superpotential}.
   144â†’The O'Raifeartaigh model---the simplest realization of $F$-term
   145â†’SUSY breaking with three chiral superfields---produces a fermionic
   146â†’mass spectrum that is an exact Koide seed triple when the ratio of
   147â†’the cubic coupling to the bilinear mass parameter satisfies
   148â†’$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$. This single algebraic constraint is the
   149â†’microscopic content of the energy-balance condition. Separately,
   150â†’a Diophantine system on the scalar content ($rs = 2N$,
   151â†’$r(r+1)/2 = 2N$, $r^2+s^2-1=4N$) uniquely selects $N=3$
   152â†’generations, and a Casimir eigenvalue equation predicts
   153â†’$\sin^2\theta_W = 0.22310$ to $0.4\sigma$.
   154â†’
   155â†’\paragraph{New results in this paper.}
   156â†’Compared to the prior publication \cite{Rivero2005}, the
   157â†’following are first reported here: the signed-charge formalism
   158â†’and the meson Koide triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$; the seed-triple
   159â†’concept and the universal ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$; the
   160â†’$v_0$-doubling relation $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$;
   161â†’the O'Raifeartaigh derivation $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ and its
   162â†’K\"ahler stabilization at the pole $c = -1/12$; the Pauli theorem
   163â†’for symmetric diquarks; the bloom instability under perturbative
   164â†’$R$-breaking; the dual Koide on $(d,s,b)$ and its connection to
   165â†’the Seiberg seesaw; the bion K\"ahler mechanism for the
   166â†’$v_0$-doubling; the self-consistent prediction chain
   167â†’$m_s \to m_c \to m_b \to m_t$; and the scale relation
   168â†’$M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$ tying the lepton Koide scale
   169â†’to the pion decay constant; and the prediction $\tan\beta = 1$
   170â†’from the mixed up/down-type Koide triples.
   171â†’The Casimir eigenvalue equation and the $SO(32)$
   172â†’decomposition appeared in our prior work; here we add the
   173â†’algebraic proof linking the Casimir ratio to the de~Vries form,
   174â†’a uniqueness analysis of the equation, the
   175â†’$N_f$-phase structure of the SQCD realization, and the
   176â†’three-instanton origin of the $\cos(9\delta)$ potential
   177â†’that selects the lepton Koide phase.
   178â†’
   179â†’%======================================================================
   180â†’\section{The Koide Formula as Energy Balance}

180â†’\section{The Koide Formula as Energy Balance}
   181â†’\label{sec:koide_algebra}
   182â†’%======================================================================
   183â†’
   184â†’\subsection{Charges, not masses}
   185â†’
   186â†’The fundamental variable is the signed mass charge $\zch = z_0 + z_i 
   187â†’\in \mathbb{R}$, with the physical mass $m = \zch^2 \geq 0$.
   188â†’
   189â†’\subsection{Derivation of $Q = 2/3$}
   190â†’
   191â†’For three charges $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k$ with $z_0$ the mean 
   192â†’($\sum z_k = 0$):
   193â†’\begin{equation}
   194â†’Q = \frac{\sum \zch_k^2}{(\sum \zch_k)^2} 
   195â†’= \frac{3z_0^2 + \sum z_k^2}{9z_0^2}
   196â†’= \frac{1}{3} + \frac{\langle z_k^2\rangle}{3z_0^2}\,.
   197â†’\end{equation}
   198â†’Therefore:
   199â†’\begin{equation}
   200â†’\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 
   201â†’\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2\,.}
   202â†’\end{equation}
   203â†’
   204â†’The two conditions are:
   205â†’\begin{enumerate}
   206â†’\item \textbf{Zero-sum perturbations:} $z_1 + z_2 + z_3 = 0$ 
   207â†’(definition of $z_0$ as the mean charge).
   208â†’\item \textbf{Energy balance:} The mean energy of the perturbations 
   209â†’equals the energy of the unperturbed charge: 
   210â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
   211â†’\end{enumerate}
   212â†’
   213â†’Condition~2 is the physics. It says the splitting scale matches the
   214â†’base scale---the coefficient of variation equals unity:
   215â†’$\sigma(\zch)/\bar{\zch} = 1$.
   216â†’(Dimensionally, $\zch$ has units $\MeV^{1/2}$, so
   217â†’$z_0^2$ has units MeV---a mass scale, not an energy density.
   218â†’The ``energy balance'' terminology is metaphorical.) This is neither perturbative 
   219â†’($\sigma \ll \bar{\zch}$, giving $Q \to 1/3$, all equal) nor 
   220â†’dominant ($\sigma \gg \bar{\zch}$, giving $Q \to 1$, one mass 
   221â†’dominates). The Koide value $2/3$ is the equipartition point.
   222â†’
   223â†’\subsection{Why one mass can vanish}
   224â†’
   225â†’If $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$, a perturbation $z_k$ can reach 
   226â†’$|z_k| \sim z_0$, making $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k \approx 0$. A vanishing 
   227â†’mass is the \emph{natural extreme} of energy balance, not fine-tuning.
   228â†’
   229â†’%======================================================================
   230â†’\section{Seed Triples: The $(0, a, b)$ Structure}
   231â†’\label{sec:seeds}
   232â†’%======================================================================
   233â†’
   234â†’\subsection{Analytical condition}
   235â†’
   236â†’For a triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ with one massless member, the 
   237â†’Koide ratio becomes:
   238â†’\begin{equation}
   239â†’Q = \frac{\zch_a^2 + \zch_b^2}{(\zch_a + \zch_b)^2}\,.
   240â†’\end{equation}
   241â†’Setting $Q = 2/3$ and writing $r = \zch_a/\zch_b$ (with $r < 1$):
   242â†’\begin{equation}
   243â†’3(r^2 + 1) = 2(r + 1)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad r^2 - 4r + 1 = 0\,,
   244â†’\end{equation}
   245â†’giving:
   246â†’\begin{equation}
   247â†’\boxed{r = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.}
   248â†’\end{equation}
   249â†’
   250â†’Equivalently, in mass space: 
   251â†’$m_{\text{light}}/m_{\text{heavy}} = (2 - \sqrt{3})^2 = 7 - 4\sqrt{3} 
   252â†’\approx 0.07180$.
   253â†’
   254â†’This is a \emph{universal} prediction: any Koide triple with one 
   255â†’massless member has the same charge ratio between the two massive 
   256â†’members, regardless of their absolute scale.
   257â†’
   258â†’\subsection{The quark seed: $(0, s, c)$}
   259â†’
   260â†’The strange and charm quark charges give:
   261â†’\begin{equation}
   262â†’\frac{\sqrt{m_s}}{\sqrt{m_c}} = \frac{9.67}{35.64} = 0.2712\,,
   263â†’\qquad r_{\text{Koide}} = 0.2679\,, \qquad \text{deviation: } 1.2\%\,.
   264â†’\end{equation}
   265â†’
   266â†’This triple has $Q = 0.6644$, deviating from $2/3$ by only $0.35\%$.
   267â†’Its Koide scale is $v_0^2 = (\zch_s + \zch_c)^2/9 \approx 228\MeV$.
   268â†’
   269â†’\paragraph{Scale ambiguity in the quark seed.}
   270â†’Throughout this paper, quark masses follow PDG conventions:
   271â†’$m_s(2\GeV) = 93.4\MeV$, $m_c(m_c) = 1.27\GeV$,
   272â†’$m_b(m_b) = 4.18\GeV$, $m_t = 172.76\GeV$ (pole mass). This is a
   273â†’mixed convention, with the light quark evaluated at 2~GeV and heavier
   274â†’quarks at their own scale. QCD mass ratios are RG invariants at
   275â†’leading order (the anomalous dimension $\gamma_m$ is flavor-universal),
   276â†’so $Q$ is scheme-independent. However, the seed ratio
   277â†’$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ uses masses at \emph{different} scales:
   278â†’at a common $\overline{\text{MS}}$ scale the ratio shifts to
   279â†’$\sim 0.29$, worsening the seed match from 1.2\% to $\sim 8\%$.
   280â†’This is a significant limitation of the quark seed claim. The meson
   281â†’seed, which uses unambiguous physical (pole) masses, is free of this
   282â†’scheme issue and provides the cleaner test of the seed hypothesis.
   283â†’
   284â†’A historical note: Harari, Haut, and Weyers \cite{Harari} observed 
   285â†’a quark mass ratio pattern that, in the language of the present paper, 
   286â†’corresponds to an approximate seed triple---$(0, d, s)$---at a time 
   287â†’when the up quark mass was 
   288â†’still thought to be possibly zero. With current masses, 
   289â†’$\sqrt{m_d}/\sqrt{m_s} = 0.224$, deviating from $2 - \sqrt{3}$ by 
   290â†’$16\%$---too large for a good Koide triple. But if $m_s$ were 
   291â†’$\sim 150\MeV$ and $m_d \sim 7\MeV$ (values common in that era), the 
   292â†’ratio improves considerably. The seed idea was already present, ahead 
   293â†’of its time.
   294â†’
   295â†’\subsection{The meson seed: $(0, \pi, D_s)$}
   296â†’
   297â†’The pion and $D_s$ meson charges give:
   298â†’\begin{equation}
   299â†’\frac{\sqrt{m_\pi}}{\sqrt{m_{D_s}}} = \frac{11.81}{44.37} = 0.2663\,, 
   300â†’\qquad r_{\text{Koide}} = 0.2679\,, \qquad \text{deviation: } 0.6\%\,.
   301â†’\end{equation}
   302â†’
   303â†’This gives $Q = 0.6679$, deviating by $0.18\%$. It is the 
   304â†’\textbf{meson counterpart} of the quark seed $(0, s, c)$---both 
   305â†’involve the strange-charm sector. Indeed, $D_s = c\bar{s}$ is the 
   306â†’meson that directly contains the $s$ and $c$ quarks.
   307â†’
   308â†’\subsection{The lepton triple as an ``almost-seed''}
   309â†’
   310â†’The charged lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ has $\zch_e = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}$, 
   311â†’which is small but nonzero. If $m_e$ were zero, we would need 
   312â†’$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 2 - \sqrt{3} = 0.2679$; the actual value 
   313â†’is $0.2439$, deviating by $9\%$. So the leptons are \emph{not} an 
   314â†’exact seed---the electron, while very light, is not massless. But the 
   315â†’triple is clearly the same family: the near-critical Koide phase puts 
   316â†’one member close to zero.
   317â†’
   318â†’\subsection{Scale coincidence}
   319â†’
   320â†’The $v_0^2$ values of the seed triples cluster remarkably:
   321â†’
   322â†’\begin{center}
   323â†’\begin{tabular}{lcc}
   324â†’\toprule
   325â†’Triple & $v_0^2$ (MeV) & $v_0$ ($\MeV^{1/2}$) \\
   326â†’\midrule
   327â†’$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 314 & 17.72 \\
   328â†’$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 351 & 18.73 \\
   329â†’$(0, s, c)$ & 228 & 15.10 \\
   330â†’\bottomrule
   331â†’\end{tabular}
   332â†’\end{center}
   333â†’
   334â†’The lepton and meson seed triples share a Koide scale around
   335â†’310--350~MeV, squarely in the QCD scale range. This is the
   336â†’sBootstrap connection made quantitative: \textbf{the lepton triple
   337â†’and the meson seed triple operate at the same scale.}
   338â†’
   339â†’For the lepton triple, the coincidence is numerically sharp:
   340â†’\begin{equation}
   341â†’M_0 \;\approx\; \frac{\fpi^2}{27}\,,
   342â†’\end{equation}
   343â†’to $0.3\%$: the right-hand side gives $314.9\MeV$ versus
   344â†’the fitted $M_0 = 313.8\MeV$.  Equivalently,
   345â†’$v_0 \approx \fpi/(3\sqrt{3})$.  If this identification is exact
   346â†’and $\delta_0 \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ (the three-instanton value),
   347â†’all three charged lepton masses follow from~$\fpi$ alone,
   348â†’each predicted to~${\sim}0.3\%$.
   349â†’
   350â†’%======================================================================
   351â†’\section{A Tale of Two Tuples}
   352â†’\label{sec:two_tuples}
   353â†’%======================================================================
   354â†’
   355â†’\subsection{From seed to full triple}
   356â†’
   357â†’The seed triples grow into full triples upon symmetry breaking:
   358â†’
   359â†’\begin{center}
   360â†’\begin{tabular}{lccc}
   361â†’\toprule
   362â†’Sector & Seed triple & Full triple & $v_0^2$ (full) \\
   363â†’\midrule
   364â†’Quarks & $(0, s, c)$ & $(-s, c, b)$ & 913 MeV \\
   365â†’Mesons & $(0, \pi, D_s)$ & $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & 1230 MeV \\
   366â†’Leptons & $(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & $(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 314 MeV \\
   367â†’\bottomrule
   368â†’\end{tabular}
   369â†’\end{center}
   370â†’
   371â†’In each case:
   372â†’\begin{itemize}
   373â†’\item The seed has a massless (or near-massless) member, two massive 
   374â†’members in the ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$, and $Q \approx 2/3$.
   375â†’\item The full triple has \textbf{no} massless member: the zero has 
   376â†’been lifted (to $m_e$, or the sign-flipped strange/pion mass). The 
   377â†’$b$ quark and $B$ meson are always present as flavor degrees of 
   378â†’freedom; what the breaking does is give them their \emph{distinctive 
   379â†’masses}---the specific values that complete the Koide triple. Before 
   380â†’breaking, the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$); 
   381â†’after, it acquires $\zch_b = 64.65\MeV^{1/2}$, the value selected 
   382â†’by the energy balance with $c$ and the sign-flipped $s$. The bloom 
   383â†’is not particle creation but \emph{mass generation}.
   384â†’\end{itemize}
   385â†’
   386â†’The ``tale of two tuples'' is this: \textbf{each sector presents
   387â†’first as a seed with a massless member, then ``blooms'' into a
   388â†’full triple when SUSY breaks.} The massless member is the necessary
   389â†’initial condition; the breaking lifts it and generates the full spectrum.
   390â†’
   391â†’\paragraph{The vacuum-charge doubling (new).}
   392â†’The vacuum charge $v_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum\zch_k$ of the quark seed
   393â†’is $v_0^{(s)} = (\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c})/3 = 15.10\MeV^{1/2}$.
   394â†’For the full triple, $v_0^{(f)} = (-\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} +
   395â†’\sqrt{m_b})/3 = 30.21\MeV^{1/2}$. Their ratio is
   396â†’\begin{equation*}
   397â†’  v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)} = 2.0005 \approx 2\,.
   398â†’\end{equation*}
   399â†’If the bloom exactly doubles the vacuum charge, then
   400â†’$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$, giving
   401â†’$m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$---matching the PDG value
   402â†’$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ to $0.1\sigma$ ($0.06\sigma$ including
   403â†’propagated input uncertainties; see \S\ref{subsec:fterm}). This relation is
   404â†’independent of the Koide condition: it constrains $m_b$ from
   405â†’$(m_s, m_c)$ alone, and is more precise (0.07\%) than the
   406â†’overlapping-triples prediction (0.5\%).  A tension exists, however:
   407â†’the exact seed Koide gives $m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$ (not the PDG
   408â†’$1{,}270$), and applying the $v_0$-doubling to this exact seed
   409â†’value gives $m_b = 4{,}233\MeV$ ($1.8\sigma$ from PDG). The
   410â†’$v_0$-doubling works \emph{with the physical} $m_c$, not the exact
   411â†’seed value---so these two constraints are mutually inconsistent at the
   412â†’stated precision. The resolution may be that the seed Koide fixes the
   413â†’ratio $m_c/m_s$ only approximately (the 2.4\% charm deviation being a
   414â†’higher-order correction to the $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ condition), while the
   415â†’$v_0$-doubling constrains $m_b$ directly from the physical masses. In
   416â†’this reading, the seed condition is the approximate one and the
   417â†’$v_0$-doubling the more fundamental. This hierarchy is not derived;
   418â†’it is imposed by the data.
   419â†’
   420â†’\subsection{Precision summary}
   421â†’
   422â†’\begin{center}
   423â†’\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
   424â†’\toprule
   425â†’Triple & $Q$ & Dev.\ from $2/3$ & $v_0^2$ (MeV) & $\delta$ (rad) \\
   426â†’\midrule
   427â†’\multicolumn{5}{c}{\textit{Seed triples}} \\
   428â†’$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.6679 & 0.18\% & 351 & $\approx 3\pi/4$ \\
   429â†’$(0, s, c)$ & 0.6644 & 0.35\% & 228 & $\approx 3\pi/4$ \\
   430â†’$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.6667 & 0.001\% & 314 & 2.317 \\
   431â†’\midrule
   432â†’\multicolumn{5}{c}{\textit{Full triples}} \\
   433â†’$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.66666 & 0.001\% & 314 & 2.317 \\
   434â†’$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & 0.6674 & 0.10\% & 1230 & 2.806 \\
   435â†’$(-s, c, b)$ & 0.6750 & 1.24\% & 913 & 2.744 \\
   436â†’$(c, b, t)$ & 0.6695 & 0.42\% & 29\,580 & 2.163 \\
   437â†’\bottomrule
   438â†’\end{tabular}
   439â†’\end{center}
   440â†’
   441â†’The Koide phases $\delta$ cluster in the range $2.2$--$2.8$~rad
   442â†’($126^\circ$--$161^\circ$), all displaced from the seed value
   443â†’$3\pi/4 \approx 2.356$. The lepton triple barely moved from its
   444â†’seed; the quark and meson triples bloomed further. All phases
   445â†’lie within one $\mathbb{Z}_3$ fundamental domain ($[0, 2\pi/3]$
   446â†’modulo $2\pi/3$).
   447â†’
   448â†’The lepton triple is unique: seed and full triple are nearly
   449â†’identical, because the electron mass is small enough to serve as
   450â†’the ``zero'' while being nonzero enough to make $Q$ extremely
   451â†’precise. The $(-s,c,b)$ triple has the worst deviation (1.24\%);
   452â†’this is expected because the bloom is a nonperturbative process
   453â†’that does not exactly preserve $Q$. The $v_0$-doubling constrains
   454â†’$m_b$ independently (to 0.07\% via $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} +
   455â†’\sqrt{m_c}$), so the 1.24\% Koide deviation is the residual after
   456â†’the more fundamental $v_0$-constraint is satisfied.
   457â†’
   458â†’\subsection{Orthogonality of seed and full triples}
   459â†’
   460â†’Each Koide triple defines a perturbation vector 
   461â†’$\vec{z} = (z_1, z_2, z_3)$ constrained to the plane $\sum z_k = 0$ 
   462â†’with length $|\vec{z}|^2 = 3z_0^2$. The angle between seed and 
   463â†’full triples measures how much the breaking rotates the spectrum 
   464â†’in charge space:
   465â†’
   466â†’\begin{center}
   467â†’\begin{tabular}{lc}
   468â†’\toprule
   469â†’Pair of triples & Angle \\
   470â†’\midrule
   471â†’$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(-s,c,b)$ & $22^\circ$ \\
   472â†’$(0,\pi,D_s)$ vs.\ $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ & $26^\circ$ \\
   473â†’$(e,\mu,\tau)$ vs.\ $(0,\mu,\tau)$ & $0.8^\circ$ \\
   474â†’$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(0,\pi,D_s)$ & $0.3^\circ$ \\
   475â†’\bottomrule
   476â†’\end{tabular}
   477â†’\end{center}
   478â†’
   479â†’Two results are notable. First, the quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson 
   480â†’seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ are \textbf{nearly parallel} ($0.3^\circ$): the 
   481â†’meson seed is the composite image of the quark seed, with the GOR 
   482â†’map preserving direction while changing scale. Second, the lepton 
   483â†’triple is essentially its own seed ($0.8^\circ$)---the bloom has 
   484â†’barely begun.
   485â†’
   486â†’
   487â†’%======================================================================
   488â†’\section{The Meson Koide Triple: $(-\pi, D_s, B)$}
   489â†’\label{sec:mesonkoide}
   490â†’%======================================================================
   491â†’
   492â†’With the pion charge negative, the triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ satisfies:
   493â†’\begin{equation}
   494â†’Q(-\pi, D_s, B) = 
   495â†’\frac{m_\pi + m_{D_s} + m_B}{(-\sqrt{m_\pi} + \sqrt{m_{D_s}} + \sqrt{m_B})^2}
   496â†’= 0.6674\,,
   497â†’\end{equation}
   498â†’deviating from $2/3$ by $0.10\%$---the second most precise Koide 
   499â†’triple known. The energy balance:
   500â†’\begin{equation}
   501â†’\frac{\langle z_k^2\rangle}{z_0^2} = 1.002\,.
   502â†’\end{equation}
   503â†’
   504â†’For comparison, the ``standard'' all-positive quark triples $(d,s,b)$ 
   505â†’and $(u,c,t)$ deviate from $Q = 2/3$ by 9.7\% and 27\% respectively 
   506â†’at $\overline{\text{MS}}$ masses. \textbf{The meson triple is more
   507â†’precise than any quark triple}, including $(c,b,t)$ (0.42\%).
   508â†’
   509â†’The pion's negative charge has physical meaning: as a pseudo-Goldstone
   510â†’boson, the pion would be massless ($\zch = 0$) if chiral symmetry were
   511â†’exact. It sits just below zero ($\zch_\pi = -11.81$), approaching zero
   512â†’from the negative side as $m_q \to 0$. The sign of $\zch$ encodes
   513â†’Goldstone nature: pseudo-Goldstone bosons sit on the negative side
   514â†’of the critical point, having crossed through zero from positive
   515â†’territory during chiral symmetry breaking.
   516â†’
   517â†’\paragraph{Look-elsewhere correction.}
   518â†’Eleven pseudoscalar mesons ($\pi$, $K$, $\eta$, $\eta'$, $D$,
   519â†’$D_s$, $B$, $B_s$, $B_c$, $\eta_c$, $\eta_b$) yield
   520â†’$\binom{11}{3} = 165$ triples, times
   521â†’four independent sign patterns, giving 660 combinations.
   522â†’Scanning all 660, only $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ and the closely related
   523â†’$(-\pi, D_s, B_s)$ (deviation $0.14\%$) lie within $0.2\%$ of
   524â†’$Q = 2/3$. A Monte Carlo with $10^7$ random triples drawn
   525â†’from $[100,10{,}000]\MeV$ gives $p_{\text{single}} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$
   526â†’for a match at $0.10\%$; correcting for 660 trials yields
   527â†’$p_{\text{LEE}} = 0.13$ ($1.1\sigma$). The meson Koide triple
   528â†’is therefore \emph{not} statistically significant on its own,
   529â†’unlike the lepton triple ($2.8\sigma$ after LEE). Its interest
   530â†’lies in the structural pattern: all six closest hits share the
   531â†’sign assignment $(-\pi, D_{(s)}, B_{(s,c)})$, i.e.\ a negative
   532â†’pion paired with charm-containing and bottom-containing mesons---the
   533â†’combination predicted by the sBootstrap's $SU(5)$ flavor structure.
   534â†’
   535â†’%======================================================================
   536â†’\section{The Third Tuple: $(c, b, t)$}
   537â†’\label{sec:cbt}
   538â†’%======================================================================
   539â†’
   540â†’\subsection{A tuple without mesons}
   541â†’
   542â†’The triple $(c, b, t)$ with all positive signs gives $Q = 0.6695$, 
   543â†’deviating by $0.42\%$. Its Koide scale $v_0^2 \approx 29.6\GeV$ 
   544â†’is far above the QCD scale.
   545â†’
   546â†’This triple has \textbf{no meson counterpart}: the top quark decays 
   547â†’weakly ($t \to Wb$, lifetime $\sim 5 \times 10^{-25}$~s) before the 
   548â†’strong interaction can bind it ($\tau_{\text{QCD}} \sim 1/\Lambda_{\text{QCD}} 
   549â†’\sim 10^{-24}$~s). No $T$-meson ($t\bar{q}$) exists. The meson matrix

549â†’\sim 10^{-24}$~s). No $T$-meson ($t\bar{q}$) exists. The meson matrix 
   550â†’$M^i{}_j$ extends only to $b$-flavored states; the top sector lives 
   551â†’purely at the quark level.
   552â†’
   553â†’\subsection{Diquarks and the top}
   554â†’
   555â†’In the sBootstrap, diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ are the scalar partners of 
   556â†’antiquarks $\bar{q}_k$, with the baryon acting as the SUSY generator 
   557â†’\cite{CattoGursey, Miyazawa}. For five active flavors 
   558â†’$(u, d, s, c, b)$, the diquarks form a representation of $SU(5)_f$.
   559â†’
   560â†’The top diquarks $[tq]$ are kinematic objects only---they do not 
   561â†’hadronize. Yet their masses should follow the same pattern as other 
   562â†’diquarks: \textbf{there is no dynamical reason for top diquark masses 
   563â†’to differ from the others}. The $(c,b,t)$ Koide triple governs the 
   564â†’quark masses that enter the diquark sector, and its energy balance 
   565â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ constrains the possible diquark 
   566â†’spectrum even though the top diquarks are never observed as bound 
   567â†’states.
   568â†’
   569â†’\subsection{The $(c,b,t)$ triple has no seed}
   570â†’
   571â†’Unlike the $(0,s,c)$ and $(0,\pi,D_s)$ seeds, there is no obvious 
   572â†’$(0, x, y)$ seed for the $(c,b,t)$ triple. No pair of quarks in this 
   573â†’sector satisfies $\sqrt{m_{\text{light}}}/\sqrt{m_{\text{heavy}}} 
   574â†’\approx 2 - \sqrt{3}$: one has $\sqrt{m_c}/\sqrt{m_t} = 0.086$ 
   575â†’(too small) and $\sqrt{m_c}/\sqrt{m_b} = 0.551$ (too large).
   576â†’
   577â†’This means $(c,b,t)$ is \emph{not} a ``bloomed seed'' in the same 
   578â†’sense as $(-s,c,b)$. It may represent a fundamentally different 
   579â†’structure---a triple that was never seeded by a massless member but 
   580â†’instead emerged fully formed at a higher breaking scale, perhaps 
   581â†’connected to electroweak symmetry breaking rather than to chiral 
   582â†’symmetry breaking.
   583â†’
   584â†’%======================================================================
   585â†’\section{Mass Relations and Iterative Chains}
   586â†’\label{sec:mass_chains}
   587â†’%======================================================================
   588â†’
   589â†’The preceding sections established the Koide energy-balance condition
   590â†’$Q = 2/3$ and identified the triples that satisfy it. In this section
   591â†’we quantify how special the lepton triple really is, introduce the
   592â†’angular parametrization that reveals a hidden rational structure in
   593â†’the Koide phase, and trace an iterative chain that connects the heavy
   594â†’quarks to the strange sector. We report both successes and failures:
   595â†’the scaling rule that generates the quark triple from the lepton
   596â†’triple works once and then stops, and the iterative chain stalls at
   597â†’a mass too light for any known fermion. These negative results
   598â†’constrain the scope of the pattern.
   599â†’
   600â†’\subsection{The lepton Koide formula: how special?}
   601â†’Using PDG~2024 values $m_e = 0.51100\MeV$, $m_\mu = 105.658\MeV$,
   602â†’$m_\tau = 1776.86 \pm 0.12\MeV$:
   603â†’\begin{equation}
   604â†’Q(e,\mu,\tau) = \frac{(\sqrt{m_e} + \sqrt{m_\mu} + \sqrt{m_\tau})^2}
   605â†’{m_e + m_\mu + m_\tau} = 1.500014 \pm 0.000015\,,
   606â†’\end{equation}
   607â†’where $Q = (\sum\sqrt{m})^2 / \sum m$ is the reciprocal of the
   608â†’convention in \S\ref{sec:koide_algebra}: the Koide condition is
   609â†’$Q = 3/2$ here, equivalently $\sum m / (\sum\sqrt{m})^2 = 2/3$
   610â†’there. We use the $3/2$ convention in this section to match
   611â†’the most common form in the Koide literature.
   612â†’The deviation from $3/2$ is $|Q - 3/2| = 1.38 \times 10^{-5}$,
   613â†’corresponding to $0.91\sigma$ given the uncertainty dominated by
   614â†’$\delta m_\tau$.
   615â†’
   616â†’\paragraph{Look-elsewhere effect.}
   617â†’The Standard Model contains 9~charged fermions (3~leptons, 6~quarks).
   618â†’An exhaustive scan of all $\binom{9}{3} = 84$ mass triplets yields
   619â†’the ranking in Table~\ref{tab:koide_scan}.
   620â†’
   621â†’\begin{table}[ht]
   622â†’\centering
   623â†’\caption{Top-ranked SM triplets by proximity to $Q = 3/2$. The gap
   624â†’between rank~1 and rank~2 is a factor of~450.}
   625â†’\label{tab:koide_scan}
   626â†’\begin{tabular}{clc}
   627â†’\toprule
   628â†’Rank & Triplet & $|Q - 3/2|$ \\
   629â†’\midrule
   630â†’1 & $(e, \mu, \tau)$ & $1.38 \times 10^{-5}$ \\
   631â†’2 & $(c, b, t)$ & $6.2 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
   632â†’\bottomrule
   633â†’\end{tabular}
   634â†’\end{table}
   635â†’
   636â†’To assess the statistical weight of the lepton result, we performed a
   637â†’Monte Carlo study: $10{,}000$ random mass sets of 9~fermions drawn
   638â†’log-uniform on $[0.1, 200{,}000]\MeV$ were scanned for the best
   639â†’triplet. The fraction with $|Q - 3/2|_{\min} \leq 1.38 \times 10^{-5}$
   640â†’gives a look-elsewhere-corrected significance of $\mathbf{2.8\sigma}$.
   641â†’This is suggestive but not conclusive: the lepton Koide formula is
   642â†’unlikely to be a pure accident, but the evidence does not reach the
   643â†’discovery threshold.
   644â†’
   645â†’\paragraph{Extended scan with mesons.}
   646â†’Enlarging the pool to 30~particles (9~fundamental fermions, $W$, $Z$,
   647â†’$H$, $\fpi$, $v_{\text{EW}}$, and 16~pseudoscalar and vector mesons)
   648â†’with all $2^3$~sign combinations per triple yields $\binom{30}{3} = 4060$
   649â†’unique triples.  The lepton triple remains rank~1
   650â†’($|Q-3/2| = 1.4\times 10^{-5}$, $0.001\%$), but $(c,b,t)$ drops to
   651â†’rank~113 ($0.42\%$) and $(-s,c,b)$ to rank~324 ($1.24\%$): 112~meson-containing
   652â†’triples are closer to $Q = 3/2$ than $(c,b,t)$.  Twenty-one triples
   653â†’fall within $0.1\%$ of $3/2$, versus $\sim 12$~expected from a uniform
   654â†’distribution---a ratio of $1.7$, not statistically remarkable.  The
   655â†’second-ranked triple $(\tau, v_{\text{EW}}, B_c)$ reaches $Q = 1.50001$
   656â†’($0.004\%$), only~$3\times$ worse than the leptons, but involves a
   657â†’composite meson and a scale rather than three fundamental masses.
   658â†’\emph{Only $(e,\mu,\tau)$ involves three independent fundamental
   659â†’parameters; the significance of quark Koide triples is diluted by the
   660â†’composite-meson pool.}
   661â†’
   662â†’\subsection{The Koide angle parametrization}
   663â†’Any triple satisfying $Q = 3/2$ exactly can be written
   664â†’\cite{Koide1983}
   665â†’\begin{equation}
   666â†’\label{eq:koide_angle}
   667â†’\sqrt{m_k} = \sqrt{M_0}\left(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos\!\left(
   668â†’\frac{2\pi k}{3} + \delta_0\right)\right), \qquad k = 0,1,2\,,
   669â†’\end{equation}
   670â†’where $M_0 > 0$ is the scale parameter and $\delta_0 \in [0, 2\pi)$
   671â†’is the Koide phase. This is not an approximation: any three
   672â†’non-negative numbers whose $Q$-ratio equals $3/2$ are exactly
   673â†’parametrized by some $(M_0, \delta_0)$.
   674â†’
   675â†’Fitting to the charged lepton masses:
   676â†’\begin{equation}
   677â†’M_0 = 313.84\MeV\,, \qquad \delta_0 = 2.3166\;\text{rad}\,.
   678â†’\end{equation}
   679â†’The scale $M_0 \approx 314\MeV$ is the Koide scale $v_0^2$ of
   680â†’\S\ref{sec:seeds}, now identified as the mass parameter of the
   681â†’angular decomposition.
   682â†’
   683â†’\paragraph{The phase is close to $2/9$.}
   684â†’Unlike $Q = 2/3$, which is the algebraic consequence of the
   685â†’energy-balance condition (\S\ref{sec:koide_algebra}), the phase
   686â†’$\delta_0$ is a free parameter that the energy balance does
   687â†’not fix. Its value is an empirical datum, not a derived quantity.
   688â†’The parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle} has a $2\pi/3$ periodicity
   689â†’in $\delta_0$ (permuting the three masses). The physically
   690â†’meaningful quantity is the reduced phase
   691â†’\begin{equation}
   692â†’\delta_0 \bmod \frac{2\pi}{3} = 0.22222\;\text{rad}\,.
   693â†’\end{equation}
   694â†’This is strikingly close to the fraction $2/9 = 0.22222\ldots$:
   695â†’\begin{equation}
   696â†’\delta_0 \bmod \frac{2\pi}{3} - \frac{2}{9} = +7.4 \times 10^{-6}\,,
   697â†’\end{equation}
   698â†’a residual of $33\,\text{ppm}$ relative to $2/9$
   699â†’(using PDG 2024 central masses; within the $m_\tau$ uncertainty
   700â†’of $0.12\MeV$, the residual vanishes at $m_\tau \approx 1{,}776.97\MeV$,
   701â†’i.e.\ $0.9\sigma$ above central).
   702â†’
   703â†’\paragraph{Statistical significance of the rational approximation.}
   704â†’There are 266~reduced fractions $p/q$ with $q \leq 20$ and
   705â†’$0 < p/q < 2\pi/3$. The probability that a random phase (uniform
   706â†’on $[0, 2\pi/3)$) falls within $7.4 \times 10^{-6}$ of \emph{any}
   707â†’of these 266 fractions gives a look-elsewhere-corrected significance
   708â†’of $\mathbf{3.1\sigma}$. Evaluated specifically against the fraction
   709â†’$2/9$ alone (i.e., without look-elsewhere correction), the
   710â†’significance is $\mathbf{4.5\sigma}$.
   711â†’The deviation from~$2/9$ is $0.9\sigma$ in the
   712â†’$m_\tau$~uncertainty, consistent with an exact match.
   713â†’
   714â†’\subsection{The scaling rule: $M_1 = 3M_0$, $\delta_1 = 3\delta_0$}
   715â†’\label{subsec:scaling}
   716â†’
   717â†’A natural question is whether other Koide triples can be obtained
   718â†’from the lepton parameters by a simple scaling. The ansatz
   719â†’\begin{equation}
   720â†’M_1 = 3\,M_0 = 941.5\MeV\,, \qquad
   721â†’\delta_1 = 3\,\delta_0 = 6.9497\;\text{rad}\,,
   722â†’\end{equation}
   723â†’inserted into~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, predicts a quark triple.
   724â†’Table~\ref{tab:scaling_pred} compares predictions to PDG values.
   725â†’
   726â†’\begin{table}[ht]
   727â†’\centering
   728â†’\caption{Masses predicted by the $\times 3$ scaling rule vs.\ PDG
   729â†’central values. The quark Koide triple uses a negative sign for
   730â†’$\sqrt{m_s}$, as in \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.}
   731â†’\label{tab:scaling_pred}
   732â†’\begin{tabular}{lccc}
   733â†’\toprule
   734â†’Quark & Predicted (MeV) & PDG (MeV) & Deviation \\
   735â†’\midrule
   736â†’$s$ & 92.3 & 93.4 & $1.2\%$ \\
   737â†’$c$ & 1360 & 1270 & $7.1\%$ \\
   738â†’$b$ & 4197 & 4180 & $0.4\%$ \\
   739â†’\bottomrule
   740â†’\end{tabular}
   741â†’\end{table}
   742â†’
   743â†’The agreement for $s$ and $b$ is at the percent level; charm shows a
   744â†’larger ($7.1\%$) discrepancy. For the physical masses,
   745â†’$M_0(-s,c,b)/M_0(e,\mu,\tau) = 2.91$, not exactly $3$---the
   746â†’$\times 3$ rule is approximate. By contrast, the vacuum-charge
   747â†’doubling (\S\ref{sec:two_tuples}) predicts $m_b$ to $0.1\sigma$.
   748â†’The predicted triple satisfies the
   749â†’Koide condition with a negative square root for the strange quark:
   750â†’\begin{equation}
   751â†’Q(-\sqrt{m_s},\;\sqrt{m_c},\;\sqrt{m_b})
   752â†’= \frac{(-\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b})^2}{m_s + m_c + m_b}
   753â†’= \frac{3}{2}\,,
   754â†’\end{equation}
   755â†’holding to machine precision (residual $2.2 \times 10^{-16}$), since
   756â†’the masses are generated from the parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}
   757â†’which enforces $Q = 3/2$ identically.
   758â†’
   759â†’\paragraph{Renormalization caveat.}
   760â†’The PDG quark masses used in the comparison are quoted at different
   761â†’scales: $m_s(2\GeV)$, $m_c(m_c)$, $m_b(m_b)$, and $m_t$ as the pole
   762â†’mass. The predicted masses emerge at a single internal scale set by
   763â†’$M_1$. Running all masses to a common $\overline{\text{MS}}$ scale
   764â†’would change the individual deviations in Table~\ref{tab:scaling_pred}
   765â†’but not the structural point: the $\times 3$ scaling produces a
   766â†’recognizable quark triple. As noted in \S\ref{subsec:exactseed},
   767â†’QCD mass \emph{ratios} are RG invariants at leading order, so the
   768â†’Koide condition itself is scheme-independent, but the comparison
   769â†’of absolute masses to PDG values carries an $O(\alpha_s)$ ambiguity.
   770â†’
   771â†’\subsection{The second scaling fails}
   772â†’Iterating the scaling rule:
   773â†’\begin{equation}
   774â†’M_2 = 9\,M_0 = 2825\MeV\,, \qquad
   775â†’\delta_2 = 9\,\delta_0 = 20.849\;\text{rad}\,,
   776â†’\end{equation}
   777â†’gives predicted masses $(478,\; 92.3,\; 16{,}377)\MeV$. Only the
   778â†’middle value echoes $m_s$. The lightest ($478\MeV$) does not match
   779â†’any known quark mass, and the heaviest ($16.4\GeV$) falls between
   780â†’$m_b$ and $m_t$ with no candidate. No SM triplet accommodates these
   781â†’values.
   782â†’
   783â†’\textbf{The scaling stops at one step.} This is a negative result
   784â†’that constrains any attempt to build a recursive tower of Koide
   785â†’triples: the multiplicative rule $M \to 3M$, $\delta \to 3\delta$
   786â†’is not a general principle but a one-off relation between the lepton
   787â†’and quark sectors. Whether this single success is accidental or
   788â†’reflects a deeper connection (perhaps related to the three colors
   789â†’of QCD, or to the three generations) remains open.
   790â†’
   791â†’\subsection{Iterative chain: descending from $t$ and $b$}
   792â†’\label{subsec:chain}
   793â†’
   794â†’An alternative approach to connecting the quark masses is purely
   795â†’algebraic: given two known masses, solve the Koide equation
   796â†’$Q = 3/2$ for the third. Starting from the top and bottom quarks:
   797â†’
   798â†’\paragraph{Step~1: $Q(m_b, m_?, m_t) = 3/2$.}
   799â†’The Koide equation with $(m_b, m_t)$ known is quadratic in
   800â†’$\sqrt{m_?}$, yielding four branches (two signs for $\sqrt{m_?}$,
   801â†’two roots of each quadratic). The solutions are:
   802â†’\begin{equation}
   803â†’m_? = \{1357,\; 60{,}280,\; 1.34 \times 10^6,\;
   804â†’3.55 \times 10^6\}\MeV\,.
   805â†’\end{equation}
   806â†’Only the first branch gives a physically viable mass:
   807â†’$m_? = 1357\MeV$, which we identify with the charm quark
   808â†’(PDG: $m_c(m_c) = 1270\MeV$, deviation $6.8\%$).
   809â†’
   810â†’\paragraph{Step~2: $Q(m_c, m_?, m_b) = 3/2$.}
   811â†’With $m_c = 1357\MeV$ (the chain value, not the PDG value) and
   812â†’$m_b = 4180\MeV$:
   813â†’\begin{equation}
   814â†’m_? = 92.17\MeV\,,
   815â†’\end{equation}
   816â†’which we identify with the strange quark. The negative square root
   817â†’emerges naturally from the algebra---the solution selects the branch
   818â†’with $\sqrt{m_?} < 0$ in the signed-charge framework, consistent
   819â†’with the $(-s, c, b)$ structure of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.
   820â†’
   821â†’\paragraph{Step~3: the chain stalls.}
   822â†’Continuing from $(m_s, m_c)$ with $m_s = 92.17\MeV$:
   823â†’\begin{equation}
   824â†’m_? = 0.035\MeV\,.
   825â†’\end{equation}
   826â†’This is far too light for either $u$ ($2.16\MeV$) or $d$
   827â†’($4.67\MeV$). The chain does not reach the first-generation quarks;
   828â†’it stalls at a mass two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.
   829â†’
   830â†’\paragraph{Step~4: cyclic echo.}
   831â†’The stalled mass provides an algebraic consistency check.
   832â†’Taking $m_{\text{stall}} = 0.035\MeV$ and $m_s = 92.17\MeV$, and
   833â†’solving $Q(m_{\text{stall}}, m_?, m_s) = 3/2$:
   834â†’\begin{equation}
   835â†’m_? = 1357\MeV \quad \text{(to machine epsilon)}\,.
   836â†’\end{equation}
   837â†’The chain returns to its starting point: $t \to c \to s \to
   838â†’\text{stall} \to c \to \cdots$. This is not a numerical coincidence
   839â†’but an algebraic necessity: the Koide condition, combined with the
   840â†’specific masses of $b$ and $t$, defines a closed orbit in
   841â†’$(m_1, m_2, m_3)$ space. The cycle is:
   842â†’\begin{multline*}
   843â†’\cdots \;\to\; (b,t) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
   844â†’\;\to\; (c,b) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} s \\
   845â†’\;\to\; (s,c) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} \text{stall}
   846â†’\;\to\; (\text{stall}, s) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
   847â†’\;\to\; \cdots
   848â†’\end{multline*}
   849â†’
   850â†’The chain captures $c$, $b$, $s$ but misses $u$, $d$, and the
   851â†’leptons entirely. The first generation is outside the algebraic
   852â†’orbit seeded by the third generation.
   853â†’
   854â†’\subsection{Cross-checks and master comparison}
   855â†’Two independent routes lead to the quark masses: the
   856â†’lepton-seeded scaling rule (\S\ref{subsec:scaling}) and the
   857â†’top-bottom iterative chain (\S\ref{subsec:chain}).
   858â†’Table~\ref{tab:master} compares all predictions.
   859â†’
   860â†’\begin{table}[ht]
   861â†’\centering
   862â†’\caption{Master comparison of predicted quark masses (MeV). Chain~A
   863â†’descends from $(t,b)$ via the Koide equation. Chain~B applies
   864â†’the $\times 3$ scaling to the lepton triple. The small discrepancy
   865â†’between the two ($1357$ vs.\ $1360\MeV$ for charm) arises from
   866â†’different inputs: Chain~A uses the PDG $m_t$ and $m_b$ directly,
   867â†’while Chain~B uses the fitted $(M_0, \delta_0)$ from leptons.}
   868â†’\label{tab:master}
   869â†’\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
   870â†’\toprule
   871â†’Quark & Chain A & Chain B & PDG & Status \\
   872â†’ & (from $t,b$) & (from leptons) & & \\
   873â†’\midrule
   874â†’$s$ & 92.2 & 92.3 & 93.4 & $1\text{--}1.2\%$ deviation \\
   875â†’$c$ & 1357 & 1360 & 1270 & $7\text{--}7.1\%$ deviation \\
   876â†’$b$ & (input) & 4197 & 4180 & $0.4\%$ deviation \\
   877â†’$t$ & (input) & --- & 172\,760 & --- \\
   878â†’stall & 0.035 & --- & --- & no SM match \\
   879â†’$M_2$ set & --- & $(478,\,92,\,16377)$ & --- & no SM match \\
   880â†’\bottomrule
   881â†’\end{tabular}
   882â†’\end{table}
   883â†’
   884â†’The convergence of the two chains on $m_s \approx 92\MeV$ and
   885â†’$m_c \approx 1357\text{--}1360\MeV$ is the central positive result.
   886â†’Both routes, starting from completely different inputs (lepton masses
   887â†’vs.\ heavy quark masses), produce the same strange and charm masses
   888â†’to within $0.2\%$ of each other. Against PDG values, the strange
   889â†’quark prediction is excellent ($\sim 1\%$), and the bottom quark
   890â†’prediction is even better ($0.4\%$); the charm prediction shows a
   891â†’persistent $\sim 7\%$ overshoot.
   892â†’
   893â†’The negative results are equally informative:
   894â†’\begin{itemize}
   895â†’\item The $\times 3$ scaling does not iterate: the $\times 9$ step
   896â†’produces no viable triple.
   897â†’\item The iterative chain does not reach the first generation: it
   898â†’stalls at $0.035\MeV$, two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.
   899â†’\item The chain is algebraically cyclic: it loops through
   900â†’$(c, s, \text{stall})$ indefinitely, never escaping to new masses.
   901â†’\end{itemize}
   902â†’These failures delineate the boundary of the Koide pattern: it
   903â†’connects the second and third quark generations (and, via the
   904â†’scaling rule, the leptons), but does not extend to the first
   905â†’generation or beyond the Standard Model spectrum. Whatever
   906â†’dynamics underlies the energy-balance condition
   907â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ operates within a limited domain.
   908â†’
   909â†’%======================================================================
   910â†’\section{Composite Scalar Counting and Generation Uniqueness}
   911â†’\label{sec:counting}
   912â†’%======================================================================
   913â†’
   914â†’The sBootstrap rests on a single postulate: every scalar of the
   915â†’Supersymmetric Standard Model is a composite of two quarks (diquark,
   916â†’color triplet) or of a quark--antiquark pair (meson, color singlet),
   917â†’with the electric charge given by the sum of the constituents' charges.
   918â†’We now show that matching the number of such composites to the
   919â†’fermionic degrees of freedom of $N$ generations uniquely forces
   920â†’$N = 3$.
   921â†’
   922â†’\subsection{Setup and Diophantine system}
   923â†’Let there be $N$ generations, each containing quarks of two types:
   924â†’\emph{down} (charge $-1/3$) and \emph{up} (charge $+2/3$).  Not
   925â†’every quark need participate as a string endpoint; we allow
   926â†’$r$ light down-type quarks and $s$ light up-type quarks that form
   927â†’composites.  The Standard Model with right-handed neutrinos has
   928â†’$4N$ real (Weyl) fermionic degrees of freedom per charge sector in
   929â†’each color channel.  Matching composites to fermions, charge sector
   930â†’by charge sector, yields the following system.
   931â†’
   932â†’\begin{enumerate}
   933â†’\item \textbf{Charge $+1/3$ composites} (one up $\times$ one down,
   934â†’analogous to charged sleptons): $rs$ complex scalars must match
   935â†’$2N$ real fermionic d.o.f., giving
   936â†’\begin{equation}
   937â†’\label{eq:rs}
   938â†’rs = 2N\,.
   939â†’\end{equation}
   940â†’
   941â†’\item \textbf{Charge $-2/3$ composites} (symmetric down--down pairs,
   942â†’color sextet): $\binom{r+1}{2} = r(r+1)/2$ complex scalars must
   943â†’match $2N$ real fermionic d.o.f., giving
   944â†’\begin{equation}
   945â†’\label{eq:rr}
   946â†’\frac{r(r+1)}{2} = 2N\,.
   947â†’\end{equation}
   948â†’

948â†’
   949â†’\item \textbf{Neutral composites} (classified by $SU(r+s)$, minus
   950â†’the overall singlet trace; includes the partner of the right-handed
   951â†’neutrino): $(r+s)^2 - 1 = r^2 + s^2 + 2rs - 1$ states, of which
   952â†’$2rs$ are charged and already counted.  The neutral count is therefore
   953â†’$r^2 + s^2 - 1$, to be matched to $4N$ real d.o.f., giving
   954â†’\begin{equation}
   955â†’\label{eq:neutral}
   956â†’r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N\,.
   957â†’\end{equation}
   958â†’\end{enumerate}
   959â†’
   960â†’\noindent
   961â†’The alternative neutral equation $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 2N$ (left-handed
   962â†’neutrinos only) produces no positive-integer solution when combined
   963â†’with \eqref{eq:rs}--\eqref{eq:rr}; thus the sBootstrap \emph{requires}
   964â†’right-handed neutrinos.
   965â†’
   966â†’\subsection{The uniqueness theorem}
   967â†’
   968â†’\medskip
   969â†’\noindent\textbf{Theorem 1} (Generation uniqueness).
   970â†’\textit{The system \eqref{eq:rs}--\eqref{eq:neutral} has a unique
   971â†’solution over the positive integers:}
   972â†’\begin{equation}
   973â†’\boxed{(N,\,r,\,s) = (3,\,3,\,2)\,.}
   974â†’\end{equation}
   975â†’
   976â†’\medskip
   977â†’\noindent\textit{Proof.}
   978â†’Equating the right-hand sides of \eqref{eq:rs} and \eqref{eq:rr}
   979â†’gives $rs = r(r+1)/2$, hence
   980â†’\begin{equation}
   981â†’\label{eq:s_from_r}
   982â†’s = \frac{r+1}{2}\,,
   983â†’\end{equation}
   984â†’which requires $r$ odd.  Substituting \eqref{eq:rs} and
   985â†’\eqref{eq:s_from_r} into \eqref{eq:neutral}:
   986â†’\[
   987â†’r^2 + \Bigl(\frac{r+1}{2}\Bigr)^{\!2} - 1 \;=\; 4\,\cdot\,\frac{r\,(r+1)/2}{2}
   988â†’\;=\; r(r+1)\,.
   989â†’\]
   990â†’Expanding the left side,
   991â†’\[
   992â†’r^2 + \frac{r^2 + 2r + 1}{4} - 1 = r^2 + r\,,
   993â†’\]
   994â†’and clearing the denominator:
   995â†’\[
   996â†’4r^2 + r^2 + 2r + 1 - 4 = 4r^2 + 4r
   997â†’\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
   998â†’r^2 - 2r - 3 = 0\,,
   999â†’\]
  1000â†’i.e.\ $(r-3)(r+1) = 0$.  Since $r > 0$, we obtain $r = 3$, whence
  1001â†’$s = 2$ and $N = rs/2 = 3$.
  1002â†’\hfill$\square$
  1003â†’
  1004â†’\medskip
  1005â†’The solution has $r + s = 5$ light quarks acting as string endpoints,
  1006â†’furnishing the fundamental representation of an $SU(5)$ flavor group.
  1007â†’The top quark is the unique ``elephant''---too heavy to serve as a
  1008â†’constituent of the scalar composites---in precise agreement with the
  1009â†’Standard Model, where $m_t \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ prevents
  1010â†’top-flavored hadrons from forming.
  1011â†’
  1012â†’\subsection{Partial solutions and the hexagonal-number family}
  1013â†’Equations \eqref{eq:rs} and \eqref{eq:rr} alone, without the
  1014â†’neutral constraint, require $2N$ to be a hexagonal number
  1015â†’$h_k = k(2k-1)$:
  1016â†’\[
  1017â†’2N = 1,\; 6,\; 15,\; 28,\; 45,\; 66,\; \ldots
  1018â†’\]
  1019â†’Of these, only $2N = 1$ and $2N = 6$ have $r \leq 16$ (the
  1020â†’asymptotic-freedom bound $2n_f < 33$ limits $r + s \leq 16$).
  1021â†’The first is inadmissible ($N = 1/2$); the second gives $N = 3$.
  1022â†’
  1023â†’\medskip
  1024â†’\noindent\textbf{Remark} (The next hexagonal solution).
  1025â†’The case $(N, r, s) = (14, 7, 4)$ with
  1026â†’$r + s = 11$ light flavors and $2N = 28$ total satisfies
  1027â†’\eqref{eq:rs}--\eqref{eq:rr} but fails \eqref{eq:neutral}:
  1028â†’$r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 64 \neq 4 \times 14 = 56$.
  1029â†’Asymptotic freedom ($b_0 > 0$ in the QCD beta function) would
  1030â†’also require $2n_f < 33$, i.e.\ $n_f \leq 16$; with $N = 14$
  1031â†’generations one would need $2N = 28$ flavors, which violates the
  1032â†’bound decisively.  The neutral equation and the asymptotic-freedom
  1033â†’bound independently single out $N = 3$.
  1034â†’
  1035â†’\subsection{Identification with $SU(5)$ representations}
  1036â†’With $r = 3$ and $s = 2$, the $r + s = 5$ light quarks form the
  1037â†’fundamental $\mathbf{5}$ of $SU(5)_f$.  The composites organize
  1038â†’into three $SU(5)$ representations:
  1039â†’
  1040â†’\paragraph{Mesons (quark--antiquark, color singlet):}
  1041â†’The $\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}}$ decomposes as
  1042â†’\begin{equation}
  1043â†’\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}}
  1044â†’= \mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{1}\,.
  1045â†’\end{equation}
  1046â†’The $\mathbf{24}$ carries the sleptons (charged and neutral) and
  1047â†’the meson matrix.  Under
  1048â†’$SU(5) \supset SU(3)_d \times SU(2)_u$:
  1049â†’\begin{equation}
  1050â†’\mathbf{24} = (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})
  1051â†’+ (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})
  1052â†’+ (\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})
  1053â†’+ (\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})
  1054â†’+ (\mathbf{8},\mathbf{1})\,.
  1055â†’\end{equation}
  1056â†’
  1057â†’\paragraph{Diquarks (quark--quark, color triplet):}
  1058â†’The symmetric product
  1059â†’\begin{equation}
  1060â†’\mathbf{5} \otimes_S \mathbf{5}
  1061â†’= \mathbf{15}
  1062â†’\end{equation}
  1063â†’decomposes as
  1064â†’\begin{equation}
  1065â†’\mathbf{15} = (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})
  1066â†’+ (\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})
  1067â†’+ (\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})\,,
  1068â†’\end{equation}
  1069â†’and its conjugate $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ provides the
  1070â†’antidiquarks.
  1071â†’
  1072â†’\medskip
  1073â†’\noindent\textbf{Proposition 1} (Symmetric product is forced).
  1074â†’\textit{The counting equation \eqref{eq:rr} selects the
  1075â†’symmetric product $\mathbf{15}$, not the antisymmetric
  1076â†’$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.}
  1077â†’
  1078â†’\medskip
  1079â†’\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1080â†’The charge $-2/3$ composites are down--down pairs.
  1081â†’Under $SU(3)_d$, the symmetric product
  1082â†’$\mathbf{3} \otimes_S \mathbf{3} = \mathbf{6}$ has dimension
  1083â†’$r(r+1)/2 = 6$, matching equation~\eqref{eq:rr}.  The antisymmetric
  1084â†’product $\mathbf{3} \otimes_A \mathbf{3} = \bar{\mathbf{3}}$ has
  1085â†’dimension $r(r-1)/2 = 3 \neq 2N = 6$.  The symmetric color sextet,
  1086â†’and hence the $\mathbf{15}$, is the unique choice consistent
  1087â†’with the sBootstrap counting.
  1088â†’\hfill$\square$
  1089â†’
  1090â†’\subsection{Charge census of $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15}
  1091â†’\oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$}
  1092â†’Treating the $SU(5)$ simultaneously as a Georgi--Glashow
  1093â†’unification group \cite{Rivero2005}, one assigns standard electric
  1094â†’charges via $Q_{\text{em}} = T_3 - Y/6$ to every component.
  1095â†’Table~\ref{tab:census} displays the resulting charge census for
  1096â†’$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  1097â†’
  1098â†’\begin{table}[ht]
  1099â†’\centering
  1100â†’\begin{tabular}{lcccccc@{\qquad}c}
  1101â†’\toprule
  1102â†’& \multicolumn{6}{c}{Number of states at $|Q_{\text{em}}|$} & \\
  1103â†’\cmidrule(lr){2-7}
  1104â†’& $0$ & $\tfrac{1}{3}$ & $\tfrac{2}{3}$ & $1$
  1105â†’& $\tfrac{4}{3}$ & $2$ & Dim \\
  1106â†’\midrule
  1107â†’$\mathbf{24}$ & 10 & $2\!\times\!3$ & ---
  1108â†’& $2\!\times\!1$ & $2\!\times\!3$ & --- & 24 \\[2pt]
  1109â†’$\mathbf{15}$ & 1 & 3 & 9
  1110â†’& 1 & --- & 1 & 15 \\[2pt]
  1111â†’$\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ & 1 & 3 & 9
  1112â†’& 1 & --- & 1 & 15 \\[2pt]
  1113â†’\midrule
  1114â†’Total & 12 & $2\!\times\!6$ & $2\!\times\!9$
  1115â†’& $2\!\times\!2$ & $2\!\times\!3$ & $2\!\times\!1$ & 54 \\
  1116â†’\bottomrule
  1117â†’\end{tabular}
  1118â†’\caption{Charge census of
  1119â†’$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1120â†’of $SU(5)$.
  1121â†’Notation ``$2\!\times\!k$'' means $k$ states at $+|Q_{\text{em}}|$ and $k$
  1122â†’at $-|Q_{\text{em}}|$; the $\mathbf{24}$ is self-conjugate.  In the
  1123â†’$\mathbf{15}$, the 9 states at $|Q_{\text{em}}|=2/3$ split as 3 from the
  1124â†’$(\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})_{1/6}$ component at $Q_{\text{em}} = +2/3$ and 6
  1125â†’from the $(\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})_{-2/3}$ color sextet at
  1126â†’$Q_{\text{em}} = -2/3$; the $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ is the conjugate.
  1127â†’Charges $\pm 4/3$ in the $\mathbf{24}$ arise from the leptoquark
  1128â†’components $(\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})_{-5/6}$ and
  1129â†’$(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})_{5/6}$.
  1130â†’The 12 neutral states comprise $8 + 1 + 1 = 10$ from the
  1131â†’$\mathbf{24}$ plus one each from $\mathbf{15}$ and
  1132â†’$\overline{\mathbf{15}}$.}
  1133â†’\label{tab:census}
  1134â†’\end{table}
  1135â†’
  1136â†’\noindent
  1137â†’Three cross-checks tie the census to the Diophantine system:
  1138â†’\begin{itemize}
  1139â†’\item \textbf{Neutral composites:} The 12 states at $Q_{\text{em}} = 0$
  1140â†’match $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 9 + 4 - 1 = 12 = 4N$.
  1141â†’\item \textbf{Charge $+1/3$ composites} (one up $\times$ one
  1142â†’down): $rs = 3 \times 2 = 6$ composites, matching equation
  1143â†’\eqref{eq:rs} with $rs = 6 = 2N$.  These states distribute as
  1144â†’3 from $(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})_{5/6} \subset \mathbf{24}$
  1145â†’and 3 from $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  1146â†’\item \textbf{Charge $-2/3$ composites} (symmetric down--down):
  1147â†’$r(r+1)/2 = 6$ symmetric pairs fill the color sextet
  1148â†’$(\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})_{-2/3} \subset \mathbf{15}$, matching
  1149â†’equation~\eqref{eq:rr} with $r(r+1)/2 = 6 = 2N$.
  1150â†’\end{itemize}
  1151â†’
  1152â†’\subsection{Anomaly cancellation and asymptotic freedom}
  1153â†’The representation $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus
  1154â†’\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ must be anomaly-free if it is to appear
  1155â†’in a consistent gauge theory.
  1156â†’
  1157â†’\medskip
  1158â†’\noindent\textbf{Proposition 2} (Anomaly cancellation).
  1159â†’\textit{The cubic $SU(5)$ anomaly of $\mathbf{24} \oplus
  1160â†’\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ vanishes.}
  1161â†’
  1162â†’\medskip
  1163â†’\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1164â†’The anomaly coefficient $A(\mathbf{R})$ for $SU(n)$ satisfies
  1165â†’$A(\text{adjoint}) = 0$ (the adjoint is real),
  1166â†’$A(\mathbf{15}) = n + 4 = 9$, and
  1167â†’$A(\overline{\mathbf{15}}) = -(n+4) = -9$.  Therefore
  1168â†’\[
  1169â†’A(\mathbf{24}) + A(\mathbf{15}) + A(\overline{\mathbf{15}})
  1170â†’= 0 + 9 + (-9) = 0\,.
  1171â†’\]
  1172â†’\hfill$\square$
  1173â†’
  1174â†’\medskip
  1175â†’\noindent\textbf{Proposition 3} (Asymptotic freedom).
  1176â†’\textit{An $SU(5)$ gauge theory with matter in the
  1177â†’$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1178â†’is asymptotically free.}
  1179â†’
  1180â†’\medskip
  1181â†’\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1182â†’The one-loop beta-function coefficient is
  1183â†’\[
  1184â†’b_0 = \frac{11}{3}\,C_2(G) - \frac{2}{3}\,\sum_{\text{Weyl}} T(\mathbf{R})\,,
  1185â†’\]
  1186â†’where $C_2(G) = n = 5$ for $SU(5)$, $T(\mathbf{adj}) = n = 5$,
  1187â†’and $T(\mathbf{15}) = (n+2)/2 = 7/2$.  The Weyl fermion content
  1188â†’consists of one adjoint and one $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1189â†’(treating each as a Weyl fermion), giving
  1190â†’$\sum T = 5 + 7/2 + 7/2 = 12$.  Therefore
  1191â†’\[
  1192â†’b_0 = \frac{11}{3} \times 5 - \frac{2}{3} \times 12
  1193â†’= \frac{55 - 24}{3} = \frac{31}{3} > 0\,.
  1194â†’\]
  1195â†’\hfill$\square$
  1196â†’
  1197â†’\medskip
  1198â†’Both properties are nontrivial: anomaly cancellation ensures
  1199â†’the gauge symmetry is quantum-mechanically consistent, and
  1200â†’$b_0 > 0$ ensures the theory is well-defined in the ultraviolet.
  1201â†’Together they confirm that the representation forced by the
  1202â†’sBootstrap counting is physically viable.
  1203â†’
  1204â†’\subsection{Summary of the counting argument}
  1205â†’The logical chain is:
  1206â†’\begin{enumerate}
  1207â†’\item The sBootstrap postulate (composites $=$ scalars of the SSM)
  1208â†’yields three Diophantine equations in $(N, r, s)$.
  1209â†’\item The unique positive-integer solution is $(3, 3, 2)$:
  1210â†’three generations, with the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ forced over
  1211â†’the antisymmetric $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.
  1212â†’\item The solution organizes into $SU(5)$ representations
  1213â†’$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$,
  1214â†’which are anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
  1215â†’\item Right-handed neutrinos are required: without them, no
  1216â†’integer solution exists.
  1217â†’\item The top quark is excluded from the composite sector by
  1218â†’the counting itself ($s = 2 < N = 3$), matching its
  1219â†’kinematic decoupling ($m_t \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$).
  1220â†’\end{enumerate}
  1221â†’
  1222â†’%======================================================================
  1223â†’\section{The Diquark Sector and SU(5) Flavor}
  1224â†’\label{sec:diquarks}
  1225â†’%======================================================================
  1226â†’
  1227â†’\subsection{Counting partners}
  1228â†’
  1229â†’In the full sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005}, the scalar partners of 
  1230â†’the Standard Model fermions include both mesons ($q\bar{q}$) and 
  1231â†’diquarks ($qq$). For five active flavors, the meson matrix gives 
  1232â†’$5 \times 5 = 25$ scalars. The diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ form a 
  1233â†’representation of $SU(5)_f$:
  1234â†’\begin{equation}
  1235â†’\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{5} = \overline{\mathbf{10}}_A 
  1236â†’\oplus \mathbf{15}_S\,.
  1237â†’\end{equation}
  1238â†’
  1239â†’The symmetric $\mathbf{15}_S$ gives 15 diquarks including 
  1240â†’same-flavor states $[uu]$, $[dd]$, \ldots, while the antisymmetric 
  1241â†’$\overline{\mathbf{10}}_A$ gives 10 diquarks with $i \neq j$ only.
  1242â†’
  1243â†’\subsection{The symmetric representation}
  1244â†’\label{subsec:sym15}
  1245â†’
  1246â†’The sBootstrap SUSY generator acts asymmetrically on the two
  1247â†’fermion sectors. When it acts on a \emph{lepton}, it produces a
  1248â†’meson: $\mathcal{Q} \cdot \ell \sim q\bar{q}$. Since the quark and antiquark 
  1249â†’are distinguishable, the meson lies in the full 
  1250â†’$\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}} = \mathbf{24} + \mathbf{1}$. 
  1251â†’There is no Pauli constraint, and the meson spectrum is perfectly 
  1252â†’reproduced.
  1253â†’
  1254â†’When it acts on a \emph{quark}, it produces a diquark:
  1255â†’$\mathcal{Q} \cdot q_i \sim q_i q_j$. The bosonic SUSY generator applied to 
  1256â†’a single flavor index $i$ naturally produces the \textbf{symmetric} 
  1257â†’product---the $\mathbf{15}_S$ representation, not the antisymmetric 
  1258â†’$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. This is the representation identified 
  1259â†’in the $SO(32)$ decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}: the adjoint of 
  1260â†’$SO(32)$ under $SU(5)_f$ decomposes to include the 
  1261â†’$\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ (diquarks) alongside the 
  1262â†’$\mathbf{1} + \mathbf{24}$ (mesons).
  1263â†’
  1264â†’The 15 includes 5 same-flavor states ($[uu]$, $[dd]$, $[ss]$, 
  1265â†’$[cc]$, $[bb]$) absent from the $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. Among 
  1266â†’these are exotic $+4/3$-charged diquarks such as $[uu]$. The cost: 
  1267â†’an additional chiral $+4/3$ quark per generation, as identified 
  1268â†’in \cite{Rivero2005}. Whether these exotic states appear in nature 
  1269â†’as resonances, or whether they decouple at some high scale, is an 
  1270â†’open question.
  1271â†’
  1272â†’\subsection{The Pauli theorem for scalar diquarks}
  1273â†’\label{subsec:pauli}
  1274â†’
  1275â†’The requirement of the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ conflicts with 
  1276â†’Fermi statistics---not merely for $L=0$ diquarks, but for 
  1277â†’\emph{all} scalar diquarks in the color $\bar{3}$ channel, 
  1278â†’regardless of internal orbital structure.
  1279â†’
  1280â†’\medskip
  1281â†’\noindent\textbf{Theorem.}
  1282â†’\textit{For a spin-0 ($J=0$) diquark in the color $\bar{3}$ 
  1283â†’(antisymmetric) representation, the flavor wave function is 
  1284â†’antisymmetric for \textbf{all} values of the orbital angular 
  1285â†’momentum $L$ and spin $S$.}
  1286â†’
  1287â†’\medskip
  1288â†’\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1289â†’$J = 0$ requires $L = S$ (since $|L - S| \leq J \leq L + S$). 
  1290â†’The exchange symmetry of the spin-orbital wave function is 
  1291â†’$(-1)^{L+S+1} = (-1)^{2L+1} = -1$ for all $L$. Combined with 
  1292â†’the color $\bar{3}$ factor $(-1)$, the non-flavor product is 
  1293â†’$(-1)(-1) = +1$ (symmetric). Total antisymmetry then requires 
  1294â†’flavor to be antisymmetric: $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. \hfill$\square$
  1295â†’
  1296â†’\medskip
  1297â†’This result is purely kinematic---independent of dynamics, 
  1298â†’binding mechanisms, or spatial extent. No wave function, however 
  1299â†’extended, can produce a symmetric-flavor scalar diquark in color 
  1300â†’$\bar{3}$ while respecting Fermi statistics. The key cases:
  1301â†’
  1302â†’\begin{center}
  1303â†’\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
  1304â†’\toprule
  1305â†’$L$ & $S$ & Spin-orbital $(-1)^{2L+1}$ & $\times$ Color & Flavor \\
  1306â†’\midrule
  1307â†’0 & 0 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1308â†’1 & 1 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1309â†’2 & 2 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1310â†’\bottomrule
  1311â†’\end{tabular}
  1312â†’\end{center}
  1313â†’
  1314â†’The $L=1$, $S=1$ row closes the ``P-wave diquark'' escape route 
  1315â†’that might otherwise seem available.
  1316â†’
  1317â†’The theorem has a sharp consequence: \textbf{the sBootstrap 
  1318â†’diquark in the $\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite.} It 
  1319â†’must be either:
  1320â†’\begin{enumerate}
  1321â†’\item An algebraically generated object---the image of the 
  1322â†’Miyazawa supercharge \cite{Miyazawa, CattoGursey} acting on 
  1323â†’an antiquark, whose symmetry is determined by the superalgebra 
  1324â†’rather than by the statistics of constituent quarks; or
  1325â†’\item A fundamental string-theoretic object, as in the $SO(32)$ 
  1326â†’decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}, where the $\mathbf{15}$ 
  1327â†’arises from the adjoint representation of the gauge group and 
  1328â†’the open-string diquark is not built from point-like quarks.
  1329â†’\end{enumerate}
  1330â†’
  1331â†’The contrast with the meson sector is instructive. When the 
  1332â†’SUSY generator acts on a lepton, it produces a meson 
  1333â†’($q\bar{q}$): the quark and antiquark are distinguishable, 
  1334â†’no Pauli constraint applies, and the meson spectrum is 
  1335â†’reproduced without tension. When it acts on a quark, it 
  1336â†’produces a diquark in the ``wrong'' representation---and the 
  1337â†’theorem shows this cannot be fixed by any internal structure 
  1338â†’of a $qq$ pair. The asymmetry between the two SUSY directions 
  1339â†’is absolute: mesons work as composites; diquarks do not. 
  1340â†’This is an unresolved obstruction: the sBootstrap requires
  1341â†’the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$, but QCD composite diquarks cannot
  1342â†’fill it. The proposed resolutions---Miyazawa supercharges and
  1343â†’SO(32) string embeddings---are directions for future work, not
  1344â†’established mechanisms. The diquark sector remains the weakest
  1345â†’point of the program.
  1346â†’
  1347â†’%======================================================================

content.text: Now let me read the sections on Two Algebraic Structures (de Vries angle and Casimir equation), which go to Paper B.

content.content:
1347â†’%======================================================================
  1348â†’\section{Two Algebraic Structures}
  1349â†’\label{sec:two_structures}
  1350â†’%======================================================================
  1351â†’
  1352â†’The sBootstrap particle content and mass spectrum are constrained by
  1353â†’two \emph{independent} algebraic structures.  Part~A
  1354â†’(\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}--\ref{subsec:so32_open}) derives the
  1355â†’representation content from an $SO(32)$ embedding.  Part~B
  1356â†’(\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}--\ref{subsec:casimir_open}) derives an
  1357â†’electroweak mass ratio from a Casimir eigenvalue equation.
  1358â†’No algebraic connection between the two has been found
  1359â†’(see \S\ref{subsec:two_gap}).
  1360â†’
  1361â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1362â†’\subsection{Part A: The SO(32) embedding chain}
  1363â†’\label{subsec:so32_chain}
  1364â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1365â†’
  1366â†’The $SO(32)$ gauge group of Type~I / heterotic string theory
  1367â†’admits the maximal-subgroup chain
  1368â†’\begin{equation}
  1369â†’\label{eq:so32_chain}
  1370â†’SO(32) \;\supset\; SU(16) \times U(1)_A
  1371â†’  \;\supset\; SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B \,.
  1372â†’\end{equation}
  1373â†’The first step is the standard embedding $SO(2n) \supset SU(n)
  1374â†’\times U(1)$; the second decomposes $SU(16)$ under the regular
  1375â†’subalgebra $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_B$.
  1376â†’Here $SU(5)$ is the Georgi--Glashow unification group and $SU(3)$
  1377â†’is a \emph{horizontal} (flavor) symmetry acting on the three
  1378â†’light generations---it is \textbf{not} $SU(3)_c$.
  1379â†’
  1380â†’The fundamental $\mathbf{32}$ of $SO(32)$ decomposes as
  1381â†’\begin{equation}
  1382â†’\label{eq:32decomp}
  1383â†’\mathbf{32} = \underbrace{(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})}_{R_1}
  1384â†’  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})}_{R_2}
  1385â†’  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_3}
  1386â†’  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_4}\,,
  1387â†’\end{equation}
  1388â†’where $\dim = 15 + 15 + 1 + 1 = 32$ and the four summands carry
  1389â†’distinct $U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$ charges.
  1390â†’
  1391â†’\subsection{Decomposition of the adjoint}
  1392â†’The adjoint of $SO(32)$ is the antisymmetric square of the
  1393â†’fundamental: $\mathbf{496} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$.  Distributing
  1394â†’over the four summands in \eqref{eq:32decomp} and using the identity
  1395â†’\begin{equation}
  1396â†’\wedge^2(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})
  1397â†’  = \bigl[\wedge^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes S^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1398â†’    \;\oplus\;
  1399â†’    \bigl[S^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1400â†’  = (\mathbf{10},\mathbf{6})
  1401â†’    \;\oplus\;
  1402â†’    (\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})\,,
  1403â†’\end{equation}
  1404â†’one obtains the full decomposition listed in
  1405â†’Table~\ref{tab:496}.
  1406â†’
  1407â†’\begin{table}[ht]

  1410â†’under $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$.}
  1411â†’\label{tab:496}
  1412â†’\begin{tabular}{lcrrc}
  1413â†’\toprule
