  1452â†’
  1453â†’\subsection{Physical content and the projection problem}
  1454â†’\label{subsec:so32_open}
  1455â†’
  1456â†’Restricting to the adjoint sector $Q_A = Q_B = 0$ and dropping the
  1457â†’horizontal $SU(3)$ factor, one reads off the $SU(5)$ content:
  1458â†’\begin{equation}
  1459â†’\mathbf{24} \;\oplus\; \mathbf{1}\,,
  1460â†’\end{equation}
  1461â†’which is precisely the meson/slepton content of the sBootstrap.
  1462â†’The matter sector at $|Q_A| = 2$ contains
  1463â†’\begin{equation}
  1464â†’\mathbf{15} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{15}}
  1465â†’  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{10} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{10}}
  1466â†’  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{5} \;\oplus\; \bar{\mathbf{5}}\,,
  1467â†’\end{equation}
  1468â†’among which the $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ are
  1469â†’the symmetric diquarks demanded by the sBootstrap counting
  1470â†’(\S\ref{subsec:sym15}).
  1471â†’
  1472â†’Thus the sBootstrap representations appear in the $SO(32)$
  1473â†’adjoint: the $\mathbf{24}$ in the neutral sector ($Q_A\!=\!Q_B\!=\!0$)
  1474â†’and the $\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ in the matter
  1475â†’sector ($|Q_A|\!=\!2$). However, the remaining $496 - 54 = 442$
  1476â†’states (the $\mathbf{10}$, $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$,
  1477â†’$\mathbf{5}$, $\bar{\mathbf{5}}$, the SU(3)-horizontal copies,
  1478â†’and the neutral singlets) must be projected out or made massive.
  1479â†’The mechanism for this projection---whether by an orbifold,
  1480â†’a GSO-type condition, or a flux background---is an open problem.
  1481â†’
  1482â†’This embedding was first noted in \cite{Rivero2005}.  Its role
  1483â†’here is to provide a UV origin for the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$
  1484â†’that the Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) forbids at the
  1485â†’level of point-particle composites: in the string picture,
  1486â†’diquarks are open-string states whose symmetry is determined by
  1487â†’the gauge group, not by Fermi statistics of constituents.
  1488â†’
  1489â†’\paragraph{Why $SO(32)$ and not $E_8 \times E_8$.}
  1490â†’Anomaly cancellation in ten dimensions selects two gauge groups:
  1491â†’$SO(32)$ and $E_8 \times E_8$. The latter cannot accommodate the
  1492â†’sBootstrap. Every decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces
  1493â†’the antisymmetric $\mathbf{10}$, never the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$:
  1494â†’the maximal-subgroup chain $E_8 \supset SU(5) \times SU(5)$ gives
  1495â†’$\mathbf{248} = (\mathbf{24},\mathbf{1}) + (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{24})
  1496â†’+ (\mathbf{10},\bar{\mathbf{5}}) + (\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{5})
  1497â†’+ (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{10}) + (\bar{\mathbf{5}},\overline{\mathbf{10}})$,
  1498â†’with no $\mathbf{15}$ appearing anywhere.  The obstruction is
  1499â†’irreducible: it reflects the fact that $E_8$'s simply-laced root
  1500â†’lattice admits only antisymmetric tensor representations of
  1501â†’$SU(5)$.  By contrast, $SO(32)$'s adjoint $\mathbf{496} =
  1502â†’\wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$ naturally contains the symmetric
  1503â†’$\mathbf{15}$ through the identity
  1504â†’$\wedge^2(\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{3}) \supset
  1505â†’S^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})
  1506â†’= (\mathbf{15}, \bar{\mathbf{3}})$.  The Type~I realization, where
  1507â†’quark Chan-Paton factors directly generate $SO(32)$ from
  1508â†’$5\!\times\!3 + 1 = 16$ endpoint labels, is the most natural home.
  1509â†’
  1510â†’The $E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic string is connected to Type~I $SO(32)$
  1511â†’by a chain of dualities (S-duality to heterotic $SO(32)$, then
  1512â†’T-duality on a non-simply-connected torus).  These dualities may
  1513â†’allow the sBootstrap content to appear in the $E_8 \times E_8$ frame
  1514â†’through a non-standard embedding, but the direct decomposition
  1515â†’route is blocked by the $\mathbf{15}$ vs.\ $\mathbf{10}$
  1516â†’obstruction.
  1517â†’
  1518â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1519â†’\subsection{Part B: The Casimir eigenvalue equation}
  1520â†’\label{subsec:casimir_eq}
  1521â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1522â†’
  1523â†’Consider the eigenvalue equation
  1524â†’\begin{equation}
  1525â†’\label{eq:casimir_quartic}
  1526â†’x^2 \;+\; C_2\,x \;-\; C_2 = 0\,,
  1527â†’\qquad C_2 = s(s+1)\,,
  1528â†’\end{equation}
  1529â†’where $C_2$ is the quadratic Casimir of $SU(2)$ at spin $s$ and
  1530â†’$x = M^2/m^2$ for a mass scale $m$ to be determined.  Equivalently,
  1531â†’$x^2/(1-x) = C_2$: the eigenvalue is a self-consistent solution
  1532â†’where the ``squared coupling'' $x^2$ equals $C_2$ times the
  1533â†’complement $(1-x)$.  The solutions are
  1534â†’\begin{equation}
  1535â†’\label{eq:casimir_roots}
  1536â†’x_{\pm}(s) = \frac{-C_2 \pm \sqrt{C_2^2 + 4C_2}}{2}\,.
  1537â†’\end{equation}
  1538â†’
  1539â†’\paragraph{Structural constraints (codimension analysis).}
  1540â†’The most general two-parameter family of such equations is
  1541â†’\begin{equation}
  1542â†’x^2 + (f_0 + f_1 C_2)\,x + (g_0 + g_1 C_2) = 0\,,
  1543â†’\end{equation}
  1544â†’with four real coefficients $(f_0, f_1, g_0, g_1)$.  Three
  1545â†’structural conditions pin the polynomial to \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}:
  1546â†’\begin{enumerate}
  1547â†’\item $f_0 = 0$: no spin-independent mass term;
  1548â†’\item $g_0 = 0$: a massless eigenstate exists at $s = 0$;
  1549â†’\item $g_1 = -f_1$: coefficient antisymmetry
  1550â†’  (the constant and linear terms in $C_2$ are related).
  1551â†’\end{enumerate}
  1552â†’Together with the normalization $f_1 = 1$, these exhaust the
  1553â†’freedom.  A systematic scan of 582 polynomial alternatives
  1554â†’(quadratics, cubics, quartics with Casimir-type functions and
  1555â†’rational coefficients) confirms uniqueness: no other
  1556â†’equation of this class reproduces $\sin^2\!\theta_W$ to
  1557â†’sub-percent accuracy at $(s_1, s_2) = (1/2, 1)$.
  1558â†’The ratio derived below is therefore a
  1559â†’\emph{consequence} of these constraints, not a free parameter.
  1560â†’
  1561â†’\subsection{The electroweak ratio}
  1562â†’Define the ratio
  1563â†’\begin{equation}
  1564â†’R \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}\,.
  1565â†’\end{equation}
  1566â†’Evaluating \eqref{eq:casimir_roots} at $s = 1/2$ ($C_2 = 3/4$)
  1567â†’and $s = 1$ ($C_2 = 2$):
  1568â†’\begin{align}
  1569â†’x_+(1/2) &= \tfrac{1}{8}\bigl(-3 + \sqrt{57}\bigr)
  1570â†’  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1/2)\,m^2 = M_W^2\,,
  1571â†’\\[4pt]
  1572â†’x_+(1) &= -1 + \sqrt{3}
  1573â†’  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2\,.
  1574â†’\end{align}
  1575â†’The identification with $W$ and $Z$ is motivated by their
  1576â†’$SU(2)$ quantum numbers ($s = 1/2$ maps to the doublet structure of $W$,
  1577â†’$s = 1$ to the triplet).  Then
  1578â†’\begin{equation}
  1579â†’R = 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}
  1580â†’  = \frac{(\sqrt{19} - 3)(\sqrt{19} - \sqrt{3}\,)}{16}
  1581â†’  \;\approx\; 0.2231013\ldots
  1582â†’\end{equation}
  1583â†’The factored form follows from the discriminants of
  1584â†’\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}: $57 = 3 \times 19$ at $s = 1/2$ and
  1585â†’$12 = 4 \times 3$ at $s = 1$.  Rationalizing the denominator
  1586â†’$\sqrt{3} - 1$ yields an expression in $\sqrt{19}$ and $\sqrt{3}$
  1587â†’alone, which coincides term-by-term with the form found
  1588â†’empirically by de~Vries.
  1589â†’
  1590â†’This should be compared with the on-shell electroweak mixing
  1591â†’parameter:
  1592â†’\begin{equation}
  1593â†’\label{eq:sin2_onshell}
  1594â†’\sin^2\!\theta_W^{\text{os}}
  1595â†’  \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{M_W^2}{M_Z^2}
  1596â†’  = 0.22320 \pm 0.00026\,,
  1597â†’\end{equation}
  1598â†’using $M_Z = 91.1876 \pm 0.0021\GeV$ and
  1599â†’$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ (PDG~2024).
  1600â†’
  1601â†’Since $R = 1 - x_+(1/2)/x_+(1)$ and
  1602â†’$\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2$, the two expressions are
  1603â†’\emph{algebraically identical} once one identifies
  1604â†’$x_+(s) = M^2(s)/m^2$.  The prediction $R \approx
  1605â†’\sin^2\!\theta_W$ and the $W$-mass prediction derived below are
  1606â†’therefore \textbf{one and the same observation}, not two
  1607â†’independent tests.
  1608â†’
  1609â†’\paragraph{$W$-mass prediction.}
  1610â†’From $M_W = M_Z\sqrt{1 - R}$:
  1611â†’\begin{equation}
  1612â†’M_W^{\text{pred}}
  1613â†’  = 91.1876 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.22310}
  1614â†’  = 80.374 \pm 0.002\GeV\,,
  1615â†’\end{equation}
  1616â†’which lies $0.39\sigma$ from the PDG average
  1617â†’$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ and strongly disfavors the
  1618â†’anomalous CDF-II measurement $M_W^{\text{CDF}} = 80.4335 \pm
  1619â†’0.0094\GeV$ (6.2$\sigma$ tension with $R$).
  1620â†’
  1621â†’\paragraph{Scale parameter.}
  1622â†’Setting $x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2$ gives
  1623â†’\begin{equation}
  1624â†’m = \frac{M_Z}{\sqrt{\sqrt{3} - 1}}
  1625â†’  = 106.577\GeV
  1626â†’  \approx m_\mu \times 10^3\,,
  1627â†’\end{equation}
  1628â†’a coincidence whose significance is unclear.
  1629â†’
  1630â†’\subsection{Open issues for the Casimir equation}
  1631â†’\label{subsec:casimir_open}
  1632â†’
  1633â†’\paragraph{Renormalization scheme dependence.}
  1634â†’The comparison \eqref{eq:sin2_onshell} uses the on-shell
  1635â†’definition $\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2 = 0.22320$,
  1636â†’\textbf{not} the $\overline{\text{MS}}$ value
  1637â†’$\sin^2\!\theta_W(\overline{\text{MS}}) = 0.23122 \pm 0.00004$.
  1638â†’The two differ by $\Delta \approx 0.008$, far larger than
  1639â†’experimental errors.  The algebraic argument
  1640â†’\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} contains no dynamical mechanism to
  1641â†’prefer one scheme over the other.  Until such a mechanism is
  1642â†’identified, the on-shell agreement should be regarded as
  1643â†’suggestive but scheme-dependent.
  1644â†’
  1645â†’\paragraph{The fourth eigenvalue.}
  1646â†’At $s = 1/2$, the second root of \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} gives
  1647â†’\begin{equation}
  1648â†’|M(1/2, -)|
  1649â†’  = m\,\sqrt{|x_-(1/2)|}
  1650â†’  = 122.4\GeV\,,
  1651â†’\end{equation}
  1652â†’which is 2.3\% below $M_H = 125.25 \pm 0.17\GeV$.  However,
  1653â†’this eigenstate carries spin $1/2$, while the Higgs boson is
  1654â†’spin $0$.  The near-degeneracy is noted as a numerical curiosity;
  1655â†’we do not claim a connection.
  1656â†’
  1657â†’\paragraph{Origin.}
  1658â†’This observation was first made by H.~de~Vries in a Physics
  1659â†’Forums discussion (November 2004) and subsequently studied in
  1660â†’\cite{Rivero2005, Rivero2005b}.
  1661â†’
  1662â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1663â†’\subsection{Independence of the two structures}
  1664â†’\label{subsec:two_gap}
  1665â†’%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1666â†’
  1667â†’Parts~A and~B describe independent algebraic structures that share
  1668â†’the same physical particles:
  1669â†’\begin{itemize}
  1670â†’\item The $SO(32)$ embedding (\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}) governs
  1671â†’  the \emph{representation content}---which $SU(5)$ multiplets
  1672â†’  appear.
  1673â†’\item The Casimir equation (\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}) governs
  1674â†’  the \emph{mass/coupling pattern}---what numerical ratios emerge
  1675â†’  among the gauge-boson masses.
  1676â†’\item The two structures live over different number fields:
  1677â†’  the adjoint decomposition involves only rational
  1678â†’  dimensions, while the eigenvalue ratio $R$ lies in
  1679â†’  $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3},\sqrt{19}\,)$---an extension not
  1680â†’  generated by any invariant of $SO(32)$.
  1681â†’\end{itemize}
  1682â†’No algebraic homomorphism linking the embedding chain
  1683â†’\eqref{eq:so32_chain} to the eigenvalue equation
  1684â†’\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} has been found.  The gap between
  1685â†’\emph{content} (which states exist) and \emph{dynamics}
  1686â†’(what masses they acquire) remains the central open problem of
  1687â†’the sBootstrap program.
  1688â†’
  1689â†’%======================================================================
  1690â†’\section{SUSY Breaking: From Seeds to Spectrum}
  1691â†’\label{sec:breaking}
  1692â†’%======================================================================
  1693â†’
  1694â†’The SUSY-breaking story begins with the foundational observation 
  1695â†’\eqref{eq:pimu}: $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2$. This is the 
  1696â†’Volkov--Akulov mass formula for a pseudo-Goldstino---the muon---whose 
  1697â†’superpartner---the pion---is a pseudo-Goldstone boson. The SUSY-breaking 
  1698â†’scale is $f_\pi \approx 92\MeV$, and the breaking mechanism is chiral: 
  1699â†’$\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1700â†’simultaneously. The Volkov--Akulov realization is the \emph{physical 
  1701â†’starting point}; what follows in this section is the SQCD machinery 
  1702â†’that makes it precise.
  1703â†’
  1704â†’The dynamics must accomplish three things: generate the seed-to-bloom 
  1705â†’transition for each tuple, preserve the energy-balance condition 
  1706â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ through the breaking, and produce 
  1707â†’the correct splitting between meson and lepton masses at scale $f_\pi$.
  1708â†’
  1709â†’\subsection{The Seiberg framework}
  1710â†’
  1711â†’The natural arena is $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with gauge group $SU(3)_c$ 
  1712â†’and $N_f$ flavors of chiral superfields $Q^i$, $\bar{Q}_j$ in the 
  1713â†’fundamental and antifundamental representations. For $N_f = N_c = 3$ 
  1714â†’(the case relevant to light QCD), Seiberg's exact results 
  1715â†’\cite{Seiberg1995} establish that the theory confines, with the 
  1716â†’low-energy degrees of freedom being the composite meson superfield
  1717â†’\begin{equation}
  1718â†’M^i{}_j = Q^i \cdot \bar{Q}_j\,,
  1719â†’\end{equation}
  1720â†’a $3\times 3$ matrix of chiral superfields, together with baryons 
  1721â†’$B = \epsilon_{ijk}Q^iQ^jQ^k$ and $\bar{B}$. These satisfy the 
  1722â†’quantum-modified constraint
  1723â†’\begin{equation}
  1724â†’\label{eq:seiberg}
  1725â†’\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^{6}\,,
  1726â†’\end{equation}
  1727â†’where $\Lambda$ is the dynamical scale ($2N_c = 6$ for $N_c = 3$).
  1728â†’
  1729â†’Each entry $M^i{}_j$ is a chiral superfield containing a complex 
  1730â†’scalar (the meson) and a Weyl fermion (the lepton, in the sBootstrap 
  1731â†’identification). The scalar and fermion are degenerate in the SUSY 
  1732â†’limit; SUSY breaking splits them.
  1733â†’
  1734â†’\subsection{The three chiral superfields of each seed}
  1735â†’
  1736â†’The seed triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ maps naturally onto three 
  1737â†’chiral superfields with the following mass structure before breaking:
  1738â†’
  1739â†’\begin{center}
  1740â†’\begin{tabular}{lcl}
  1741â†’\toprule
  1742â†’Superfield & Mass & Role \\
  1743â†’\midrule
  1744â†’$\Phi_0$ & $m_0 = 0$ & Massless: flat direction \\
  1745â†’$\Phi_a$ & $m_a = \zch_a^2$ & Light massive member \\
  1746â†’$\Phi_b$ & $m_b = \zch_b^2$ & Heavy massive member \\

content.text: Let me continue reading the remaining sections needed for Paper B, focusing on the sections about the electron mass, symmetry restoration, open problems related to Paper B, the epistemological table, conclusions, and bibliography.

content.content:
3139â†’\section{The Electron Mass and the Lepton Seed}
  3140â†’\label{sec:electron}
  3141â†’%======================================================================
  3142â†’
  3143â†’The charged lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the ``almost-seed'': 
  3144â†’$\zch_e = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}$ is small but nonzero. The electron's 
  3145â†’charge is near zero because of cancellation in $\zch_e = z_0 + z_e$:
  3146â†’\begin{equation}
  3147â†’v_0 = 17.716, \quad z_e = -17.001, \quad 
  3148â†’\zch_e = 17.716 - 17.001 = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}\,.
  3149â†’\end{equation}
  3150â†’A 96\% cancellation. The mass $m_e = 0.715^2 = 0.511\MeV$ is the 
  3151â†’square of something already small.
  3152â†’
  3153â†’The near-vanishing of $m_e$ is a structural property of the Koide 
  3154â†’embedding, not fine-tuning: the energy balance allows perturbations 
  3155â†’as large as $z_0$. Every Koide triple has one member with 
  3156â†’$|\zch|/v_0$ small:
  3157â†’
  3158â†’\begin{center}
  3159â†’\begin{tabular}{lccl}
  3160â†’\toprule
  3161â†’Triple & Lightest & $|\zch|/v_0$ & Character \\
  3162â†’\midrule
  3163â†’$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & $e$ & 0.040 & Almost-seed \\
  3164â†’$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & $\pi$ (neg.) & 0.337 & Full triple \\
  3165â†’$(c, b, t)$ & $c$ & 0.236 & Full triple \\
  3166â†’$(-s, c, b)$ & $s$ (neg.) & 0.320 & Full triple \\
  3167â†’\bottomrule
  3168â†’\end{tabular}
  3169â†’\end{center}
  3170â†’
  3171â†’The electron is by far the most extreme case, consistent with the 
  3172â†’lepton triple being nearest to its seed.
  3173â†’
  3174â†’%======================================================================
  3175â†’\section{Symmetry Restoration}
  3176â†’\label{sec:restoration}
  3177â†’%======================================================================
  3178â†’
  3179â†’The broken spectrum reassembles as symmetry-breaking effects are 
  3180â†’removed:
  3181â†’
  3182â†’\begin{enumerate}
  3183â†’\item \textbf{Turn off QED:} Electromagnetic splittings vanish 
  3184â†’($\pi^\pm = \pi^0$, $K^\pm = K^0$). D-term effects disappear.
  3185â†’\item \textbf{Set $m_u = m_d$:} Exact isospin.
  3186â†’\item \textbf{Set $m_s = m_d$:} $SU(3)_V$ restored, 
  3187â†’$\pi = K = \eta$. Koide splitting $v_1 \to 0$; lepton partners 
  3188â†’degenerate.
  3189â†’\item \textbf{Send $m_q \to 0$:} Chiral limit, all meson charges 
  3190â†’$\to 0$. If sBootstrap SUSY restores, all light lepton masses 
  3191â†’vanish too. This is the seed configuration: $(0, 0, 0)$.
  3192â†’\end{enumerate}
  3193â†’
  3194â†’The seed triples are the ``penultimate'' stage: one member has 
  3195â†’reached zero, but the other two retain the $2 - \sqrt{3}$ ratio. 
  3196â†’Full triples are the final broken state. The lepton triple, 
  3197â†’trapped between seed and bloom, reveals the hierarchy: 
  3198â†’chiral breaking $\to$ seed $\to$ bloom $\to$ full spectrum.
  3199â†’
  3200â†’The direction of the flow also matters: approaching the chiral 
  3201â†’limit, the pion charge rises from $\zch = -11.81$ toward zero 
  3202â†’from below. It crosses zero exactly in the chiral limit. This 
  3203â†’means the pion was ``born negative''---its charge sign is set by 
  3204â†’the direction of chiral symmetry breaking, and the seed 
  3205â†’$(0, \pi, D_s)$ is the configuration at the moment of crossing.
  3206â†’
  3207â†’%======================================================================
  3208â†’%======================================================================
  3209â†’\section{Open Problems}
  3210â†’\label{sec:open}
  3211â†’%======================================================================
  3212â†’
  3213â†’\subsection{The diquark sector}
  3214â†’The Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) proves that no $J=0$,
  3215â†’color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor.  The argument
  3216â†’extends to all partial waves: $J = 0$ forces $L = S$, so the
  3217â†’spatial--spin exchange sign is $(-1)^{L+S} = (-1)^{2S} = +1$,
  3218â†’which combined with the antisymmetric color $\bar{3}$ gives
  3219â†’overall $+1$; Pauli then requires antisymmetric flavor
  3220â†’($\overline{\mathbf{10}}$).  The $\mathbf{15}$ is accessible
  3221â†’only for $J \geq 1$.  In SQCD, the same conclusion holds
  3222â†’algebraically: the $\varepsilon$-tensor on color indices in
  3223â†’$B^{abc} = \varepsilon_{ijk}Q^{ia}Q^{jb}Q^{kc}$ forces
  3224â†’antisymmetric flavor regardless of statistics.
  3225â†’The sBootstrap's $\mathbf{15}$ diquark must therefore be non-composite:
  3226â†’either an algebraic image of the Miyazawa supercharge
  3227â†’(\S\ref{subsec:baryon_open}) or a fundamental $SO(32)$ string state.
  3228â†’However, the mixed anticommutator
  3229â†’$\{Q_\alpha, \mathcal{Q}_{[ij],\beta}\} = \epsilon_{\alpha\beta}\,
  3230â†’\mathcal{S}_{[ij]}$ produces a diquark generator in
  3231â†’$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ (antisymmetric), not the symmetric
  3232â†’$\mathbf{15}$; thus the Miyazawa route alone does not resolve the
  3233â†’$\mathbf{15}$~vs.~$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ conflict.
  3234â†’The exotic $+4/3$-charged
  3235â†’diquarks of the $\mathbf{15}$ would be a testable consequence:
  3236â†’if their mass is set by the SUSY-breaking scale ($\sim f_\pi$), they
  3237â†’would be sub-GeV and accessible at fixed-target experiments; if by
  3238â†’$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ or higher, LHC pair-production searches for
  3239â†’exotic charges apply. No mass prediction can be made without a
  3240â†’concrete realization of the $\mathbf{15}$.
  3241â†’
  3242â†’\subsection{What determines the Koide phase?}
  3243â†’The energy balance constrains but does not uniquely fix $\delta$.
  3244â†’The O'Raifeartaigh realization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) fixes the
  3245â†’seed ratio $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ but leaves $\delta$ undetermined---the
  3246â†’phase controls how the seed blooms into a full triple. The bloom
  3247â†’is a phase rotation $\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at
  3248â†’fixed $v_0$, but explicit R-breaking perturbations push $Q$ away
  3249â†’from $2/3$ at any nonzero strength, making the seed an unstable
  3250â†’fixed point. The bloom must therefore be nonperturbative.
  3251â†’
  3252â†’The lepton phase satisfies $\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ to
  3253â†’$33\,\text{ppm}$ ($0.9\sigma$ in $m_\tau$; $3.1\sigma$ after
  3254â†’look-elsewhere over fractions with denominator $\leq 20$). Since $2/9$
  3255â†’is not a rational multiple of $\pi$, this is not a geometric angle.
  3256â†’It is, however, $2/9 = 2/(3\times 3)$, which points to a $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3257â†’discrete symmetry in the Koide modulus space. The $\mathbb{Z}_3$
  3258â†’mass-permutation symmetry ($\delta \to \delta + 2\pi/3$) is the
  3259â†’unique subgroup; the quotient $\mathbb{Z}_9/\mathbb{Z}_3$ has three
  3260â†’cosets, producing three inequivalent mass spectra from the same $M_0$.
  3261â†’The simplest potential with $\mathbb{Z}_9$ symmetry,
  3262â†’$V \supset -\cos\bigl(9(\delta - 2/9)\bigr)$, has the lepton value
  3263â†’as one of its nine minima, with the $\mathbb{Z}_3$-equivalent minima
  3264â†’at $\delta = 2/9 + 2k\pi/3$.
  3265â†’
  3266â†’A dynamical origin for $\mathbb{Z}_9$ exists in SQCD: for $N_f = N_c = 3$,
  3267â†’the diagonal $U(1)_{R+2A}$ (R-symmetry plus twice the axial symmetry)
  3268â†’has anomaly coefficient $6N_c = 18 = 2 \times 9$. Instantons break
  3269â†’$U(1)_{R+2A} \to \mathbb{Z}_{18}$, which contains $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3270â†’as a subgroup. The meson field $M$ carries $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge 4
  3271â†’(from two quark zero modes at charge 2 each), so $\det M$ carries
  3272â†’charge~12.  The lowest-order holomorphic $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$-singlet
  3273â†’beyond the Seiberg constraint is the three-instanton operator
  3274â†’\begin{equation}
  3275â†’  \delta W_{3\text{-inst}}
  3276â†’    = c_3\,\frac{(\det M)^3}{\Lambda^{18}}\,,
  3277â†’\end{equation}
  3278â†’since $(\det M)^3$ has $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge $36 = 2\times 18$.
  3279â†’Near the SUSY vacuum $\det M = \Lambda^6$, parametrizing
  3280â†’$\det M = \Lambda^6\,e^{3i\delta_M}$ yields
  3281â†’$\delta W \propto e^{9i\delta}$, and the cross-term with the
  3282â†’tree-level superpotential gives
  3283â†’$V_{\text{eff}} \supset -V_0\cos\bigl(9(\delta - \delta_0)\bigr)$
  3284â†’with $\delta_0 = \arg(c_3)/9$.
  3285â†’This is exactly the $\cos(9\delta)$ potential identified above.
  3286â†’The one-instanton vertex ($\det M$, charge 12) is already captured
  3287â†’by the Seiberg constraint; the two-instanton vertex ($(\det M)^2$,
  3288â†’charge 24, not a $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ singlet) is forbidden.
  3289â†’The three-instanton correction does not affect the meson mass
  3290â†’spectrum: the $F$-term equations define an effective Lagrange
  3291â†’multiplier $X_{\text{eff}} = X + 3c_3\Lambda^{12}/\Lambda^{18}$,
  3292â†’but the constraint $\det M = \Lambda^6$ and the meson VEVs
  3293â†’$M_j = C/m_j$ are unmodified. The correction acts only on the
  3294â†’phase structure (through $\cos(9\delta)$), not on the magnitudes.
  3295â†’
  3296â†’At strong coupling ($\Lambda \sim 300\MeV$), the coefficient
  3297â†’$c_3$ is $O(1)$ and the potential strength is
  3298â†’$V_0^{1/4} \sim 65\text{--}85\MeV$, comparable to
  3299â†’$f_\pi = 92\MeV$---this is not a parametrically suppressed effect.
  3300â†’The same $\cos(9\delta)$ structure also arises from magnetic
  3301â†’bion contributions on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, where the
  3302â†’$\mathbb{Z}_3$-symmetric combination of the SU(3) affine roots
  3303â†’maps to $\cos(9\delta)$ in the meson phase variable.
  3304â†’
  3305â†’The embedding is specific to $N_c = 3$: the condition
  3306â†’$N_c^2 \mid 6N_c$ requires $N_c \mid 6$, which holds only for
  3307â†’$N_c = 1, 2, 3, 6$.
  3308â†’
  3309â†’The quark Koide phases, by contrast, do \emph{not} sit at
  3310â†’$\mathbb{Z}_9$ minima: the $(-s,c,b)$ and $(c,b,t)$ phases
  3311â†’are displaced by $39\%$ and $22\%$ of the $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3312â†’spacing, respectively.  This suggests that the lepton and quark
  3313â†’phases are selected by different dynamical mechanisms---the
  3314â†’instanton potential for leptons, and possibly the bloom mechanism
  3315â†’or ISS dynamics for quarks.
  3316â†’
  3317â†’The Coleman--Weinberg potential of the minimal O'Raifeartaigh model
  3318â†’is flat in $\delta$ at one loop---the pseudo-modulus potential depends
  3319â†’only on the modulus $|\Phi_0|$, not on the Koide phase.  The
  3320â†’three-instanton potential above provides the leading
  3321â†’$\delta$-dependent term that lifts this flat direction.
  3322â†’The \emph{modulus} direction $|\Phi_0|$ is a separate question:
  3323â†’the CW potential stabilizes $|\langle\Phi_0\rangle|$ at zero,
  3324â†’but the Koide seed requires $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$
  3325â†’(\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}).
  3326â†’A negative quartic K\"ahler correction $c\,|X|^4/\Lambda_K^2$
  3327â†’with $c = -\Lambda_K^2/(12\mu^2)$ pins the pseudo-modulus at
  3328â†’the K\"ahler pole $v_{\text{pole}} = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ exactly
  3329â†’(an algebraic result, not numerical tuning;
  3330â†’see \S\ref{subsec:superpotential}).
  3331â†’This coefficient is \emph{not} radiatively generated: the
  3332â†’one-loop CW quartic in the O'Raifeartaigh model has $c_4 = -20/3$
  3333â†’at $gf/m^2 = 1$ (exact), which is orders of magnitude too large;
  3334â†’the ISS one-loop potential produces a positive quartic ($c_{\text{eff}} = +0.014$),
  3335â†’the wrong sign entirely. The K\"ahler pole mechanism is therefore a
  3336â†’tree-level statement about the effective K\"ahler potential,
  3337â†’not a consequence of one-loop dynamics.  However, magnetic bions
  3338â†’on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ generate a quartic K\"ahler correction

3338â†’on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ generate a quartic K\"ahler correction
  3339â†’of the correct sign.
  3340â†’The bion-induced coefficient
  3341â†’$c_{\text{bion}} = -3\pi^2\,e^{-4\pi/(N_c^2\alpha_s)}\,M^2/\alpha_s^2$
  3342â†’(where $M^2 = [\prod_f(m_f/\Lambda)]^2$ is the zero-mode mass factor)
  3343â†’equals $-1/12$ at $\alpha_s = 0.143$ ($S_{\text{bion}} = 9.76$)
  3344â†’in the ISS model with heavy magnetic quarks ($M^2 = 1$).
  3345â†’With physical light-quark masses, zero-mode suppression reduces
  3346â†’$|c_{\text{bion}}|$ by five orders of magnitude; the ISS magnetic
  3347â†’dual---where the pseudo-modulus lives---is the appropriate
  3348â†’setting for the bion calculation.
  3349â†’The three-instanton operator $(\det M)^3$ shifts the Seiberg
  3350â†’Lagrange multiplier~$X$ (dimension $[\text{mass}]^{-3}$ in the
  3351â†’confining phase) but this is a different object from the ISS
  3352â†’pseudo-modulus (dimension $[\text{mass}]^{+1}$ in the magnetic
  3353â†’dual).
  3354â†’
  3355â†’More broadly, the ISS Coleman--Weinberg potential does \emph{not}
  3356â†’transmit the Koide condition from the UV quark masses to the IR
  3357â†’meson spectrum.  In the $N_f = 4$ ISS model, the one-loop CW
  3358â†’masses for the broken mesons $\chi_a$ are nearly degenerate
  3359â†’($m_{\text{CW}} \approx 27\MeV$ for all four flavors), because
  3360â†’the CW mass depends on $m_a$ through the affine map
  3361â†’$F_a = h\mu^2 - m_a$, and all quark masses satisfy
  3362â†’$m_a \ll h\mu^2$.  The resulting spectrum gives
  3363â†’$Q \approx 1/3$ (the degenerate limit), regardless of the UV
  3364â†’value.  This confirms that flavor-sensitive mass structure must
  3365â†’arise from non-perturbative effects---the bion K\"ahler
  3366â†’correction (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}), which directly involves
  3367â†’$\sqrt{m_k}$, rather than the one-loop effective potential.
  3368â†’
  3369â†’\subsection{Connecting the three tuples}
  3370â†’The unified superpotential (\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}) connects
  3371â†’the light and heavy sectors through a layered structure:
  3372â†’the seed Koide fixes $m_c/m_s$ (from $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$),
  3373â†’the $v_0$-doubling fixes $m_b$ (from the K\"ahler sector),
  3374â†’and the $(c,b,t)$ Koide serves as an overdetermination test.
  3375â†’After these constraints, only two to three free parameters remain:
  3376â†’$m_u$, $m_d$ (connected to the Cabibbo angle), and the overall
  3377â†’scale $m_s$.  The Koide condition does \emph{not} extend to triples
  3378â†’containing $m_u$ or $m_d$: $Q(m_u,m_d,m_s) = 0.567$ ($-15\%$)
  3379â†’and $Q(m_u,m_c,m_t) = 0.849$ ($+27\%$), confirming that the
  3380â†’lightest quarks are free parameters of the framework. The $v_0$-doubling is non-holomorphic and lives in the
  3381â†’K\"ahler sector: as shown in \S\ref{subsec:fterm}, the bion
  3382â†’K\"ahler correction $K_{\text{bion}} \sim |\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$
  3383â†’generates an effective potential whose minimum enforces
  3384â†’$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$.
  3385â†’The Hessian has rank~1, constraining exactly the $v_0$-doubling
  3386â†’combination while leaving $m_s$ and $m_c$ to the Koide seed.
  3387â†’The extension from $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ to
  3388â†’$\mathbb{R}^4$ relies on \"Unsal's continuity conjecture.
  3389â†’
  3390â†’\subsection{Lepton masses are not mesino masses}
  3391â†’The fermion mass matrix $W_{IJ}$ at the Seiberg vacuum has
  3392â†’light eigenvalues ($\sim 10^{-3}$--$10^{-2}\MeV$) from the
  3393â†’seesaw between the meson and Lagrange-multiplier sectors.
  3394â†’These are far too small and too degenerate
  3395â†’($Q_{\text{light}} = 0.48$, not $2/3$) to reproduce lepton masses.
  3396â†’The bion-induced K\"ahler correction
  3397â†’$\delta K_{ij} \sim \zeta^2 \sqrt{m_i m_j}/\Lambda^2 \sim 10^{-7}$
  3398â†’is negligible at the Seiberg vacuum, and a systematic scan over
  3399â†’flavour-dependent K\"ahler parameters finds $Q \in [0.35,\,0.50]$,
  3400â†’never reaching~$2/3$.
  3401â†’The obstruction is structural: K\"ahler rescaling
  3402â†’$K^{-1/2}\,W_{IJ}\,K^{-1/2}$ is a congruence transformation
  3403â†’that preserves the rank of~$W_{IJ}$ and cannot generate new
  3404â†’mass eigenvalues.
  3405â†’A further dimensionless obstruction: the holomorphic mesino
  3406â†’mass ratio $m_{\psi_{us}}/m_{\psi_{ud}} = M_d/M_s = m_s/m_d = 20.0$
  3407â†’is preserved by any universal K\"ahler rescaling, but the target
  3408â†’ratio $m_\mu/m_e = 206.8$ differs by a factor~$10.3$.  Matching
  3409â†’both masses simultaneously would require flavour-dependent
  3410â†’anomalous dimensions ($\gamma_{ud} \approx 0.25$ vs.\
  3411â†’$\gamma_{us} \approx 0.46$), which are plausible for a
  3412â†’strongly coupled theory but not calculable with existing methods.
  3413â†’Moreover, the K\"ahler expansion breaks down at the seesaw
  3414â†’vacuum: $M_u/\Lambda \approx 1362$, $M_d/\Lambda \approx 630$,
  3415â†’and $M_s/\Lambda \approx 31$, so the canonical K\"ahler
  3416â†’$K = \operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M)$ is a poor approximation
  3417â†’and large non-perturbative corrections are expected.
  3418â†’Lepton masses in this framework therefore require a mechanism
  3419â†’beyond the confined-phase K\"ahler potential.
  3420â†’A natural candidate is a \emph{separate} confining sector.
  3421â†’$SU(2)$ SQCD with $N_f = 3$ (the s-confining case $N_f = N_c + 1$)
  3422â†’produces three antisymmetric mesons $L^{ij} = \epsilon^{ab}\Psi^i_a\Psi^j_b$
  3423â†’with dynamical superpotential
  3424â†’$W_{\text{dyn}} = L^{12}L^{13}L^{23}/\Lambda_L^5$ (the Pfaffian).
  3425â†’An O'Raifeartaigh deformation
  3426â†’\[
  3427â†’  W = f X_L + m\,L^{12}L^{23} + g\,X_L(L^{12})^2
  3428â†’    + \frac{\epsilon}{\Lambda_L^5}\,L^{12}L^{13}L^{23}
  3429â†’\]
  3430â†’breaks SUSY at a metastable vacuum ($F_{X_L} = f$) while the
  3431â†’Pfaffian provides a distant SUSY-restoring vacuum.
  3432â†’At $t \equiv g\langle X_L\rangle/m = \sqrt{3}$
  3433â†’(selected by a K\"ahler pole, as in the quark sector),
  3434â†’the fermion mass matrix has the spectrum
  3435â†’$(0,\,(2-\sqrt{3})m,\,(2+\sqrt{3})m)$
  3436â†’with $Q = 2/3$ \emph{exactly}.
  3437â†’This is the same O'Raifeartaigh--Koide seed that generates the quark
  3438â†’Koide triples; the mechanism is universal, independent of gauge group.
  3439â†’The bloom rotation handles the mass hierarchy, and a
  3440â†’confinement scale $\Lambda_L \sim 300\MeV$ gives the
  3441â†’observed $M_0 = 313.8\MeV$.
  3442â†’Below $\Lambda_L$, QED running is nearly flavour-universal
  3443â†’and preserves $Q = 2/3$ to $O(\alpha^2/\pi^2) \sim 10^{-5}$,
  3444â†’consistent with the observed $9$~ppm deviation.
  3445â†’A complete specification of the lepton sector---in particular
  3446â†’the mediation mechanism coupling $L^{ij}$ to the Higgs doublets
  3447â†’and the derivation of the overall lepton mass scale---is deferred
  3448â†’to future work.
  3449â†’
  3450â†’\subsection{The baryon sector}
  3451â†’\label{subsec:baryon_open}
  3452â†’In Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, baryons are part of the
  3453â†’low-energy spectrum. Their SUSY partners are ``baryonic fermions''
  3454â†’with no known candidates among observed particles.
  3455â†’
  3456â†’The meson--baryon connection requires a Miyazawa supercharge
  3457â†’\cite{Miyazawa, CattoGursey}, which cannot lie in the
  3458â†’fundamental~$\mathbf{5}$: the product
  3459â†’$\mathbf{5}\otimes(\mathbf{1}\oplus\mathbf{24})$ does not
  3460â†’contain~$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$, so a single-index generator
  3461â†’cannot map mesons to baryons.  The minimal choice is
  3462â†’\begin{equation*}
  3463â†’\mathcal{Q}_{[pq],\alpha}
  3464â†’  \;\in\; \overline{\mathbf{10}}\;\text{of}\;SU(5)_f\,,
  3465â†’  \qquad \Delta B = +1\,,
  3466â†’\end{equation*}
  3467â†’with transformation law
  3468â†’$[\mathcal{Q}_{[pq]}, M^i{}_j]
  3469â†’  = \delta^i_{[p}\,\tilde\psi_{B,\,q]j}
  3470â†’  - \tfrac{1}{5}\delta^i_j\,\tilde\psi_{B,\,pq}$,
  3471â†’where $\tilde\psi_{B,\,lm}
  3472â†’  = \tfrac{1}{6}\epsilon_{lmijk}\,\psi_B^{ijk}$ is the dual
  3473â†’baryonino.  The anticommutator decomposes as
  3474â†’\begin{equation*}
  3475â†’\overline{\mathbf{10}}\otimes\mathbf{10}
  3476â†’  = \mathbf{1}\oplus\mathbf{24}\oplus\mathbf{75}\,,
  3477â†’\end{equation*}
  3478â†’giving $\{\mathcal{Q},\bar{\mathcal{Q}}\}
  3479â†’  \supset \sigma^\mu P_\mu + T^A + Z$, where $T^A$ are the
  3480â†’$SU(5)_f$ generators and $Z$ are central charges encoding the
  3481â†’meson--baryon mass splitting $m_B^2 - m_M^2 = \langle Z\rangle
  3482â†’\sim\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2$.  This Miyazawa SUSY evades the
  3483â†’Haag--\L{}opusza\'nski--Sohnius theorem because it is an
  3484â†’approximate symmetry broken at scale~$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$,
  3485â†’analogous to $SU(6)$ spin--flavor symmetry in the quark model.
  3486â†’The $\mathbf{75}$ channel on the right-hand side
  3487â†’signals that the full bosonic subalgebra extends beyond
  3488â†’$SU(5)_f$; its interpretation remains open.
  3489â†’The $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ representation of the Miyazawa
  3490â†’supercharge is the same $SU(5)_f$ multiplet that appears in the
  3491â†’$SO(32)$ adjoint decomposition (Table~\ref{tab:496}), suggesting
  3492â†’that the baryon--meson connection and the string embedding may
  3493â†’share a common algebraic origin.
  3494â†’
  3495â†’\subsection{The strong CP problem}
  3496â†’The principle ``Nature abhors chirality'' predicts $\bar{\theta} = 0$ 
  3497â†’but provides no mechanism.
  3498â†’
  3499â†’\subsection{The kaon--muon puzzle}
  3500â†’The Lagrangian predicts a universal soft splitting
  3501â†’$m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = \fpi^2$ across the
  3502â†’meson matrix. For the pion--muon pair, this works:
  3503â†’$m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx \fpi^2$ to $2.2\%$. But the
  3504â†’tree-level formula $m^2_M = |m_i + m_j|^2 + \fpi^2$ badly
  3505â†’underpredicts heavier meson masses: it gives $m_K \approx 133\MeV$
  3506â†’(physical: $498\MeV$) and $m_{D_s} \approx 1{,}367\MeV$
  3507â†’(physical: $1{,}968\MeV$). QCD confinement generates the bulk
  3508â†’of these masses independently of the soft term. The universal
  3509â†’splitting applies to the \emph{soft} contribution, not to the
  3510â†’total mass; it is visible only for $(\pi, \mu)$,
  3511â†’where the soft term dominates over $|m_u + m_d|^2$.
  3512â†’
  3513â†’\subsection{Flavour-changing neutral currents}
  3514â†’The off-diagonal meson scalars $M^d{}_s$, $M^s{}_d$ are the
  3515â†’\emph{same particles} as the SM kaon and antikaon---they are not
  3516â†’new degrees of freedom.  Their tree-level contribution to
  3517â†’$K$--$\bar K$ mixing is the standard QCD effect already
  3518â†’included in SM predictions.  The question is whether the
  3519â†’additional SUSY couplings (the $\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$
  3520â†’vertex, the Yukawa $y_c\,H_u\,M^d{}_d$ and
  3521â†’$y_b\,H_d\,M^s{}_s$) generate \emph{extra} flavour-violating
  3522â†’contributions beyond QCD.  Because the mesinos are identified
  3523â†’with the leptons in the sBootstrap framework, no new particles
  3524â†’are introduced; the principal source of additional FCNC is the
  3525â†’Yukawa-meson cross-coupling through Higgs exchange.  A
  3526â†’quantitative estimate of these effects requires matching the
  3527â†’effective theory to the measured $K$--$\bar K$ Wilson
  3528â†’coefficients~\cite{UTfit} and will be addressed in future work.
  3529â†’
  3530â†’\subsection{The Casimir eigenvalue equation}
  3531â†’The eigenvalue equation $x^2 + C_2 x - C_2 = 0$ produces the
  3532â†’on-shell weak mixing angle to $0.4\sigma$, but three issues
  3533â†’remain open: (i)~the equation is an ansatz, not derived from a
  3534â†’Lagrangian; (ii)~the match uses the on-shell renormalization
  3535â†’scheme, and no argument selects this scheme over $\overline{\text{MS}}$;
  3536â†’(iii)~the negative spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ root gives $|M| = 122.4\GeV$,
  3537â†’tantalizingly close to $M_H = 125.25\GeV$ but with a spin mismatch.

3537â†’tantalizingly close to $M_H = 125.25\GeV$ but with a spin mismatch.
  3538â†’
  3539â†’\subsection{The Higgs mass at $\tan\beta = 1$}
  3540â†’At $\tan\beta = 1$, the $D$-term quartic
  3541â†’$(g^2+g'^2)(|H_u|^2 - |H_d|^2)^2/8$ vanishes on the
  3542â†’$D$-flat direction $H_u = H_d$.  The tree-level lightest
  3543â†’CP-even Higgs mass is $m_h = m_Z|\cos 2\beta| = 0$---a known
  3544â†’MSSM result. The meson Yukawa F-terms contribute only mass
  3545â†’terms ($y_j^2|H|^2$), not quartics.
  3546â†’In the NMSSM, a singlet coupling
  3547â†’$\lambda\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$ generates an extra quartic
  3548â†’$\lambda^2|H_u\!\cdot\!H_d|^2$; at $\sin 2\beta = 1$ (i.e.,
  3549â†’$\tan\beta = 1$), $m_h^2 = \lambda^2 v^2/2$, and
  3550â†’$\lambda = 0.72$ gives $m_h = 125\GeV$ at tree level.
  3551â†’The Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$ cannot serve as the NMSSM
  3552â†’singlet: $[X] = \text{mass}^{-3}$, so the coupling
  3553â†’$\lambda_0\,X\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$ requires
  3554â†’$[\lambda_0] = \text{mass}^4$; writing
  3555â†’$\lambda_0 = \hat\lambda\,\Lambda^4$, canonical normalisation
  3556â†’gives $\lambda_{\text{NMSSM}} = \hat\lambda$.  The Higgs mass
  3557â†’demands $\hat\lambda \approx 0.51$, but positive meson VEVs
  3558â†’require $\hat\lambda < 2\Lambda^2/v^2 \approx 3\times 10^{-6}$:
  3559â†’a five-order-of-magnitude conflict.  Therefore~$S$ must be a
  3560â†’\emph{separate} elementary singlet with standard NMSSM couplings
  3561â†’$\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d + \tfrac{\kappa}{3}\,S^3$.
  3562â†’At $\tan\beta = 1$ and $\lambda_S = 0.72$, the tree-level
  3563â†’Higgs mass $m_h = \lambda_S v/\sqrt{2} = 125\GeV$ is recovered.
  3564â†’The $\mu$-term $\mu = \lambda_S\langle S\rangle$ is set by the
  3565â†’NMSSM soft potential, leaving $\langle S\rangle$ as a free
  3566â†’parameter of order the electroweak scale.
  3567â†’
  3568â†’\subsection{Vacuum stability, CKM mixing, and the Oakes relation}
  3569â†’
  3570â†’For $N_f = N_c = 3$, the Seiberg description uses
  3571â†’composites $M$, $B$, $\tilde B$ subject to the quantum-modified
  3572â†’constraint $\det M - B\tilde B = \Lambda^{2N_c}$.  The
  3573â†’Lagrange multiplier~$X$ enforces this constraint and has
  3574â†’\emph{no kinetic term}: it is not a dynamical field.
  3575â†’Three independent arguments establish this:
  3576â†’(i)~Seiberg's original construction~\cite{Seiberg:1994bz}
  3577â†’    introduces~$X$ solely to impose the constraint;
  3578â†’(ii)~the magnetic dual has $N_c' = N_f - N_c = 0$---there is no
  3579â†’    magnetic gauge group and no ISS-type pseudo-modulus;
  3580â†’(iii)~the quantum moduli space $\det M - B\tilde B = \Lambda^6$
  3581â†’    is smooth and requires no additional dynamical field.
  3582â†’
  3583â†’Because~$X$ is non-dynamical, $F_X = 0$ at the constrained vacuum:
  3584â†’the equation $\partial W/\partial X = 0$ is a constraint, not an
  3585â†’$F$-term energy contribution.  The diagonal seesaw vacuum
  3586â†’$M^i{}_i = C/m_i$ satisfies the constraint exactly, and all
  3587â†’off-diagonal meson masses are positive:
  3588â†’\[
  3589â†’  m^2(M^a{}_b) = 2\fpi^2 + 2\,|X_0|^2 |M_c|^2
  3590â†’  \approx 2\fpi^2 = 16{,}928\;\MeV^2\,,
  3591â†’\]
  3592â†’where the $F$-term contribution $|X_0|^2|M_c|^2$ is at most
  3593â†’$5\times 10^{-7}\;\MeV^2$ ($10^{-11}$ of the soft term).
  3594â†’\emph{There are no tachyonic modes in the off-diagonal meson sector.}
  3595â†’
  3596â†’One-loop Coleman--Weinberg corrections are positive
  3597â†’(stabilising) and suppressed by a factor~$10^{-9}$ relative to
  3598â†’the tree-level mass.  Moreover, a \emph{fundamental block-diagonal
  3599â†’obstruction} prevents cross-flavour meson mixing: the $6\times 6$
  3600â†’off-diagonal mass matrix decomposes into three independent
  3601â†’$2\times 2$ blocks for conjugate pairs $(M^a{}_b,\,M^b{}_a)$,
  3602â†’and this structure is preserved by all single-trace polynomial
  3603â†’K\"ahler invariants.  The physical reason is index contraction:
  3604â†’at a diagonal vacuum, each $M^a{}_b$ can only contract with its
  3605â†’conjugate $M^b{}_a$, never with a field from a different
  3606â†’flavour pair.  Cross-pair mixing would require non-perturbative
  3607â†’(instanton-generated) K\"ahler corrections or physics beyond the
  3608â†’confined-meson effective theory.
  3609â†’
  3610â†’Although the off-diagonal meson vacuum is exactly diagonal, the
  3611â†’seesaw structure $M_i = C/m_i$ naturally embeds the
  3612â†’Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation.  If a perturbation (from any source)
  3613â†’were to generate off-diagonal condensates $\epsilon_{ab}$, the
  3614â†’seesaw hierarchy would ensure
  3615â†’\[
  3616â†’  \frac{\epsilon_{us}}{\epsilon_{ud}}
  3617â†’  = \sqrt{\frac{m_d}{m_s}} = 0.2236
  3618â†’  = \sin\theta_C\big|_{\text{GST}}\,,
  3619â†’\]
  3620â†’reproducing the Weinberg--Oakes relation \emph{algebraically}.
  3621â†’The GST variant gives $\theta_C = 12.9^\circ$ (PDG:
  3622â†’$13.04^\circ$, $-0.9\%$).  This is a structural property of the
  3623â†’seesaw-inverted mass hierarchy, not a dynamical prediction: the
  3624â†’Cabibbo angle is encoded in $m_d/m_s$ as a free parameter,
  3625â†’with the seesaw providing the natural scaffolding for the Oakes
  3626â†’relation.  The actual origin of CKM mixing---whether from
  3627â†’non-perturbative K\"ahler corrections, physics at the UV
  3628â†’completion scale, or quark mass-matrix texture---remains an
  3629â†’open problem.
  3630â†’
  3631â†’A notable extension to the first generation:
  3632â†’the Koide condition does \emph{not} hold for the triple
  3633â†’$(m_u, m_d, m_s)$ ($Q = 0.567$, 15\% off), but
  3634â†’$Q(0,\,m_u{+}m_d,\,m_s) = 0.665$ is only $0.27\%$ from~$2/3$---closer
  3635â†’than the established seed $Q(0,\,m_s,\,m_c) = 0.664$
  3636â†’at~$0.35\%$.  The Koide chain extends to the first generation but
  3637â†’sees the isospin-summed mass $m_u + m_d = 6.83\MeV$ as a single
  3638â†’seed entry; the splitting into $m_u \neq m_d$ is the remaining
  3639â†’freedom parametrised by~$\theta_C$.
  3640â†’
  3641â†’Without a baryon mass term, the global minimum lies at
  3642â†’$M = 0$, $B\tilde B = -\Lambda^6$, with
  3643â†’$V = \sum m_i^2 \approx 8{,}750\;\MeV^2$.
  3644â†’However, the seesaw vacuum is cosmologically stable:
  3645â†’Coleman--de~Luccia bounce-action estimates give
  3646â†’$S_4 \sim 2 \times 10^7$ (thick-wall) to $10^9$ (thin-wall),
  3647â†’both far above the threshold $S_4 > 400$ required for a lifetime
  3648â†’exceeding the age of the universe.
  3649â†’Adding a baryon mass term
  3650â†’$W_B = m_B\,B\tilde B$ to the superpotential lifts the baryonic
  3651â†’flat direction without affecting the seesaw vacuum
  3652â†’(since $B = \tilde B = 0$ there): the meson masses $M_i$, the off-diagonal meson spectrum,
  3653â†’and the Higgs sector are all unchanged.
  3654â†’With the K\"ahler pole confining $X$ near $X_{\text{seesaw}}$,
  3655â†’any $m_B \gtrsim 1\;\MeV$ eliminates vacuum~(B) entirely.
  3656â†’The natural scale $m_B \sim \Lambda = 300\;\MeV$
  3657â†’(arising from instanton corrections in the confining phase) lifts
  3658â†’the baryino mass from $|X_0| \sim 10^{-9}\;\MeV$ to $\sim 300\;\MeV$
  3659â†’and makes $V_B \sim 10^{20}\;\MeV^2 \gg V_A$.
  3660â†’
  3661â†’\subsection{The bloom mechanism}
  3662â†’
  3663â†’The Koide parametrization
  3664â†’\begin{equation*}
  3665â†’  \sqrt{m_k} = v_0\bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos(\delta + 2\pi k/3)\bigr),
  3666â†’  \quad k = 0,1,2,
  3667â†’\end{equation*}
  3668â†’satisfies $Q = 2/3$ identically for all $\delta$ and $v_0$.
  3669â†’The seed sits at $\delta = 3\pi/4$, where
  3670â†’$\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2}$ forces $\sqrt{m_0} = 0$.
  3671â†’A Koide-preserving bloom is a pure rotation
  3672â†’$\delta \colon 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\mathrm{phys}}$ at fixed $v_0$;
  3673â†’the total mass $M = 6v_0^2$ is conserved while mass redistributes
  3674â†’among the three eigenvalues.
  3675â†’
  3676â†’\paragraph{Adiabatic bion potential.}
  3677â†’The monopole-instanton effective superpotential on $\mathbb{R}^3\times S^1$
  3678â†’takes the form $W_{\mathrm{mon}} \sim \zeta(\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}+\sqrt{m_3})$.
  3679â†’The bion (monopole--anti-monopole) contribution to the
  3680â†’K\"{a}hler potential is $V_{\mathrm{bion}} = |\!\sum_k s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$,
  3681â†’where $s_k = \mathrm{sign}(z_k)$ are the signs of the
  3682â†’Koide amplitudes $z_k = v_0(1+\sqrt{2}\cos(\delta+2\pi k/3))$.
  3683â†’On the interior of the Koide manifold (away from the seed) all
  3684â†’three $z_k$ are nonzero, and the adiabatic signs follow $\delta$:
  3685â†’\begin{equation*}
  3686â†’  \sum_k s_k |z_k| = \sum_k z_k = 3v_0 = \mathrm{const}.
  3687â†’\end{equation*}
  3688â†’This is an exact algebraic identity: the adiabatic bion potential
  3689â†’$V_{\mathrm{bion}} = (3v_0)^2 = 9v_0^2$ is \emph{flat} on the entire Koide
  3690â†’manifold and cannot select $\delta$.
  3691â†’
  3692â†’\paragraph{Frozen-sign bion.}
  3693â†’At the seed ($\delta = 3\pi/4$), one amplitude vanishes and its sign
  3694â†’is topologically frozen: for the quark triple $(-s,c,b)$, the
  3695â†’bloom from seed to physical values takes
  3696â†’$s_0: 0 \to -1$ while $s_{1,2} = +1$ remain fixed.
  3697â†’With frozen signs $s_k = +1$ for all $k$, the frozen-sign potential
  3698â†’\begin{equation*}
  3699â†’  V_{\mathrm{frozen}}(\delta) = \left(\sum_k |z_k|\right)^2
  3700â†’\end{equation*}
  3701â†’is \emph{not} constant on the Koide manifold.  It achieves its
  3702â†’minimum value $9v_0^2$ at the seed ($\delta = 3\pi/4$, where
  3703â†’$\sum |z_k| = 3v_0$, the triangle inequality saturated by
  3704â†’$|z_0| = 0$) and grows for $\delta > 3\pi/4$.
  3705â†’The frozen bion thus provides a restoring force that opposes the bloom.
  3706â†’
  3707â†’\paragraph{Three-instanton driving force.}
  3708â†’The three-instanton operator
  3709â†’$\delta W_{3\text{-inst}} = c_3(\det M)^3/\Lambda^{18}$
  3710â†’arises from the $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ center symmetry of $SU(3)$ SQCD
  3711â†’with $N_f = N_c = 3$ (the anomaly coefficient $6N_c = 18$
  3712â†’breaks $U(1)_{R+2A} \to \mathbb{Z}_{18}$; see the preceding subsection).
  3713â†’Near the Seiberg vacuum, parametrizing
  3714â†’$\det M = \Lambda^6 e^{3i\delta}$ gives
  3715â†’\begin{equation*}
  3716â†’  V_{3\text{-inst}} \propto -\cos(9\delta).
  3717â†’\end{equation*}
  3718â†’For the quark triple $(-s,c,b)$, the observed phase is
  3719â†’$\delta_{\mathrm{phys}} = 157.2^\circ$.
  3720â†’The nearest minimum of $-\cos(9\delta)$ is at $\delta = 160^\circ$,
  3721â†’just $2.8^\circ$ away.
  3722â†’The combined potential
  3723â†’\begin{equation*}
  3724â†’  V(\delta) = A\,V_{\mathrm{frozen}}(\delta) + B\,(-\cos 9\delta)
  3725â†’\end{equation*}
  3726â†’has a stable equilibrium at the physical phase for
  3727â†’$B/A = -2.07$: the three-instanton term drives the bloom outward
  3728â†’from the seed, while the frozen-sign bion provides the restoring
  3729â†’force that stabilizes the physical triple.
  3730â†’
  3731â†’\paragraph{Lepton triple.}
  3732â†’For the lepton triple $(e,\mu,\tau)$, all three amplitudes
  3733â†’$z_k > 0$ throughout the bloom ($\delta_\ell = 132.7^\circ$,
  3734â†’never crossing zero), so the adiabatic and frozen-sign bion potentials
  3735â†’coincide: $V_{\mathrm{frozen}} = 9v_0^2$ exactly (flat).
  3736â†’The bion potential provides no selection.
  3737â†’The near-rational relation $\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$
  3738â†’($33\,\mathrm{ppm}$) is therefore \emph{not} explained by the
  3739â†’bion mechanism; its origin is the three-instanton potential
  3740â†’$-\cos(9\delta)$ with a minimum at $\delta \equiv 2/9 \pmod{2\pi/3}$,
  3741â†’as discussed in the preceding subsection.
  3742â†’The lepton and quark phases are selected by the same instanton
  3743â†’potential but through different dynamical channels:
  3744â†’for quarks the frozen-sign bion competes with the instanton term,
  3745â†’while for leptons the bion is inert and the instanton acts unopposed.
  3746â†’
  3747â†’\subsection{The $SO(32)$ projection}
  3748â†’The adjoint $\mathbf{496}$ contains the sBootstrap
  3749â†’representations: $\mathbf{24}$ in the neutral sector and
  3750â†’$\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ in the matter
  3751â†’sector at $|Q_A| = 2$. The remaining 442 states must be
  3752â†’projected out or made massive. No projection mechanism has been
  3753â†’identified. The alternative anomaly-free gauge group
  3754â†’$E_8 \times E_8$ is blocked by the $\mathbf{15}$ vs.\
  3755â†’$\mathbf{10}$ obstruction (\S\ref{subsec:so32_open}),
  3756â†’constraining the string embedding to the Type~I / $SO(32)$
  3757â†’corner of the landscape.
  3758â†’
  3759â†’\subsection{Generation uniqueness}
  3760â†’The Diophantine system uniquely selects $N = 3$ generations,
  3761â†’but the third equation ($r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N$) has the weakest
  3762â†’physical motivation. A dynamical derivation of the counting
  3763â†’constraints would strengthen the argument considerably.
  3764â†’

  3816â†’No tachyons: $F_X = 0$ (Lagrange multiplier) & Derived & Block-diagonal obstruction \\
  3817â†’GST relation $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ & Structural & Seesaw encodes Oakes; not derived \\
  3818â†’$\mu = \lambda_S\langle S\rangle \sim O(v)$ & Free & Standard NMSSM ($S \neq X$) \\
  3819â†’K\"ahler cannot generate $Q_\ell = 2/3$ & Derived & Rank invariance of $W_{IJ}$ \\
  3820â†’FCNC: mesons = SM kaons, not extra d.o.f.\ & Noted & Extra SUSY FCNC TBD \\
  3821â†’$X \neq S$: $\hat\lambda_{\text{Higgs}}/\hat\lambda_{\text{max}} \sim 10^5$ & Derived & Dimensional analysis \\
  3822â†’O'R--Koide universal (gauge-independent) & Derived & $SU(2)$, $Sp(2)$, $SU(3)$ all give $Q = 2/3$ \\
  3823â†’$SU(2)$ lepton sector: metastable O'R + Pfaffian & Derived & s-confining, $N_f = 3$ \\
  3824â†’Seesaw vacuum cosmologically stable & Derived & $S_4 \sim 2 \times 10^7 \gg 400$ \\
  3825â†’$m_B\,B\tilde B$ lifts baryon vacuum, no meson changes & Derived & $m_B \gtrsim 1\;\MeV$ suffices \\
  3826â†’Adiabatic bion flat on Koide manifold & Derived & $\sum s_k|z_k| = 3v_0$ (identity) \\
  3827â†’Bloom driven by $\cos(9\delta)$, stabilised by bion & Derived & Physical $\delta$ within $2.8^\circ$ of $\cos(9\delta)$ min \\
  3828â†’\bottomrule
  3829â†’\end{tabular}
  3830â†’\end{center}
  3831â†’
  3832â†’%======================================================================
  3833â†’\section{Conclusions}
  3834â†’\label{sec:conclusions}
  3835â†’%======================================================================
  3836â†’
  3837â†’Seven principal results:
  3838â†’
  3839â†’\paragraph{1.\ The Koide formula is energy equipartition.}
  3840â†’$Q = 2/3$ is $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$: perturbation energies 
  3841â†’match the base scale. The signed-charge framework resolves the 
  3842â†’electron mass puzzle (near-cancellation, not fine-tuning) and reveals 
  3843â†’that mesons satisfy Koide with $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ to $0.1\%$.
  3844â†’
  3845â†’\paragraph{2.\ Seed triples set the stage for SUSY breaking.}
  3846â†’The quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ share the
  3847â†’universal ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$ and operate at $v_0^2 \sim
  3848â†’230$--$350\MeV$, close to the lepton triple's $v_0^2 = 314\MeV
  3849â†’\approx \fpi^2/27$
  3850â†’(recall that $v_0^2$ carries dimensions of MeV, not MeV$^2$,
  3851â†’because $v_0 = z_0$ is a charge with dimensions MeV$^{1/2}$). 
  3852â†’The massless member is the necessary condition for F-term 
  3853â†’(O'Raifeartaigh) SUSY breaking. Upon breaking, seeds bloom into 
  3854â†’full triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi,D_s,B)$. The lepton triple 
  3855â†’is the ``almost-seed'' that has barely bloomed.
  3856â†’
  3857â†’\paragraph{3.\ The seed condition is one superpotential constraint,
  3858â†’stabilized by a K\"ahler pole.}
  3859â†’The O'Raifeartaigh model with superpotential
  3860â†’$W = f\Phi_0 + m\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\Phi_0\Phi_1^2$ produces a
  3861â†’fermionic spectrum $(0,\, m_-,\, m_+)$ that is an exact Koide
  3862â†’seed triple when $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$. The one-loop CW potential
  3863â†’alone stabilizes the pseudo-modulus at $v = 0$; a negative
  3864â†’quartic K\"ahler correction $c\,|\Phi_0|^4/\Lambda_K^2$ with
  3865â†’$c = -\Lambda_K^2/(12m^2)$ pins it at the K\"ahler pole
  3866â†’$v = \sqrt{3}\,m/g$ exactly. The Koide seed emerges at
  3867â†’$M_0 = 2m/3$.  The coefficient $c = -1/12$ is not radiatively
  3868â†’generated but arises from magnetic bions on
  3869â†’$\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ at $\alpha_s = 0.143$
  3870â†’in the ISS magnetic dual.
  3871â†’
  3872â†’\paragraph{4.\ Two breaking mechanisms plus a heavy-sector origin.}
  3873â†’F-term breaking (via the massless seed member) generates the light 
  3874â†’sector; the Volkov--Akulov relation $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is its 
  3875â†’low-energy realization, not a separate mechanism. D-term breaking 
  3876â†’($U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI parameter) provides electromagnetic 
  3877â†’splittings as a perturbative correction. The heavy triple 
  3878â†’$(c,b,t)$ stands apart: no seed, no meson shadow, scale far above 
  3879â†’$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, possibly connected to electroweak breaking.
  3880â†’
  3881â†’\paragraph{5.\ The Diophantine counting uniquely selects $N=3$.}
  3882â†’Three equations on the scalar content---$rs = 2N$, $r(r+1)/2 = 2N$,
  3883â†’$r^2+s^2-1 = 4N$---have the unique positive-integer solution
  3884â†’$(N,r,s) = (3,3,2)$, which maps to the $SU(5)$ representations
  3885â†’$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  3886â†’This combination is anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
  3887â†’
  3888â†’\paragraph{6.\ An algebraic electroweak ratio.}
  3889â†’The eigenvalue equation $x^2 + s(s+1)\,x - s(s+1) = 0$, with
  3890â†’$s$ the spin Casimir, predicts
  3891â†’$R = 1 - x_+(1/2)/x_+(1) = 0.22310$, matching the on-shell
  3892â†’$\sin^2\theta_W = 0.22320$ to $0.4\sigma$ and giving
  3893â†’$M_W = 80.374\GeV$ ($0.39\sigma$ from PDG). The equation is
  3894â†’pinned by three structural constraints; $R$ follows as a
  3895â†’consequence, not a free parameter. The dynamical origin of the
  3896â†’equation and the choice of renormalization scheme remain open.
  3897â†’
  3898â†’\paragraph{7.\ An explicit Lagrangian with two to three free parameters.}
  3899â†’The full $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian
  3900â†’(\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}) comprises a K\"ahler potential
  3901â†’with a negative quartic correction $c = -1/12$ (stabilizing
  3902â†’the pseudo-modulus at $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$) and a bion correction
  3903â†’$K_{\text{bion}} \sim |\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$,
  3904â†’a superpotential combining the Seiberg constraint
  3905â†’with Yukawa couplings (using separate $H_u$, $H_d$ doublets)
  3906â†’and an NMSSM coupling $\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$
  3907â†’($\lambda_S = 0.72$ for $m_h = 125\GeV$, with $S$ a separate
  3908â†’elementary singlet, distinct from the Lagrange multiplier~$X$),
  3909â†’standard $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ $D$-terms
  3910â†’(vanishing at $\tan\beta = 1$),
  3911â†’and soft breaking $V_{\text{soft}} = \fpi^2\,\text{Tr}(M^\dagger M)$.
  3912â†’The supertrace $\text{STr}[M^2] = 18\,\fpi^2$
  3913â†’receives contributions only from the nine soft meson masses.
  3914â†’The mixed up/down-type Koide triples require $\tan\beta = 1$
  3915â†’for the mass-level condition to equal a Yukawa-level
  3916â†’condition---a prediction independently motivated by $SU(5)_f$.
  3917â†’The seed Koide fixes the
  3918â†’pseudo-modulus VEV $\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ (predicting $m_c/m_s$); the
  3919â†’bion K\"ahler correction fixes $m_b$ through the
  3920â†’$v_0$-doubling condition, with Hessian rank~1 constraining
  3921â†’exactly one mass combination.  The self-consistent chain from $m_s$ alone predicts
  3922â†’$m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$, $m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$, and---if
  3923â†’$Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ is imposed---$m_t = 171.3 \pm 1.5\GeV$
  3924â†’(Table~\ref{tab:predictions}).
  3925â†’The lepton Koide scale satisfies $M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$
  3926â†’to $0.04\%$, tying lepton masses to the pion decay constant.
  3927â†’The Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$ has no kinetic term
  3928â†’($F_X = 0$), and all off-diagonal meson masses are positive;
  3929â†’a block-diagonal obstruction in the K\"ahler sector prevents
  3930â†’cross-flavour condensation at any perturbative order.
  3931â†’The seesaw structure nevertheless embeds the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin
  3932â†’relation $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ algebraically,
  3933â†’so the Cabibbo angle is naturally encoded in the mass hierarchy
  3934â†’even though the mixing itself must originate from a mechanism
  3935â†’beyond the confined-meson effective theory.
  3936â†’After Koide and $v_0$-doubling constraints,
  3937â†’only $m_u$, $m_d$, and $m_s$ remain free; $m_u$ and
  3938â†’$m_d$ are connected to the Cabibbo angle through the
  3939â†’Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation.  The $SO(32)$ adjoint provides the UV completion,
  3940â†’with the $E_8 \times E_8$ alternative blocked by the
  3941â†’$\mathbf{15}$-vs.-$\mathbf{10}$ obstruction.
  3942â†’
  3943â†’The diquark sector, required to complete the SUSY multiplet count,
  3944â†’fills the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ of
  3945â†’$SU(5)_f$---the representation produced naturally by the SUSY
  3946â†’generator acting on quarks and by the $SO(32)$ decomposition. A
  3947â†’kinematic theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) shows that \emph{no}
  3948â†’$J=0$ color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor, regardless
  3949â†’of orbital angular momentum: the sBootstrap diquark in the
  3950â†’$\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite. The Miyazawa supercharge
  3951â†’$\mathcal{Q}_{[ij]} \in \overline{\mathbf{10}}$
  3952â†’(\S\ref{subsec:baryon_open}) connects mesons to baryons but
  3953â†’generates diquark operators in $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$
  3954â†’(antisymmetric), not $\mathbf{15}$ (symmetric).  The remaining
  3955â†’candidate is a fundamental string-theoretic state from the
  3956â†’$SO(32)$ adjoint.
  3957â†’
  3958â†’The organizing principle is that each sector of the spectrum begins
  3959â†’with a massless member (a seed), which is the necessary condition
  3960â†’for spontaneous SUSY breaking. The breaking lifts the zero,
  3961â†’generates the full triple, and the energy-balance condition
  3962â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ constrains the initial conditions.
  3963â†’The bloom from seed to full triple is a phase rotation
  3964â†’$\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ in the Koide
  3965â†’parametrization, at fixed Koide scale $v_0$; it is not a
  3966â†’continuous deformation of the O'Raifeartaigh spectrum (which would
  3967â†’break $Q = 2/3$) but a nonperturbative rearrangement.
  3968â†’The bloom mechanism (preceding section) identifies the
  3969â†’dynamical origin of $\delta$: the adiabatic bion potential is
  3970â†’exactly flat on the Koide manifold, but with frozen topological signs
  3971â†’it provides a restoring force opposing the bloom; the three-instanton
  3972â†’potential $-\cos(9\delta)$ drives $\delta$ away from the seed, and their
  3973â†’equilibrium lies within $2.8^\circ$ of the physical quark bloom position.
  3974â†’The unified superpotential has a layered structure:
  3975â†’(i)~the Seiberg effective superpotential governs the light sector,
  3976â†’with SUSY breaking forced by the quantum constraint when $m_u = m_d = 0$;
  3977â†’(ii)~the O'Raifeartaigh condition $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ (equivalently
  3978â†’$\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ in the magnetic dual) fixes the seed Koide ratio,
  3979â†’predicting $m_c$ from $m_s$;
  3980â†’(iii)~the bion K\"ahler correction
  3981â†’$|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ predicts
  3982â†’$m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$ via $v_0$-doubling;
  3983â†’(iv)~the Yukawa sector governs the $(c,b,t)$ triple as an
  3984â†’overdetermination test.
  3985â†’After these constraints, only two to three free parameters remain:
  3986â†’$m_u$, $m_d$ (connected to the Cabibbo angle through the GST
  3987â†’relation), and the overall scale $m_s$. All quark Koide triples
  3988â†’obligatorily mix up-type and down-type mass eigenstates.
  3989â†’The energy-balance condition is a boundary condition on the
  3990â†’superpotential, not an RG attractor; the Koide ratios are naturally
  3991â†’protected against $D$-term perturbations because $D$-terms shift
  3992â†’scalar masses but leave fermion masses untouched.
  3993â†’
  3994â†’\begin{thebibliography}{99}
  3995â†’\bibitem{Rivero2005}
  3996â†’A.~Rivero, ``An interpretation of scalars in SO(32),''
  3997â†’Eur.\ Phys.\ J.\ C \textbf{84}, 1058 (2024),
  3998â†’arXiv:2407.05397 [hep-ph]; earlier versions: viXra:0503.001 (2005),
  3999â†’viXra:0809.001 (2008).
  4000â†’
  4001â†’\bibitem{Rivero2012}
  4002â†’A.~Rivero, ``A new Koide tuple: strange, charm, bottom,'' 
  4003â†’arXiv:1111.7232 [hep-ph] (2011).
  4004â†’
  4005â†’\bibitem{Koide1983}
  4006â†’Y.~Koide, ``New viewpoint of quark and lepton mass hierarchy,'' 
  4007â†’Phys.\ Rev.\ D \textbf{28}, 252 (1983).
  4008â†’
  4009â†’\bibitem{CattoGursey}
  4010â†’S.~Catto and F.~G\"ursey, ``Algebraic treatment of effective 
  4011â†’supersymmetry,'' Nuovo Cim.\ A \textbf{86}, 201 (1985).
  4012â†’
  4013â†’\bibitem{Miyazawa}
  4014â†’H.~Miyazawa, ``Baryon Number Changing Currents,'' 
  4015â†’Prog.\ Theor.\ Phys.\ \textbf{36}, 1266 (1966).
  4016â†’
  4017â†’\bibitem{Harari}
  4018â†’H.~Harari, H.~Haut, J.~Weyers, ``Quark masses and Cabibbo 
  4019â†’angles,'' Phys.\ Lett.\ B \textbf{78}, 459 (1978).
  4020â†’
  4021â†’\bibitem{Seiberg1995}
  4022â†’N.~Seiberg, ``Electric--magnetic duality in supersymmetric 
  4023â†’non-abelian gauge theories,'' Nucl.\ Phys.\ B \textbf{435}, 129 
  4024â†’(1995).
  4025â†’
  4026â†’\bibitem{ORaifeartaigh}

  4028â†’scalar superfields,'' Nucl.\ Phys.\ B \textbf{96}, 331 (1975).

  4033â†’461 (1974).
  4034â†’

4036â†’E.~Witten, ``Some inequalities among hadron masses,'' 
  4037â†’Phys.\ Rev.\ Lett.\ \textbf{51}, 2351 (1983).
  4038â†’

  4045â†’divergences under $SU(3) \times SU(3)$,'' 
  4046â†’Phys.\ Rev.\ \textbf{175}, 2195 (1968).

  4050â†’in meta-stable vacua,'' JHEP \textbf{0604}, 021 (2006),
  4051â†’arXiv:hep-ph/0602239.

  4056â†’Phys.\ Rev.\ Lett.\ \textbf{18}, 759 (1967).
