content.text: Now let me also read the parts I skipped (the O'Raifeartaigh section and some later sections) for any content needed by Paper B, specifically the v0-doubling derivation and the "exact seed" discussion.

content.content:
1746â†’$\Phi_b$ & $m_b = \zch_b^2$ & Heavy massive member \\
  1747â†’\bottomrule
  1748â†’\end{tabular}
  1749â†’\end{center}
  1750â†’
  1751â†’The ratio $\zch_a/\zch_b = 2 - \sqrt{3}$ is enforced by the 
  1752â†’Koide energy-balance condition applied to the seed.
  1753â†’
  1754â†’In the Seiberg description, these three superfields are entries of 
  1755â†’the meson matrix $M^i{}_j$. For the $(0, s, c)$ seed, the relevant 
  1756â†’entries involve the strange and charm quarks; $\Phi_0$ is the entry 
  1757â†’whose quark-mass combination vanishes in the seed limit (i.e., the 
  1758â†’direction in flavor space where $m_i + m_j \to 0$ through GOR).
  1759â†’
  1760â†’\subsection{F-term breaking: the O'Raifeartaigh mechanism}
  1761â†’\label{subsec:fterm}
  1762â†’
  1763â†’The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} tells us \emph{that} SUSY is broken at 
  1764â†’scale $f_\pi$. To understand \emph{how}, we need the microscopic 
  1765â†’mechanism. The O'Raifeartaigh model \cite{ORaifeartaigh} is the simplest 
  1766â†’realization of spontaneous $F$-term SUSY breaking. With three chiral 
  1767â†’superfields, the superpotential
  1768â†’\begin{equation}
  1769â†’W = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_1^2
  1770â†’\end{equation}
  1771â†’gives $F$-terms:
  1772â†’\begin{align}
  1773â†’F_{\Phi_0}^* &= f + g\,\phi_1^2\,, \\
  1774â†’F_{\Phi_1}^* &= m\,\phi_2 + 2g\,\phi_0\phi_1\,, \\
  1775â†’F_{\Phi_2}^* &= m\,\phi_1\,.
  1776â†’\end{align}
  1777â†’If $f \neq 0$ and $m \neq 0$, then $F_{\Phi_2}^* = 0$ requires 
  1778â†’$\phi_1 = 0$, but then $F_{\Phi_0}^* = f \neq 0$: SUSY is 
  1779â†’\textbf{necessarily broken}. The key features are:
  1780â†’\begin{enumerate}
  1781â†’\item A massless field ($\Phi_0$) exists at tree level---it is the 
  1782â†’pseudo-modulus.
  1783â†’\item The breaking is triggered because $F_{\Phi_0}$ cannot vanish.
  1784â†’\item After including loop corrections, $\Phi_0$ acquires a mass 
  1785â†’(the Coleman--Weinberg potential lifts the flat direction).
  1786â†’\end{enumerate}
  1787â†’
  1788â†’The structural parallel with the seed triples is:
  1789â†’
  1790â†’\begin{center}
  1791â†’\begin{tabular}{llll}
  1792â†’\toprule
  1793â†’O'Raifeartaigh & Flavor & Seed & After breaking \\
  1794â†’\midrule
  1795â†’$\Phi_0$ (massless) & $b$ / $B$ & $\zch = 0$ (flat direction) & $\zch_b = 64.65$ (mass generated) \\
  1796â†’$\Phi_1$ (light) & $s$ / $\pi$ & $\zch_s = 9.67$ & $\zch \to -9.67$ (sign-flipped) \\
  1797â†’$\Phi_2$ (heavy) & $c$ / $D_s$ & $\zch_c = 35.64$ & $\zch_c \approx 35.64$ (shifted) \\
  1798â†’\midrule
  1799â†’ & & $f = 0$ (SUSY) & $f = f_\pi$ (broken) \\
  1800â†’\bottomrule
  1801â†’\end{tabular}
  1802â†’\end{center}
  1803â†’
  1804â†’This parallel is more than structural: the minimal O'Raifeartaigh
  1805â†’model gives $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ a single mass parameter $m$, but the
  1806â†’\emph{fermionic} mass matrix at the vacuum ($\phi_1 = 0$,
  1807â†’$\phi_0 = v$) is
  1808â†’\begin{equation*}
  1809â†’  W_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2gv & m \\ 0 & m & 0 \end{pmatrix},
  1810â†’\end{equation*}
  1811â†’with eigenvalues $0$ (the goldstino) and $m_\pm = \sqrt{g^2v^2 + m^2} \pm gv$.
  1812â†’The mass ratio $m_-/m_+ = (\sqrt{t^2+1} - t)^2$, where $t = gv/m$,
  1813â†’is \emph{not} unity.  Setting $\sqrt{m_-}/\sqrt{m_+} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$
  1814â†’(the Koide seed ratio) requires $\sqrt{t^2+1} - t = 2 - \sqrt{3}$,
  1815â†’which gives
  1816â†’\begin{equation*}
  1817â†’  t = \frac{gv}{m} = \sqrt{3}\,,
  1818â†’\end{equation*}
  1819â†’the unique value at which $t^2 + 1 = 4$, so $m_\pm = (2 \pm \sqrt{3})\,m$.
  1820â†’The Koide ratio for the triple $(0, m_-, m_+) = (0,\, (2-\sqrt{3})\,m,
  1821â†’\, (2+\sqrt{3})\,m)$ evaluates to $Q = 4m/(\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}} +
  1822â†’\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}})^2 = 4/6 = 2/3$ exactly, since
  1823â†’$\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}}\cdot\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}} = 1$ and the squared sum
  1824â†’telescopes to $6$.
  1825â†’
  1826â†’The Koide condition on the seed is therefore \emph{one algebraic
  1827â†’constraint on the superpotential parameters}: $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$.
  1828â†’The coupling $g$, the pseudo-modulus VEV $v$, and the mass
  1829â†’parameter $m$ are individually undetermined, but their ratio is fixed.
  1830â†’This is the microscopic content of the energy-balance condition
  1831â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ applied to the seed.
  1832â†’
  1833â†’The $b$ quark and $B$ meson are
  1834â†’always present as flavor degrees of freedom; the seed configuration
  1835â†’$(0, s, c)$ means the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction before
  1836â†’breaking. The bloom is not particle creation but mass generation:
  1837â†’breaking lifts $\Phi_0$ from zero to $\zch_b \neq 0$, simultaneously
  1838â†’flipping the sign of $\zch_s$, so that the same three flavors
  1839â†’rearrange from $(0, s, c)$ into $(-s, c, b)$.
  1840â†’
  1841â†’\paragraph{The bloom as a phase rotation.}
  1842â†’In the Koide parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, the seed sits at
  1843â†’$\delta = 3\pi/4$, where $\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2}$ forces one
  1844â†’mass to vanish: $\sqrt{m_0} = \sqrt{M_0}(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos\delta) = 0$.
  1845â†’A Koide-preserving bloom ($Q = 2/3$ maintained) is a pure rotation
  1846â†’$\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at fixed scale $v_0$
  1847â†’and fixed amplitude $r = \sqrt{2}\,v_0$. The total mass
  1848â†’$M = 6v_0^2$ is conserved---mass merely redistributes among the three
  1849â†’eigenvalues. The sign flip is instantaneous: the seed sits exactly at
  1850â†’the boundary $\zch_0 = 0$, and any infinitesimal rotation generates
  1851â†’a nonzero charge with definite sign.
  1852â†’
  1853â†’Explicit R-symmetry breaking in the O'Raifeartaigh model (adding a
  1854â†’term $\delta W \sim \varepsilon\,\Phi_0\Phi_2$) lifts the goldstino
  1855â†’mass as $m_0 \sim \varepsilon^2/m$, but simultaneously pushes
  1856â†’$Q$ away from $2/3$---no nonzero $\varepsilon$ preserves the Koide
  1857â†’condition. The Koide seed is an \emph{unstable fixed point} of the
  1858â†’R-breaking perturbation. In the dynamical (SQCD) realization, the
  1859â†’bloom must therefore be a nonperturbative rearrangement---not a
  1860â†’smooth deformation of the O'Raifeartaigh spectrum.
  1861â†’
  1862â†’A striking difference from standard O'Raifeartaigh: the 
  1863â†’pseudo-modulus $\Phi_0$ normally acquires the \emph{smallest} mass 
  1864â†’in the spectrum (a loop-suppressed Coleman--Weinberg mass, 
  1865â†’parametrically $\sim g^2 f/16\pi^2 m$). In the sBootstrap bloom, 
  1866â†’the $b$-direction acquires the \emph{largest} mass 
  1867â†’($m_b \gg m_s$ and $m_b > m_c$). The bloom is far more dramatic than a 
  1868â†’perturbative CW lifting---it is a nonperturbative, strong-dynamics 
  1869â†’effect in which confinement generates a mass of order $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/m_q$, 
  1870â†’not $g^2/(16\pi^2)$. Numerically, the one-loop CW mass for the
  1871â†’pseudo-modulus at the Seiberg vacuum is $m_{\text{CW}} \approx 13\MeV$,
  1872â†’more than two orders of magnitude below the physical $m_b = 4{,}180\MeV$.
  1873â†’This inversion is a signature of the dynamical (SQCD) realization
  1874â†’versus the perturbative (superpotential) one.
  1875â†’
  1876â†’\paragraph{A candidate mechanism: fractional instantons.}
  1877â†’On $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, SU($N_c$) instantons fractionalize into
  1878â†’$N_c$ monopole-instantons \cite{Unsal2008}. Each monopole-instanton
  1879â†’carries a single fermionic zero mode per flavor; when the zero mode is
  1880â†’lifted by the quark mass $m_i$, the amplitude acquires a factor
  1881â†’$\sqrt{m_i}$ (not $m_i$). Since the $N_c = 3$ monopole contributions
  1882â†’enter \emph{additively} in the effective superpotential,
  1883â†’\begin{equation*}
  1884â†’  W_{\text{mon}} \sim \zeta\,\bigl(\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2} + \sqrt{m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1885â†’\end{equation*}
  1886â†’where $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/3}$ is the monopole fugacity. This is
  1887â†’\emph{exactly} the sum-of-square-roots structure of the vacuum charge
  1888â†’$v_0 = (\sum \sqrt{m_k})/3$.
  1889â†’The $v_0$-doubling then maps to the counting of active
  1890â†’monopole-instanton contributions: the seed (two active flavors) involves
  1891â†’two monopole amplitudes, while the bloom (three active flavors, one with
  1892â†’a sign flip) involves three, doubling $v_0$.
  1893â†’
  1894â†’This mechanism is non-holomorphic in the sense required: the
  1895â†’K\"ahler potential receives monopole--anti-monopole (bion) cross terms
  1896â†’of the form $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$, where $m_k$ are the
  1897â†’current quark masses. Expanding for $N_c = 3$:
  1898â†’\begin{equation*}
  1899â†’  K_{\text{bion}} = \frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\bigl(
  1900â†’  m_1 + m_2 + m_3
  1901â†’  + 2s_1 s_2\sqrt{m_1 m_2}
  1902â†’  + 2s_1 s_3\sqrt{m_1 m_3}
  1903â†’  + 2s_2 s_3\sqrt{m_2 m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1904â†’\end{equation*}
  1905â†’where the diagonal terms $s_k^2 m_k = m_k$ are self-bions and the
  1906â†’cross terms $\sqrt{m_i m_j}$ are mixed bion amplitudes---their
  1907â†’geometric mean structure is what produces $\sum\sqrt{m_k}$.
  1908â†’Note that $m_k$ here are the quark masses (superpotential couplings),
  1909â†’\emph{not} the meson VEVs $M^k{}_k \propto 1/m_k$ of the Seiberg
  1910â†’seesaw; the bion amplitude involves quark zero modes, not meson fields.
  1911â†’Organizing the bion amplitudes into
  1912â†’seed-sector ($S_{\text{seed}} = \sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$) and
  1913â†’bloom-sector ($S_{\text{bloom}} = -\!\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b}$)
  1914â†’channels, the K\"ahler correction takes the form
  1915â†’\begin{equation*}
  1916â†’  \delta K \sim \lambda_1\,|S_{\text{seed}}|^2
  1917â†’  + \lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2\,.
  1918â†’\end{equation*}
  1919â†’The second term is a perfect square that vanishes
  1920â†’\emph{at the minimum} when $S_{\text{bloom}} = 2\,S_{\text{seed}}$,
  1921â†’i.e., $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$---the
  1922â†’$v_0$-doubling condition. The relation between bion couplings
  1923â†’($\lambda_3 = -4\lambda_2$ for cross-channel bions) is what
  1924â†’enforces the doubling. Numerically,
  1925â†’$S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}} = 0.023\MeV^{1/2}$
  1926â†’(effectively zero: $m_b = 4{,}177$ vs.\ PDG $4{,}180$).
  1927â†’The Hessian of $\lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  1928â†’has rank~1: its single nonzero eigenvector is
  1929â†’$\partial f/\partial m_i$ (the gradient of
  1930â†’$f = \sqrt{m_b} - 3\sqrt{m_s} - \sqrt{m_c}$), while the two flat
  1931â†’directions correspond to independent variation of $m_s$ and $m_c$
  1932â†’at fixed $f$.  Thus the bion K\"ahler constrains exactly \emph{one}
  1933â†’mass combination; the remaining two masses are fixed by the Koide
  1934â†’seed in the superpotential. Including propagated uncertainties
  1935â†’($\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$, $\delta m_c = 20\MeV$), the prediction
  1936â†’is $m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$, consistent with the PDG value
  1937â†’$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ at $0.06\sigma$.
  1938â†’
  1939â†’The principal caveat is that this analysis
  1940â†’applies on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$; connecting to $\mathbb{R}^4$
  1941â†’physics requires \"Unsal's continuity conjecture~\cite{Unsal2012},
  1942â†’which posits that center-symmetric confinement at small $S^1$ is
  1943â†’continuously connected to the large-$S^1$ confining vacuum.
  1944â†’The conjecture is supported by lattice evidence for $SU(N)$ pure
  1945â†’Yang--Mills and by the absence of phase transitions in the
  1946â†’center-stabilized theory, but remains unproven for theories with
  1947â†’matter.
  1948â†’
  1949â†’The identification $\langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle = f \sim \fpi^2$ is the
  1950â†’sBootstrap's central claim: the SUSY-breaking $F$-term is the chiral
  1951â†’condensate parameter.
  1952â†’
  1953â†’In SQCD, this is realized dynamically rather than through a 
  1954â†’superpotential: the chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ acts as the 
  1955â†’nonzero $F$-term, the impossibility of restoring chiral symmetry 
  1956â†’in the QCD vacuum is the dynamical analogue of $f \neq 0$, and the 
  1957â†’pion---the would-be Goldstone boson that acquires mass from explicit 
  1958â†’chiral breaking---plays the role of $\Phi_0$ whose mass is lifted 
  1959â†’by loop corrections (here, by quark masses).
  1960â†’
  1961â†’\subsection{The Goldstino direction and the Volkov--Akulov realization}
  1962â†’\label{subsec:va}
  1963â†’
  1964â†’When SUSY is spontaneously broken, a massless fermion appears: the 
  1965â†’Goldstino, the fermionic analogue of the Goldstone boson. Its decay 
  1966â†’constant $f_{\text{VA}}$ sets the SUSY-breaking scale through the 
  1967â†’Volkov--Akulov Lagrangian \cite{VolkovAkulov1973}:
  1968â†’\begin{equation}
  1969â†’\mathcal{L}_{\text{VA}} = -f_{\text{VA}}^2 \det\!\left(
  1970â†’\delta_\mu^\nu + \frac{i}{f_{\text{VA}}^2}\,\bar{\lambda}\bar{\sigma}^\nu
  1971â†’\partial_\mu\lambda\right),
  1972â†’\end{equation}
  1973â†’where $\lambda$ is the Goldstino field. At low energies, the Goldstino 
  1974â†’couples derivatively with strength $\sim 1/f_{\text{VA}}^2$, exactly 
  1975â†’as the pion couples with strength $\sim 1/f_\pi^2$ in the chiral 
  1976â†’Lagrangian.
  1977â†’
  1978â†’The sBootstrap identification is:
  1979â†’\begin{equation}
  1980â†’f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi\,, \qquad 
  1981â†’\text{Goldstino} = \text{muon (partner of }\pi\text{)}\,.
  1982â†’\end{equation}
  1983â†’More precisely, the muon is a \emph{pseudo-Goldstino}: in the 
  1984â†’limit $m_q \to 0$ where the pion becomes exactly massless (a true 
  1985â†’Goldstone boson), its fermionic partner also becomes massless (a 
  1986â†’true Goldstino). The finite quark masses explicitly break both 
  1987â†’chiral symmetry (giving $m_\pi \neq 0$) and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1988â†’(giving $m_\mu \neq m_\pi$), with the splitting controlled by 
  1989â†’$f_\pi$:
  1990â†’\begin{equation}
  1991â†’m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2\,.
  1992â†’\end{equation}
  1993â†’
  1994â†’This is the Volkov--Akulov relation for the pseudo-Goldstino: the 
  1995â†’mass splitting between the scalar (pion) and its fermionic partner

content.text: Now let me also check the "exact seed" discussion and the (c,b,t) triple interpretation sections, which are needed for Paper B.

content.content:
2397â†’\subsection{The exact seed and what breaks it}
  2398â†’\label{subsec:exactseed}
  2399â†’
  2400â†’The seed configuration $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ requires \emph{two} 
  2401â†’independent conditions:
  2402â†’\begin{enumerate}
  2403â†’\item One member at $\zch = 0$ exactly (the flat direction).
  2404â†’\item The ratio of the massive members equals $2 - \sqrt{3}$:
  2405â†’\begin{equation}
  2406â†’\frac{\sqrt{m_a}}{\sqrt{m_b}} = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.
  2407â†’\end{equation}
  2408â†’\end{enumerate}
  2409â†’These are logically distinct. The chiral limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$, 
  2410â†’$\qqbar \neq 0$) enforces condition (1) for the meson sector: the 
  2411â†’pion is an exact Goldstone boson, $m_\pi = 0$, and in the 
  2412â†’picture of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} the $B$-meson direction sits at 
  2413â†’the flat direction before acquiring mass. But the chiral limit says 
  2414â†’\emph{nothing} about condition (2)---it does not constrain 
  2415â†’$\sqrt{m_{D_s}}/\sqrt{m_B}$ or $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ 
  2416â†’to equal $2 - \sqrt{3}$.
  2417â†’
  2418â†’The physical deviations from the exact ratio are small but 
  2419â†’measurable:
  2420â†’
  2421â†’\begin{center}
  2422â†’\begin{tabular}{lccc}
  2423â†’\toprule
  2424â†’Seed & Exact ratio & Physical ratio & Deviation \\
  2425â†’\midrule
  2426â†’$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2427â†’$\sqrt{m_\pi}/\sqrt{m_{D_s}} = 0.2663$ & $-0.6\%$ \\
  2428â†’$(0, s, c)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2429â†’$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} = 0.2712$ & $+1.2\%$ \\
  2430â†’$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2431â†’$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 0.2439$ & $-9.0\%$ \\
  2432â†’\bottomrule
  2433â†’\end{tabular}
  2434â†’\end{center}
  2435â†’
  2436â†’The meson seed is closest to the exact ratio (0.6\% deviation). 
  2437â†’The quark seed deviates by 1.2\%. A natural thought is that QCD 
  2438â†’running might shift the quark ratio: perhaps at some scale $\mu^*$ 
  2439â†’the ratio $\sqrt{m_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{m_c(\mu^*)}$ hits $2 - \sqrt{3}$ 
  2440â†’exactly. But this is \emph{not possible within QCD alone}. At leading 
  2441â†’order, all quark masses run with the same anomalous dimension 
  2442â†’$\gamma_m$:
  2443â†’\begin{equation}
  2444â†’m_i(\mu) = m_i(\mu_0)\left(\frac{\alpha_s(\mu)}{\alpha_s(\mu_0)}\right)^{\gamma_m/\beta_0},
  2445â†’\end{equation}
  2446â†’so the ratio $m_s(\mu)/m_c(\mu)$---and therefore 
  2447â†’$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$---is an RG invariant of QCD. Higher-order 
  2448â†’corrections break this invariance, but only at the $O(\alpha_s^2)$ 
  2449â†’level, far too small to account for 1.2\%.
  2450â†’
  2451â†’The quark seed deviation is therefore \emph{intrinsic}: the 
  2452â†’$\overline{\text{MS}}$ mass ratio $m_s/m_c$ is a fixed number 
  2453â†’(at any QCD scale), and it misses the Koide target by 1.2\%. 
  2454â†’Three possible explanations present themselves:
  2455â†’
  2456â†’\paragraph{Electroweak running.} Above the electroweak scale, 
  2457â†’quark masses are controlled by Yukawa couplings $y_i$ to the 
  2458â†’Higgs, which \emph{do} run differently for different flavors: 
  2459â†’the $\beta$-functions for $y_s$ and $y_c$ involve different 
  2460â†’electroweak quantum numbers ($SU(2)_L$ doublet structure, 
  2461â†’hypercharge). The ratio $y_s/y_c$ evolves with scale, and 
  2462â†’there may exist a scale $\mu^*$ above the electroweak 
  2463â†’threshold where $\sqrt{y_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{y_c(\mu^*)} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$. 
  2464â†’This would identify the ``seed scale'' as an electroweak-sector 
  2465â†’phenomenon, not a QCD one---consistent with the sBootstrap's 
  2466â†’identification of the breaking with $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ 
  2467â†’rather than $SU(3)_c$.
  2468â†’
  2469â†’\paragraph{Charge running.} Even if mass ratios are QCD-invariant,
  2470â†’the Koide decomposition $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k$ may have its own
  2471â†’flow. The Koide scale $v_0 = z_0^2$ sets the common scale,
  2472â†’and the fluctuations $z_k$ encode the deviations. As shown in
  2473â†’\S\ref{subsec:fixedpoint}, simple RG running drives $Q \to 1/3$
  2474â†’rather than $2/3$, so the energy-balance condition cannot be an
  2475â†’attractor in the usual sense. However, the O'Raifeartaigh
  2476â†’realization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) shows that the condition is a
  2477â†’boundary condition on the superpotential ($gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$).
  2478â†’The 1.2\% deviation of the quark seed ratio may then reflect
  2479â†’the distance between the physical quark masses and the exact
  2480â†’superpotential constraint.
  2481â†’
  2482â†’\paragraph{Meson vs.\ quark deviations.} The meson deviation 
  2483â†’(0.6\%) is smaller than the quark deviation (1.2\%), and for 
  2484â†’a different reason: meson masses are \emph{not} simple functions 
  2485â†’of quark mass ratios. The pion mass is given by the GOR 
  2486â†’relation $m_\pi^2 f_\pi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$; the $D_s$ 
  2487â†’mass involves heavy-quark effective theory; and both receive 
  2488â†’chiral perturbation theory corrections. These nonperturbative 
  2489â†’QCD effects are the dominant source of the meson seed deviation, 
  2490â†’and are in principle calculable at NLO in ChPT.
  2491â†’
  2492â†’\paragraph{A remarkable feature of the VA relation.} The 
  2493â†’foundational equation $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2$ contains 
  2494â†’no QCD coupling constant: no $\alpha_s$, no $N_c$, no 
  2495â†’$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$. It is a pure mass relation among three 
  2496â†’experimentally measured quantities---two masses and a decay 
  2497â†’constant---holding to 2.2\%. In the sBootstrap, this is natural: 
  2498â†’the relation is a SUSY mass splitting, set by the breaking 
  2499â†’scale $f_\pi$, and SUSY mass relations do not involve the gauge 
  2500â†’coupling of the confining theory. Standard QCD \emph{explains} 
  2501â†’$f_\pi$ in terms of $\qqbar$ and $\alpha_s$; the sBootstrap 
  2502â†’\emph{uses} $f_\pi$ as the fundamental parameter, with QCD 
  2503â†’providing the dynamical origin but not appearing in the mass 
  2504â†’formula. The absence of QCD factors in \eqref{eq:pimu} is a 
  2505â†’hint that the relevant symmetry is not $SU(3)_c$ but the 
  2506â†’hadronic superalgebra that connects pions to leptons.
  2507â†’
  2508â†’The lepton ``seed'' has the worst ratio match ($-9\%$), which 
  2509â†’is consistent with the observation that the lepton triple is 
  2510â†’\emph{not} a true seed: $m_e \neq 0$, and the electron was never 
  2511â†’at the flat direction. The leptons are an ``almost-seed'' whose 
  2512â†’excellent $Q = 0.66666$ arises not from the seed structure but 
  2513â†’from the smallness of $m_e$, which makes the two conditions 
  2514â†’approximately degenerate. For the leptons, condition (1) is 
  2515â†’approximate rather than exact, and condition (2) is correspondingly 
  2516â†’relaxed.
  2517â†’
  2518â†’\subsection{What the $(c,b,t)$ triple tells us}
  2519â†’
  2520â†’The $(c,b,t)$ triple ($Q = 0.6695$, $v_0^2 = 29.6\GeV$) has 
  2521â†’neither a seed nor a meson shadow. It operates at a scale far 
  2522â†’above $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, suggesting its origin is different:
  2523â†’
  2524â†’\begin{enumerate}
  2525â†’\item It may be governed by the electroweak Higgs mechanism rather
  2526â†’than the chiral condensate. In a two-Higgs-doublet model with
  2527â†’$\tan\beta = 1$ (as predicted by the mixed-triple Koide condition,
  2528â†’\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}), $y_t \approx \sqrt{2}$;
  2529â†’the Koide condition on $(c,b,t)$ would then constrain the Yukawa
  2530â†’coupling matrix.
  2531â†’\item It may represent a Volkov--Akulov realization at the 
  2532â†’electroweak scale: if there is a second SUSY breaking at 
  2533â†’$f' \sim v_{\text{EW}}$, the $(c,b,t)$ triple would be its 
  2534â†’analog of the $(\pi, \mu)$ system, but with $f'$ replacing $f_\pi$.
  2535â†’\item Or it may be a purely kinematic accident---the quark masses 
  2536â†’happen to satisfy the Koide condition without a dynamical reason. 
  2537â†’The 0.42\% precision makes this unlikely but not impossible.
  2538â†’\end{enumerate}
  2539â†’
  2540â†’Interpretation (1) is most natural within the sBootstrap: the
  2541â†’$(c,b,t)$ triple involves quarks whose masses are dominated by
  2542â†’Yukawa couplings to the Higgs, not by the chiral condensate.
  2543â†’Since $m_i = y_i\,v_{\text{EW}}/\sqrt{2}$, the Koide condition
  2544â†’$Q = 2/3$ translates into a constraint on the Yukawa couplings
  2545â†’(see \S\ref{subsec:superpotential}):
  2546â†’$\sum y_i/(\sum \sqrt{y_i})^2 = 2/3$.
  2547â†’With $y_t \approx 1$ fixed by the top mass, this determines
  2548â†’$y_b/y_t$ and $y_c/y_t$ through the Koide parametrization.
  2549â†’The top Yukawa $y_t = 0.99$ is the only coupling of order unity;
  2550â†’the charm and bottom Yukawas ($y_c = 0.010$, $y_b = 0.034$) are
  2551â†’effective couplings generated by the Seiberg seesaw, not independent
  2552â†’fundamental parameters.  In this picture, the entire fermion mass
  2553â†’hierarchy derives from a single Yukawa coupling ($y_t \approx 1$)
  2554â†’plus confining dynamics at $\Lambda \sim 300\MeV$.
  2555â†’The chiral sector and the electroweak sector would each
  2556â†’independently produce a Koide-type energy balance, with the charm
  2557â†’and bottom quarks serving as the bridge between the two.
  2558â†’
  2559â†’\paragraph{CKM mixing and the Koide triples.}
  2560â†’A striking feature of the quark Koide triples is that they
  2561â†’\emph{obligatorily mix up-type and down-type quarks}: $(-s,c,b)$
  2562â†’contains one up-type ($c$) and two down-type ($s,b$), while
  2563â†’$(c,b,t)$ contains two up-type ($c,t$) and one down-type ($b$). An
  2564â†’exhaustive scan of all $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ quark triples with all
  2565â†’sign choices confirms that \emph{every} triple close to $Q = 2/3$ is
  2566â†’mixed---neither the pure down-type $(d,s,b)$ nor the pure up-type
  2567â†’$(u,c,t)$ comes close ($Q = 0.73$ and $Q = 0.85$ respectively).
  2568â†’This mixing of mass eigenvalues from different Yukawa sectors is
  2569â†’puzzling in the Standard Model, where up-type and down-type masses are
  2570â†’eigenvalues of independent matrices $Y_u$ and $Y_d$. It is natural,
  2571â†’however, in an $SU(5)_f$ flavor-symmetric framework where all five
  2572â†’quarks are treated democratically.
  2573â†’
  2574â†’The Koide constraints on the two overlapping triples, together with
  2575â†’the seed condition on $(0,s,c)$, determine $(s,c,b)$ from a single
  2576â†’input ($m_t$). The unconstrained masses are $m_u$ and $m_d$---the
  2577â†’first generation. The Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation~\cite{GST1968} $|V_{us}| \approx
  2578â†’\sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ connects precisely these free parameters to the
  2579â†’Cabibbo angle ($|V_{us}| = 0.224$ vs.\ PDG $0.2243$,
  2580â†’Table~\ref{tab:predictions}), suggesting that the CKM matrix
  2581â†’encodes the residual freedom left undetermined by the Koide
  2582â†’structure.
  2583â†’
  2584â†’If interpretation (2) is also invoked, the sBootstrap has two breaking 
  2585â†’scales:
  2586â†’\begin{equation}
  2587â†’f_{\text{chiral}} = f_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \qquad
  2588â†’f_{\text{EW}} \sim v_{\text{EW}} \approx 246\GeV\,,
  2589â†’\end{equation}
  2590â†’and the full spectrum requires both. The light sector (leptons, 
  2591â†’light mesons) is governed by $f_\pi$; the heavy sector (top, and 
  2592â†’by extension charm and bottom through the Koide network) is 
  2593â†’governed by $v_{\text{EW}}$. The charm and bottom quarks, 
  2594â†’appearing in both the $(0,s,c)/(-s,c,b)$ light-sector triples 
  2595â†’and the $(c,b,t)$ heavy-sector triple, are the ``bridges'' 
  2596â†’between the two scales.

content.content:
2273â†’\paragraph{The Seiberg seesaw and dual Koide.}
  2274â†’At the Seiberg vacuum with $B = \bar B = 0$, the diagonal meson VEVs
  2275â†’scale as $\langle M^j{}_j\rangle \propto 1/m_j$: heavy quarks produce
  2276â†’light mesons and vice versa. This seesaw prompts a dual question: does
  2277â†’the inverse-mass spectrum $\{1/m_i\}$ satisfy a Koide condition?
  2278â†’Evaluating $Q = \sum(1/m_i)/(\sum\sqrt{1/m_i})^2$ for the pure
  2279â†’down-type quarks $(d,s,b)$:
  2280â†’\begin{equation*}
  2281â†’  Q(1/m_d,\, 1/m_s,\, 1/m_b) = 0.6652\,,
  2282â†’\end{equation*}
  2283â†’a 0.22\% deviation from $2/3$---better than any direct quark Koide
  2284â†’triple. No other quark triple comes close under
  2285â†’the Seiberg seesaw (the next best, $(d,s,c)$, deviates by 4.2\%).
  2286â†’This ``dual Koide'' on $(d,s,b)$ (first reported here) connects
  2287â†’the UV mass hierarchy to the IR meson VEVs: the Seiberg constraint
  2288â†’maps the down-type quark masses into a near-Koide spectrum of
  2289â†’meson condensates. However, the
  2290â†’result is sensitive to $m_d$: within $\pm 1\sigma$ ($m_d = 4.67
  2291â†’\pm 0.48\MeV$), $Q$ ranges from $0.655$ to $0.676$, and the deviation
  2292â†’from $2/3$ varies from $0.2\%$ to $1.7\%$. The dual Koide is an
  2293â†’observation at the edge of the current $m_d$ uncertainty.
  2294â†’Setting $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 2/3$ exactly predicts
  2295â†’$m_b = 4562\MeV$ ($9\%$ from PDG, $12.7\sigma$)---much worse
  2296â†’than $v_0$-doubling ($m_b = 4177\MeV$, $0.07\%$).  The $0.22\%$
  2297â†’closeness of $Q$ to $2/3$ does not translate to a precise mass
  2298â†’prediction because the Koide quadratic amplifies small $Q$
  2299â†’deviations into large mass deviations.
  2300â†’
  2301â†’A simple algebraic identity connects the seed to the seesaw:
  2302â†’for any two masses, the Koide ratio
  2303â†’$Q(a,b) = (a+b)/(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^2$ satisfies
  2304â†’$Q(a,b) = Q(1/a,\,1/b)$ identically.
  2305â†’The seed $(0, m_s, m_c)$ sits at this self-dual point:
  2306â†’its Koide ratio is the same whether evaluated on quark masses or
  2307â†’on Seiberg-dual meson VEVs $\propto 1/m_i$.  Thus the seed Koide
  2308â†’in the electric (UV) theory is \emph{automatically} a seed Koide
  2309â†’in the magnetic (IR) theory---the Seiberg duality preserves the
  2310â†’seed condition without fine-tuning.
  2311â†’For the full bloomed triple $(-s,c,b)$, self-duality breaks:
  2312â†’$Q(-s,c,b) \neq Q(1/m_s,1/m_c,1/m_b)$ in general.
  2313â†’The dual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 0.665$ is therefore a
  2314â†’\emph{nontrivial} constraint on the down-type sector, not a
  2315â†’tautological restatement of the seed.
  2316â†’Among all $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ quark triples, $(d,s,b)$ is the
  2317â†’unique triple with inverse-mass Koide ratio within 1\% of $2/3$;
  2318â†’the next closest is $(d,s,c)$ at 4.2\% deviation---a 19:1 margin.
  2319â†’
  2320â†’\subsection{How the three supermultiplets join}
  2321â†’
  2322â†’The seed-to-bloom transition generates three separate Koide triples. 
  2323â†’But the full sBootstrap requires these triples to be \emph{embedded 
  2324â†’in a single meson matrix}. The $3\times 3$ light-flavor matrix is:
  2325â†’
  2326â†’\begin{equation}
  2327â†’M^{(3)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2328â†’u\bar{u} & u\bar{d} & u\bar{s} \\
  2329â†’d\bar{u} & d\bar{d} & d\bar{s} \\
  2330â†’s\bar{u} & s\bar{d} & s\bar{s}
  2331â†’\end{pmatrix}
  2332â†’\;\longleftrightarrow\;
  2333â†’\begin{pmatrix}
  2334â†’\pi^0/\eta & \pi^+ & K^+ \\
  2335â†’\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 \\
  2336â†’K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta'
  2337â†’\end{pmatrix}.
  2338â†’\end{equation}
  2339â†’
  2340â†’When charm and bottom are included, the matrix extends to $5\times 5$. 
  2341â†’We display it schematically, with the members of the meson Koide 
  2342â†’triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ marked by boxes:
  2343â†’
  2344â†’\begin{equation}
  2345â†’M^{(5)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2346â†’\pi^0/\eta & \boxed{\pi^+} & K^+ & D^0 & \boxed{B^+} \\
  2347â†’\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 & D^- & B^0 \\
  2348â†’K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta' & \boxed{D_s^-} & B_s \\
  2349â†’\bar{D}^0 & D^+ & D_s^+ & \eta_c & B_c \\
  2350â†’B^- & \bar{B}^0 & \bar{B}_s & \bar{B}_c & \eta_b
  2351â†’\end{pmatrix},
  2352â†’\end{equation}
  2353â†’where rows are indexed by $(u, d, s, c, b)$ and columns by 
  2354â†’$(\bar{u}, \bar{d}, \bar{s}, \bar{c}, \bar{b})$.
  2355â†’
  2356â†’The three members of the meson Koide triple occupy different 
  2357â†’off-diagonal blocks of this matrix: $\pi^+ = u\bar{d}$ is in the 
  2358â†’light $(u,d)$ block, $D_s^- = s\bar{c}$ bridges the light-charm 
  2359â†’sector, and $B^+ = u\bar{b}$ bridges the light-bottom sector. They 
  2360â†’do not form a ``slice'' in any simple linear-algebraic sense---they 
  2361â†’are scattered entries connected by the Koide condition on their 
  2362â†’\emph{masses}, not by their positions in the matrix. The meson seed 
  2363â†’$(0, \pi, D_s)$ drops the $B^+$ and replaces it with zero (the 
  2364â†’entry that would involve the top quark, absent from the matrix).
  2365â†’
  2366â†’This scattered structure is the central puzzle: the Koide triples 
  2367â†’are \emph{mass relations} among matrix entries, not submatrices. 
  2368â†’The question is whether the Seiberg constraint 
  2369â†’$\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ (appropriately generalized to 
  2370â†’$5 \times 5$), combined with the SUSY-breaking dynamics, produces 
  2371â†’correlations among these scattered entries that enforce the 
  2372â†’energy-balance condition $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
  2373â†’
  2374â†’\subsection{The energy balance is not an RG attractor}
  2375â†’\label{subsec:fixedpoint}
  2376â†’One might hope that the energy-balance condition
  2377â†’$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is a \emph{dynamical attractor}:
  2378â†’that three masses evolving under SUSY-breaking dynamics flow toward
  2379â†’the Koide surface $Q = 2/3$. However, the one-loop soft-mass RG
  2380â†’equations in $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with universal Casimirs and
  2381â†’negligible Yukawas give $dm_i/dt = \gamma(M_g^2 - m_i)$, which
  2382â†’drives all masses to a common value $m_i \to M_g^2$, yielding
  2383â†’$Q \to 1/3$---the \emph{opposite} extreme. The Koide preservation
  2384â†’condition $\sum_i F_i(3 - 2S/\sqrt{m_i}) = 0$ on the $Q = 2/3$
  2385â†’surface is algebraically incompatible with any uniform attractor
  2386â†’flow (by the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality, the required identity
  2387â†’$S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} = 9/2$ contradicts $S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} \geq 9$).
  2388â†’
  2389â†’The energy-balance condition therefore does \emph{not} arise from
  2390â†’simple RG running. It must instead be a \emph{boundary condition}
  2391â†’on the superpotential---as in the O'Raifeartaigh realization of
  2392â†’\S\ref{subsec:fterm}, where $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$ produces the exact
  2393â†’Koide seed. The condition is set at the scale of SUSY breaking and
  2394â†’then preserved by the QCD-invariant mass ratios, rather than being
  2395â†’an RG attractor.
  2396â†’
  2397â†’\subsection{The exact seed and what breaks it}
